regardless of success or failure. If multiple fear checks
are needed, *& and < of later rolls may cancel out
effects from earlier rolls.

Suggested minimum failure effects are:

e Failure: The character adds ff to each action he
takes during the encounter.

e Threat: The character suffers a number of strain
equal to the number of ¢o. If the check generates
46 46} 46 or more, the character can be staggered
for his first turn, instead.

e Despair: The character is incredibly frightened,
which increases the difficulty of all checks by one
until the end of the encounter.

Suggested success interpretations are:

e Success: The character avoids any fear effects,
except those triggered by %6¥.

¢ Advantage: Gain [_] on the character’s first check.
If spending multiple €%, grant [] to an additional
player's first check.

e Triumph: Can be spent to cancel all previous
penalties from fear checks, or spent to ensure
the character need not make any additional fear
checks during the encounter, no matter the source.

CHARACTER DEATH

Part of the appeal of playing AcE oF REBELLION is por-
traying heroic characters engaged in exciting and
dangerous situations. The galaxy is a perilous enough
place in times of peace; during the era of the Rebellion,
continuous warfare and turmoil means that death is a
constant companion. Even the heroic individuals por-
trayed by the Player Characters sometimes succumb
to mortal wounds.

Death of Player Characters is unlikely to occur in
most game sessions, but there’s always the risk. Some
players may become distraught, disappointed, or
angry at the death of their character, especially when
the player has invested a great deal of time and care
into its creation. The GM should strive to handle such
Situations fairly, perhaps even coming up with some
way to prevent the character's death. However, this
Should be avoided as much as possible, both to avoid
implications of favoritism, and to maintain the threat of
Player Character death in the future. If players feel that
their actions do not have consequences, then it’s less
likely the game will retain integrity.

When a Player Character does die, the player should
be allowed to begin creating a new character. The
player should give the Game Master some time to
determine how to insert him into the existing story at
a point that makes sense for the new character, ideally
without disrupting the flow of the story.

PLAYING A CHARACTER

As in most roleplaying games, in AcE oF REBELLION,
each player portrays a single, heroic character, and the
Game Master plays the part for every other character
in the game. Breathing life into a character is one of the
most rewarding parts of the game ; each player should
strive to make his character unique and memorable.

GIVING YOUR CHARACTER A VOICE

One of the most entertaining parts of playing any role-
playing game is making characters unique and interest-
ing. Players have the advantage of focusing on one char-
acter at a time and may invest an enormous amount of
time into crafting a character's backstory, personality,
and quirks. The characters Motivation and Duty are

THE GAME MASTER
ACE OF REBELLION

driving forces behind his personality. The Game Master
should encourage players to use these tools as a skele-
ton on which to add interesting touches. Many players
enjoy literally giving their character a separate voice. If
a player has a knack for accents, allow him the freedom
to use that voice when the character speaks directly.
Not everyone has such skill, of course. For players who
want to play their characters straight; using the play-
ers own voice is perfectly acceptable.

A character is what you make it. Even two characters
that are identical on paper, with the same Motivation
and Duty, can be portrayed in vastly different ways.
That spark of life doesn’t come from the rules or the
type of character played—it comes from the player
himself. During play, players should write down inter-
esting or unusual aspects about their characters. Per-
haps after recovering from an old Critical Injury, the PC
now walks with a slight limp and constantly complains
about the ache. Some Game Masters ask their players
to provide a brief description of what their characters
look like, along with the clothes they typically wear and
the gear they carry.

CAST OF THOUSANDS

For some Game Masters, the idea of portraying every
other being in the galaxy may seem impossible. How-
ever, the job of the Game Master is to move the story
along and let the players have fun, not create an
entirely unique personality and backstory for every
NPC they meet.

The exceptions, of course, are the villains and impor-
tant NPCs, who should be created using the same char-
acter creation rules as those for the Player Characters.
These NPCs should be well-defined in both personality
and purpose.

For “stock NPCs, it’s sufficient to give them one
particular personality trait to help the Player Char-
acters remember them. Archetypes work well here:
the exceedingly bored bureaucrat, aggressively rude
bouncer, or laconic mechanic. However, to make even
stock NPCs notable, it’s fine to mix things up and
break type. While you don’t have to give a unique
voice to each character, small inflections also help,
such as a lisp, drawl, or halting way of speaking. The
players are more likely to remember the cantina
owner prone to breaking out in song than the terse,
glass-wiping stereotype.

IMPROVISING

One of the most important skills for any GM to mas-
ter Is improvisation, both for dialogue and for thinking
on one’s feet due to changes in the story. Even when
running published adventures with pre-scripted NPC
dialogue and plotted-out stories, there are still many
times when the GM must react to unexpected ques-
tions or actions. The Game Master should be prepared

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

a

to alter the adventure based on the way the game
plays out in order to keep the plot moving forward. If
the players come up with something for which the GM
IS unprepared, he must be able to improvise entirely
new scenes or NPCs on the fly.

AWARDING EXPERIENCE POINTS

The GM should award experience points after every ses-
sion. The amount awarded is typically 10 XP per Player
Character for a session of two or three major encoun-
ters and a handful of minor ones. An additional 5 XP
bonus may be generated for reaching key milestones or
completing story arcs. Playing to a PC’s Motivation also
grants 5 XP per session at the GM's discretion. The GM
may consider awarding an extra point or two of XP for
exceptional roleplaying or highly clever thinking. Pub-
lished adventures may recommend specific XP rewards.

The GM should give the players an idea of the
source of their XP. For example, they may receive 5
XP for capturing an Imperial shuttle and another 5
XP for blowing up an Empire communications station.
Any bonus XP awarded should definitely be explained
to the players, so they may aspire to those standards
in future sessions.

USING MUSIC AND PROPS

0°: of the Las advantages of running a
game in the Star Wars setting is the avail-
ability of music. The movie soundtracks add
instant atmosphere to a game session. Begin-
ning an adventure with the opening fanfare and
the main title score instantly sets an unmis-
takable tone. Running the soundtracks at low
volume in the background of the game session
can add ambiance. Another approach is to play
selected tracks at the proper moments to add
to the situation at hand. Nothing fills the play-
ers with a bit of ee ane says “The
Empire is here’ like playing /he /mperial March
just before announcing a Star Dostrover has
arrived to ruin their ie

With the multitude of Star Wars toys, models,
replicas, and other eee available, it is also
easy to use them as props during the game.
Starship models, books with detailed pictures,
and toy weapons can all be adapted for use at
the table, or can serve as an example. How-
ever, not all toys set the appropriate mood. A
detailed X-wing model can add an interesting
angle to an in-game discussion about the craft.
A preschoolers version of the same X-wing
may not have the same effect.

TABLE 9-2: SPENDING EXPERIENCE

Options

May spend experience to
purchase ranks in any skill,
up to rank 5.

experience.

May spend experience to
purchase talents within

Bis pae" specialization tree,
specializations. EB

May spend experience
to purchase new
specializations.

Career skills cost five times the purchased rank in

Non-career skills cost five times the purchased rank
in experience, plus 5 additional experience.

Depends on talent's position within

New specializations cost ten times the total number
of specializations the character will have. Non-career
specializations cost 10 additional experience.

Prerequisites

Whether for a career or non-career skill, each
rank must be purchased sequentially. (Rank 2
must be purchased before rank 3, and so on.)

Talents may only be purchased if they are
on the first level of a specialization, or if they
are connected by a bar to a talent already
purchased.

SPENDING EXPERIENCE POINTS

When awarding XP, the GM should keep in mind what
his players will be able to spend experience points on
and what the costs are. This is covered on page 102,
but is reprinted here for convenience. This only covers
spending experience points after character creation.

TASKS BETWEEN SESSIONS

During the time between when one session ends and
the next begins, the players and Game Master should
take stock of certain factors. The Game Master should
have players check their character sheets to make
sure that they received and spent any XP gained from
previous sessions, and he should grant any that may
have been forgotten prior. The GM should also alter
the group’s current Duty and Contribution as needed.
Players should revise their character information to
take into account changes in equipment and to record
new abilities, skills, or talents gained through XP.

It’s strongly recommended that both the Game
Master and the players keep notes throughout play
to remind them about what has transpired. These
include important names, information learned through
the adventure, any ongoing effects (particularly Critical
Injuries or damage to their starship, if they have one),
and goals for the next session, if any.

Sessions do not necessarily pick up immediately
where the previous ones left off. The Game Master may
include downtime for the characters in which a certain
amount of time passes and events take place that do
not require the player to make checks. For instance, a
Rebel spy might spend this “downtime’ receiving train-
ing for his next mission, or a soldier might perform sev-
eral routine but uneventful patrols. There is no need to
play out mundane events. The GM and players should
be ready to summarize what has occurred during the
downtime between adventures.

POST-GAME ANALYSIS
AND IMPROVEMENT

Feedback between players and the Game Master Is
important to see what's working in the game and what
isn’t. In the early stages of learning the game, the GM
should end each session with a brief recap of impor-
tant events and a discussion of what went well and
what was confusing. Players should bring up rules that
they don’t quite understand so the GM can both clarify
and plan ahead for the future, and they should pro-
vide feedback about how they think the game Is going.
Some players prefer giving feedback or asking ques-
tions one-on-one, and the GM should provide time to
any players who prefer to talk specifics in this manner.

Feedback goes both ways. If the Game Master
believes that a player isn't having fun or is disrupting
the group in some way, then he should pull that player
aside to let him know. The goal of this feedback Is to
both improve the way that the game is played and to
increase the enjoyment for everyone involved.

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION


### Player Characters and the Rebellion

PLAYER CHARACTERS AND

THE REBELLION

GE OF REBELLION focuses on the action that takes place

during the time between Episode IV A New Hope and
Episode V The Empire Strikes Back. \n this era, the rag-
tag forces of the scattered Rebellion square off against
the immense and extremely powerful Galactic Empire. It
is a time when freedoms are drastically curtailed, injus-
tice is pervasive, and those who do not obey the Empire
are crushed beneath its heel.

And yet, hope persists. Led by Princess Leia Organa,
the Rebel Alliance fights to preserve what few free-
doms remain and overthrow the shackles of Impe-
rial rule. Small and scattered throughout the galaxy,
the severely outnumbered Rebellion fights back the
only way that it can—with subterfuge, espionage,
ambushes, and rallying the spirits of freedom-loving
beings throughout the galaxy.

The Player Characters represent some of the Rebel-
lion’s best and brightest. Their skills, abilities, and tal-
ents are used to hit the Empire where it hurts the most
in hopes of regaining the freedoms and liberties lost with
the rise of the Emperor. The challenges are enormous,
and the effort required in overcoming them monumen-
tal, but the PCs are a cut above the rest. The PCs’ allies
in the Rebellion—the foot soldiers, commandos, pilots,

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

ah

gunners, medics, and spies—toil, fight, cheer for victory,
and sometimes die, serving as a backdrop for the Player
Characters to shine and change the fate of the galaxy.
No matter their backgrounds, skills, or methods, the
Player Characters and their actions are vital to the war
effort. The Rebel Alliance needs them more than ever if
the Empire is to be brought to its Knees.

PART OF A BIGGER
PICTURE

The war between the Rebel Alliance and the Empire
is vast, covering the entire breadth and width of the
galaxy. Over the course of decades, untold battles
were fought between these two forces, with the Rebels
claiming both victories and defeats. While immensely
important to the outcome of the conflict, the events
shown in the Star Wars movies were only one part of
the war. Untold numbers of Rebels fought to overthrow
the grip of the Empire, mainly in places briefly (or not
at all) mentioned in the movies and books. In short,
the scenes from the Star Wars movies are just part of
a bigger picture.

The adventures of the Player Characters fit into the
canvas of the fight between the Rebellion and the
Empire. Just as Luke Skywalker, Han Solo, and Leia
Organa have their place in the story, so too do the
Player Characters. The stories created by the Game
Master represent these “unsung moments’ that played
Just as important a part as those shown in the movies.

There are two different ways that the Game Master
can incorporate the Player Characters into the over-
all narrative shown in the Star Wars movies. First, the
players could take part in key scenes from the movies,
but “off-screen” from the specific exploits of the famous
characters. Second, the Player Characters explore new
eround, adventuring on other planets or being a part
of other key events that were not shown, but which are
still important to the Rebellion.

The most important aspect to remember is that the
Player Characters are not beholden to the structure of
the Rebel Alliance. They are free to help with the war
effort any way that they see fit. This may mean don-
ning uniforms and heading to the front lines or acting
as “specialists” who benefit the cause through different
kinds of adventures.

OFF-SCREEN OF MAJOR SCENES

In this scenario, the Player Characters are participat-
ing in the same scenes as the major characters from
the Star Wars movies. The PCs might catch a fleet-
ing glimpse of these key figures, but otherwise do not
interact with them. For example, the Player Characters
could be part of a crack commando squad ordered to
hold off the Imperial Forces as they made their way
inside the Rebel Base during the Battle of Hoth.

The downside to this type of scenario is that the
players ultimately know the outcome. Using the exam-
ple above, the Player Characters know that their efforts
are ultimately in vain and the base gets overrun. This
is fine, however, so long as the Game Master provides
goals and situations specifically concerning the PCs.
Pernaps they are tasked with blowing up the base’s
mainframe before evacuation so it doesn't fall into the
Empire's hands. If circumstances change, they may
have to come up with an alternative solution for finding
their way to the Rebels’ rendezvous location.

UNTOLD ADVENTURES

The war between the Alliance and the Empire went
on for decades and spread to every corner of the gal-
axy. While some battles are the stuff of legends—the
Battles of Yavin, Hoth, and Endor, for example—these
were just a few chapters in the story. Countless battles
were waged on distant planets and the space between.
This scenario works best for Game Masters who wish
to write their own adventures, taking the Player Char-
acters to exotic locations found in either the Star Wars
Expanded Universe or entirely new places. Regardless
of where they go, however, the Player Characters have
a duty to fight the Empire wherever it is found.

HANDLING COMMAND AMONG
PLAYER CHARACTERS

f the Player Characters are each given a spe-

cific rank, it’s quite possible that one char-
acter will be assigned as a commander. The
players need to decide how to handle this
authority during play. As with dealing with
NPC superiors, subordinate PCs are expected
to follow their commander's orders. Some
players balk at the idea of being told what to
do by another player. Avoid having this situ-
ation turn into an actual confrontation and
remind players that the goal is for the PCs
to get along and move the story forward. A
player sticking to the letter of the law, but not
necessarily the spirit, shouldn't be punished...
immediately. An overly demanding superior or
insubordinate PC could find himself in front of
a review board between sessions.

In short, don’t let players get too committed to
the power that comes with rank!

When using this setup, the Game Master is free to
create new planets, moons, bases, and other locations.
The goal is to design the location or adventure in a
way that still feels like Star Wars, even if the planet’s
name is new to the players’ ears. The familiar tropes of
a “used and beat-up universe,” droids, species inter-
mingled, and the ever-present threat of the Empire
Should still be in the background.

It's equally important to make it clear that the Player
Characters’ actions have an important, even crucial,
purpose in the war effort. After an adventure, the
Game Master may inform the Player Characters how
their actions affect the overall war, perhaps weaving
them into the events shown on the screen.

THE CHAIN OF COMMAND

Although loose when compared to the rigid hierarchy
of the Empire, the Rebel Alliance is built around a mili-
tary structure. Individuals are given ranks and expected
to follow orders from superiors and command subordi-
nates beneath them. That said, the needs of the Rebel-
lion mean that the concept of rank and subordination
is flexible. The Rebellion utilizes countless “special-
ists —individuals with skills, talents, and positions that
are crucial to the war effort, but which do not require
them to don a uniform and say “yes, sir” every time a
captain gives them an order. This freedom allows the
Player Characters to belong to a military-styled orga-
nization without the baggage that comes with it. They
are specialists whom Alliance needs and it is willing to
give latitude to in order to do what's best for the effort
In their own way.

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

Depending on the type of adventure or campaign
the Game Master wants to run, attention should be
paid to how “military” the game feels. Some players
may want to play a military-focused game, in which
they are part of cohesive units. This gives them the
benefit of access to weapons, gear, and vehicles drawn
from the supply pools, but comes with the structure
that some players may find confining. It’s best to make
the Player Characters special ops personnel, giving
them greater latitude out in the field and keeping the
pressures and rigidity of military life outside of combat
in the far background.

Chapter XI: The Rebellion provides a more in-depth
look at the ranks and structure of the Rebel Alliance.

AGE OF REBELLION is about action, not military tribu-
nals and disciplinary committees. Unless the Player
Characters commit some grievous (or traitorous) act or
are guilty of gross negligence, the Game Master should
steer clear from doling out military justice to them. The
occasional dressing-down by a ship’s captain or short
stint in the brig, however, is a good way to remind
errant players about the consequences for some of
their unwise decisions.

PLAYER CHARACTERS AND SUPERIORS

As a part of the Rebel Alliance, the Player Characters
are assumed to fall somewhere in the hierarchy and
rosters of a military operation and, as such, they will
receive orders from superior officers at some point.
The Game Master needs to figure out what level of
“engagement” the Player Characters will have with
their superior officers. In most adventures, it’s accept-
able to keep the Player Characters’ superiors mostly
off-screen, giving orders at the start of the session or
throughout the session. This way works best, since
it allows the Game Master to set things in motion
through a proxy NPC and to alter the adventure with
additional orders if things get off track or it is time for
the next adventure..

Things get more complicated when a superior offi-
cer accompanies the Player Character. While it makes
sense for the PCs to follow orders, avoid having the
officer micromanage them. The PCs should be allowed
to make their own decisions and perform their own
actions. The best thing to do is to have the officer pro-
vide initial instructions and orders, then fall back and
act as just another NPC, contributing to the scene only
when necessary (and adding his own blaster fire when
combat occurs).

PLAYER CHARACTERS
AND SUBORDINATES

Sometimes the Player Characters are tasked with com-
manding a group of lower-ranked soldiers or Alliance
personnel. This could include additional squad mem-
bers beyond the Player Characters. Rebels are loyal and

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

oe

dedicated; they are soldiers trained to follow orders.
As long as the commanding Player Character does not
order them to do anything truly suicidal, the subordi-
nates will do what they are told, within their capabilities.
PCs should be reminded that they are responsible for
any subordinates and reap the consequences if they are
killed, captured, or compromised in some way.

When running allied NPCs alongside Player Charac-
ters, it’s best to treat the allies as a single group, rolling
a single dice pool for attacks or skill checks. Unique
or more powerful allied NPCs should be treated sepa-
rately, especially if they have specific talents or skills
that could come into play.

FOLLOWING ORDERS VS. TACTICAL IMMEDIACY

There's a saying among soldiers: “No plan survives con-
tact with the enemy.” This means that even the most
thought-out, detailed, and contingency-laden plan
may crumble when the realities of the battlefield set in.
Planned airdrops may never arrive. Intelligence incor-
rectly assesses the strength of the enemy. Sometimes
equipment just doesn t work when you need it to.

The Rebellion is pragmatic in its approach for giving
orders to its soldiers. Orders are given and expected to
be carried out. However, given the overwhelming odds
stacked against them, Rebels often find themselves
having to alter a mission in order to carry it out, or
even to survive. This means that Player Characters are
free to do what they wish in order to complete a mis-
sion. Of course, they may have to explain themselves
afterward, particularly if the outcome resulted in col-
lateral damage or some other unforeseen twist. Other-
wise, players should not be concerned about how the
actions of their characters may not fall immediately in
line with the goals of Alliance High Command.

THE PRICE OF WAR

War is hell. Collateral damage is part of any conflict and
civilians can get caught in the crossfire or perish beneath
indiscriminate bombing. While the Empire cares little for
the plight of unwitting civilians, the Rebellion strives to
keep the loss of life to a minimum. This decree is hard-
wired into the Rebellion’s primary goal, and troopers
are constantly reminded that the people who might get
killed as a result of their actions are the same ones they
are trying to free from the yoke of the Empire.

Seeing the horrors of war should make an impact to
both character's Motivation and Duty. Some Motiva-
tions may get strengthened or tested by witnessing the
results of war and the brutality of the Empire. Indeed,
war acts as a lens, focusing a person's true nature to
a fine point. The Duty of a character may similarly
be affected—a PC with the Duty to destroy every TIE
fighter he sees may be forced to witness the destruc-
tion caused as his targets plunge into a populated civil-
ian area. Does this cause him to question his Duty?
Similarly, a Player Character utterly dedicated to the

war effort may pass by innocents in need of food or
medical attention, since those resources are best uti-
lized by the Alliance itself. Does ignoring the need
bolster his Duty? The GM should look at the inciden-
tal side effects of the war to see what impact it makes
on each Player Character throughout the adventure.

MISSIONS FOR THE
REBELLION

Belonging to the Rebel Alliance is fraught with dan-
ger. As members of the Alliance, the Player Charac-
ters find themselves put into harm’s way on a regu-
lar basis. Orders from command may come well in
advance, with planning, logistics, and the personnel
to get the job done right; or it may arrive at the last
minute, and the Player Characters must think on
their feet and improvise to achieve the mission’s goal.

AcE OF REBELLION iS designed to allow the Game
Master to create and run a Rebellion-focused game
any way that he sees fit. Both real-world history and
that of the Star Wars setting have plenty of examples
of various ways to run a campaign set in a galaxy of
conflict. Even within a single campaign, the Player
Characters might find themselves taking on a wide
variety of missions—starship battles at the fringes
of an asteroid field, house-to-house combat in the

y' i
-:
03 il
hig. A
bah
oe
e.

streets of a besieged capital city, and stealthy runs
inside a vehicle manufacturing plant to blow it up.

While the players belong to and receive orders from
the Rebel Alliance, they are also free to handle most
situations using their own discretion. The Rebellion
is spread out enough through the galaxy that the
High Command doesn’t have the luxury of planning
every detail, nor the personnel to guarantee success.
Indeed, the Rebel Alliance encourages improvisa-
tion and quick thinking, rapidly promoting those who
show initiative in the face of adversity.

TYPES OF MISSIONS

The term “mission” is used to describe orders given to
the Player Characters by the Rebel Alliance. Missions
carried out by the Player Characters for the Rebel
Alliance might be short and simple or long and com-
plex. A single mission might be described as a
single encounter (assaulting a bunker), an
adventure (travel to the ruins of Alder-

aan to look for a damaged probe

that survived), or a whole cam-

paign (hunt down and elimi-

nate all Imperial forces

on a given planet).

a ~B:
£
of \\
/ iii uit!
| nn ige dA a6
o e \_*

| . " iii!
.
vf | _
/ “4
. - a /

iii!

While there’s no way to cover every single type of
mission the Player Characters might receive, described
here are some broad guidelines for common types,
what they might involve, and ways for the Game Mas-
ter to implement them in play. Any one of the missions
described here could be an encounter, adventure, or
campaign in its own right.

AMBUSH MISSIONS

Considering the forces it’s up against, the Rebel Alliance
relies heavily on ambushing the Empire whenever possi-
ble. Depending on its specific goals, an ambush could be
result in complete eradication of the target or lead to its
capture. Rebels target lightly-armed and -armored sup-
ply caravans and cargo ships to help fuel the war effort,
capturing all manner of weapons, ammunition, food,
armor, and, most importantly, additional intelligence. The
elite Alliance Special Forces are masters of the ambush,
striking even daunting targets with incredible efficiency.

Ambushes require stealth, excellent intelligence, and
sufficient personnel and weaponry to succeed. Many
larger ambush missions happen because of success-
ful reconnaissance and espionage missions that took
place before them (See Reconnaissance Missions, on
page 323). Ambushes work best when the enemy is
far from any meaningful support or caught in difficult
terrain, such as winding canyons, swamplands, or a
narrow corridor of an asteroid field. Timing is critical,
as one false step could alert the enemies too soon or
allow them to escape.

Ambushes are best run as single encounters, as most
of the mission involves setting up in a single location
and waiting for the enemy to arrive—not very
exciting for a sustained set of sessions.

ESTABLISH AND DEFEND MISSIONS

Although relatively few in number, the
Rebel Alliance does have bases, out-
posts, and important depots scattered
throughout the galaxy. These
locations are critical to

the continued war

effort and are heav- >
ily defended to pre-
vent their loss—the
Rebels simply don't
have the resources to
let too many fall into
enemy hands.

There's more to
a defense mission
than standing guard
on a wall with a
blaster rifle and
pair of elec

trobinoculars. Player Characters are tasked with making
regular patrols outside the location's perimeter, assem-
bling larger ordnance for defense, or looking for weak
points to strengthen fortifications. Beyond the Empire,
the Rebels may have to deal with hostile native forces
that take a dim view to their presence.

The Player Characters may be ordered to set up a
new base of operations, depot, waypoint, or safe house
for the Alliance. This could be as simple as landing on a
remote moon and protecting the engineers as they build
a new base or as difficult as clearing out an already-
occupied location (linking this with an Assault Mission,
see Full Assault Missions on page 524).

Avoid making defense missions boring affairs for the
players. It’s fine to send them out on a mission or two
in which nothing much happens, only to lull them into a
sense of security. The enemy will use many of the same
tactics as the PCs, perhaps releasing a false transmis-
sion that pulls the Player Characters out to investigate,
opening the location up to an attack. Even if the loca-
tion is heavily attacked, a siege also becomes just as
boring over time. Look for ways to inject greater tension
into the situation, such as including of an AT-AT that is
capable of exploding open the base's blast doors, or
making the PCs hold onto the location just long enough
for a shuttle to arrive and take them to safety.

One fine example of an “establish and defend” mis-
sion (from the Alliance perspective) is the epic Battle of
Hoth from Episode V The Empire Strikes Back.

HIT-AND-RUN MISSIONS

The most common method of direct action against the
Empire, a hit-and-run involves choosing a single,
relatively easy target, swooping in fast,

>

=

— \

. ~~ |
. . i f
=), << d

——— on
=

¥
]

and getting out as soon as possible. Salvage and cap-
ture of equipment or personnel is not the purpose.
Starships and other vehicles are picked for speed and
heavy firepower but have little in the way of armor or
staying power—the best defense is speed.

Hit-and-run missions are best suited for single
encounters, although an adventure could be built
around first getting to the target or extricating the
party after the assault, especially if things go wrong.

SABOTAGE MISSIONS

The Rebellion learned long ago that one of the best
ways to cripple the Empire is to strike at its infrastruc-
ture. Sabotage missions are a blend of espionage and
mayhem, with the Player Characters infiltrating or
blasting their way into a vital location and doing some
damage. Key infrastructure locations are targeted,
such as bridges, water and food processing plants, traf-
fic control droid units, and power stations. Sabotage
missions are intended to disrupt the enemies’ plans
and make their lives miserable.

Sabotage missions need not be initially destructive.
The Player Characters may be ordered to infiltrate an
Imperial base and slice into the computer network to
deliver a piece of malicious code that shuts down the
deflector shields at crucial moment. Depending on the
circumstances, the PCs may have to go under-cover
and pose as civilians (or the enemy), or sneak in under
the cover of darkness to hit their target and get out.

The effort to destroy the shield generator on the
forest moon of Endor in Return of the Jedi is a good
example of a sabotage mission.

RECONNAISSANCE MISSIONS

In order to plan for further missions, reconnaissance
missions are vital to the Rebellion. Unlike espionage
missions, reconnaissance involves military or paramili-
tary units getting close to the enemy and making note
of troop movements, defensibility, weapon strength,
and other important information.

Stealth is crucial for the success of any reconnais-
sance. Starships and vehicles used for these missions
are typically stripped down to the bare essentials,
using the best available counter-sensor technology, or
are perfectly ordinary (and unarmed) vehicles meant to
blend in with other traffic. Unit sizes for these missions
are kept to a minimum. Many recon missions are sent
out on foot, so weapons are light and meant to get
the recon team out of danger rather than into it; most
other gear is intended for camouflage, or for recording
and evaluating the data the team acquires.

Reconnaissance missions are further complicated by
the fact that backup may be non-existent. Retrieval of
a reconnaissance team might be aborted if the team
misses a narrow window of time, at which point they’re
on their own to find a way back to safety. Due to risk
of capture, recon teams might receive little additional

information to minimize the possibility of giving vital
information back to the enemy on which they were
sent to spy.

The key to running an interesting and exciting recon-
naissance mission is to first provide the Player Charac-
ters with a clear goal beyond “go observe the enemy
encampment.” Provide them orders to procure spe-
cific information, such as the location of a particular
spaceship (that might be hidden inside one of several
hangars), or timing the exact moment when a base’s
shields drop to allow troops to exit. Characters also
have to deal with roaming patrols, the deactivation of
sensors, minefields, and finding a way back out once
the information has been gathered.

ESPIONAGE MISSIONS

The Rebels rely heavily on gathering intelligence to
Stay alive and further the cause. An intricate web of
Spies, moles, and double agents keep a steady of flow
of information back to the Alliance High Command. A
branch of the Rebellion known as Alliance Intelligence
plans and executes the most important missions,
sometimes without the knowledge (or approval) of the
High Command itself. The premier Bothan Spynet pro-
vides regular and crucial intelligence, but at a high cost,
both in credits and the in fact that the Bothans sell
their information to the highest bidder.

Espionage missions typically involve operatives going
undercover, disguising themselves to pass without sus-
picion. The exact purpose of an espionage mission
varies, but can include slicing into Imperial networks,
Stealing vital plans or experimental equipment, plant-
ing false information and, in some cases, assassination.
The risks are enormous, as captured spies are guaran-
teed “vigorous” interrogation, imprisonment, and most
likely, death. To increase their chances for survival,
Player Characters sent on such missions are provided
with an array of specialized gear and weapons meant
for easy concealment or disguised to appear as harm-
less and easily overlooked items.

Considering the specialized skills and specific situ-
ations associated with espionage, these types of mis-
sions are best used for adventures or campaigns built
specifically around them. While there's always a place
for a tough soldier handy with a blaster rifle, a party of
Player Characters built for combat on the battlefield
will find themselves seriously handicapped in many
espionage scenarios. However, the Alliance is always
short of capable individuals and the PCs may find
themselves called up for such a mission, despite their
backgrounds and skills.

RESCUE MISSIONS

Rescue missions involve attacking or infiltrating a
guarded location in order to liberate an ally. One of
the most dangerous missions possible, this task is usu-
ally reserved for crack commandos and elite, highly-
trained forces with a variety of skills; they are more

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

difficult if the enemy is aware of plans to extricate
the target. Rescue missions require careful planning
to get to the location unnoticed, breach any defenses
(sometimes without drawing any attention), locate the
target, and then repeat the process in reverse, often
with blaster fire coming from startled guards. Making
things even more difficult, the Player Characters must
find the target or targets and bring them out alive and
preferably unharmed.

Rescue missions require a good blend of careers and
skills to succeed—stealthy individuals who can disable
euards quietly, medics to treat any wounded (especially
the target of the rescue mission), tech-minded charac-
ters capable of bypassing locked doors or jury-rigging
an explosive, and pilots to get everyone out alive.

Rescue missions are perfectly suited for the types of
scenarios run in Ace oF REBELLION, blending a mix of
subtlety and combat. If things seem to go too easily for
the Player Characters, it's easy enough to add an unex-
pected change in guard shifts, incorrect maps show-
ing the location of the targets, or even targets who are
injured or downright unhelpful, despite the fact that
they are getting rescued.

The retrieval of Princess Leia from the Death Star in
Episode lV A New Hope is an example of a rescue mission.

.
a

FULL ASSAULT MISSIONS

The most rare and risky of missions is the full assault, in
which the full weight of a Rebel unit is brought to bear
directly upon the enemy. While stealth and subterfuge
do factor into the equation, the main focus is on over-
whelming strength, armor, and firepower. Space bat-
tles might include a dozen capital ships and hundreds
of fighters. Mass combat could see a blend of tanks,
walkers, and heavy artillery blasting away with missiles
and ion cannons. On a personal level, troopers by the
hundreds, armed with blaster rifles and grenades, inch
their way across the cratered battlefield to get in close
to the Imperial Forces.

Make sure to give the Player Characters specific
goals to achieve during a full assault mission, such as
capturing a laser cannon battery, destroying the flag-
ship of the enemy fleet, or overtaking a command
bunker. Also, give the PCs secondary objectives to give
them the flexibility to change their mission plans or
have more to do if they succeed In the original order.

Two of the best examples of the full assault mission
from Star Wars include the Battle of Hoth (from the
Empire’s perspective) and the attacks on the two Death
Stars at the conclusion of Episode IV A New Hope and
Episode VI Return of the Jedi.


### Using Duty

USING DUTY

D uty is a core narrative and game mechanic in AcE
OF REBELLION (See page 46 for more information).
Duty represents the unique way in which a Player Char-
acter contributes to the Rebellion, and can influence
the choices PCs make and the path an adventure takes.
Depending on how a GM utilizes Duty, it can just be a
motivator for PCs to go above and beyond, or it can be
a tool used to introduce unexpected opportunities and
plot twists.

GMs are encouraged to use Duty creatively through-
out the course of the adventure and campaign. Since the
Player Characters can gain Duty at character creation,
as well as earn it though their actions, GMs should make
sure to reward players with high Duty scores for their
commitment and accomplishment. This can be done
through tangible rewards, or by integrating more plot
hooks for that PC based on his Duty triggering. The GM
should take every opportunity to use these hooks dur-
ing the adventure or campaign. Published adventures
may include suggested ties to certain types of Duties.

GUIDING STARTING
DUTY SELECTION

During character creation, each player either selects
his character's Duty type himself or randomly deter-
mines it (see page 46). Parties may end up with a
wide variety of Duties or a limited number of types
shared between PCs. The GM generally should not limit
the types of Duty in a game but may want to work with
the players if they want to use it to craft a specific,
interesting backstory for their characters.

The players should not be allowed to optimize or
manipulate Duty to gain a substantial benefit from it.
Duties are meant to grant periodic, large rewards that
reinforce good roleplaying and character progression.

Alternatively, the GM (or players) may select or come
up with new types of Duty to fit a specific campaign or
story arc. The Duty may be a driving force behind an
adventure or campaign, rather than a periodic respon-
sibility. The GM should make sure that any Player Char-
acters with a Duty less important to the plot are not
forgotten, and are highlighted from time-to-time.

USING THE DUTY CHECK

Before each game session, the GM makes a Duty check
(see page 48). The greater the group’s Duty is, the
greater the chance that it will come directly into play
during that session. When triggered, the GM should
note which Player Character’s Duty is activated and
how that PC’s specific Duty manifests itself.

The mechanical bonuses of the Duty check on some
PCs can be significant. A particularly exciting result
(rolling doubles) should have a major story impact dur-
ing the session. If the Duty affects more than one PC,
the GM should consider altering the plot for the ses-
sion to reflect the sudden change. However, if the GM
decides it would derail the story at a critical juncture,
he can delay the actual encounter. The mechanical
bonuses should still remain in effect—perhaps the PCs
become informed of the possible opportunity and are
excited about it, even if they can’t immediately attend
to the situation.

### Using the Contribution Rank

=

¥
Y = ae

he party's Contribution rank indicates the group’s ded-

ication and value to the Rebel Alliance. It represents
the number of times a group has made large, meaningful
contributions to the Rebellion. Where the Contribution
rank number falls dictates how much authority and influ-
ence the Player Characters have when dealing with other
members of the Rebel Alliance, as well as how much of
a threat the PCs pose to the Empire. As a group’s Con-
tribution rank goes up, the more sway that group has in
choosing missions and getting access to more and better
resources, including equipment, vehicles, starships, and
any other assets it could need.

The higher the Contribution rank of a group, the
greater access the group has within the Alliance.
Although the Player Characters are in no way obligated
to join the military portion of the Alliance, suggestions
are provided for granting the military ranks they would
be promoted to based on Contribution rank; the GM is
free to ignore granting ranks if he chooses (see Table
9-3: Group Contribution Rank Guidelines).

As Player Characters rise in respect and power
within the Rebel Alliance, so too does their notoriety
rise within the Empire. The names of the PCs come up
more and more during interrogations of prisoners, and
Imperial Intelligence Officers start digging into the PCs’
pasts (if possible) to find ways to strike back at them
outside the battlefield. At low Contribution ranks, the
PCs barely register on the Empire’s sensors beyond
being “Rebel.scum that must be destroyed.” As the
PCs grow in reputation and power, however, the Empire
brings more resources to bear to capture or kill the
them, including bounty hunters and even the dreaded
Imperial assassins.

This fame and/or notoriety makes traveling to some
planets more complicated. PCs with a high Contribu-

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

USING THE CONTRIBUTION RANK

-
a, _-, “ae

tion rank find themselves thronged and well-respected
on Alliance-held planets. However, if the PCs go to
Empire-held or neutral planets, they could find them-
selves the subject of scorn, snubbing, or worse; oppor-
tunistic individuals may contact the Empire to pass
along the PCs' location, whether in hopes of a reward
or as their nationalistic duty. Table 9-3: Group Con-
tribution Rank Guidelines gives a general idea of
what sorts of perks or problems the Player Characters
face when dealing with the Alliance or Empire.

MANAGING ALLIANCE
REWARDS

When the PCs’ combined Duty value exceeds 100, the
Alliance rewards them for their contribution by supply-
ing them with resources in the form of equipment, vehi-
cles, starships, or other assets. There are guidelines for
what the PCs are allowed to obtain on page 50,
but ultimately, it’s up to the GM to decide if certain
resources are available to the party or not.

The Rebel Alliance is a large organization of people,
but they do not have unlimited resources. When the
PCs are deciding what rewards they would like from
the Alliance, the GM can set boundaries as to what
they are allowed to select based on the Rebellion’s
resources and the type of campaign the GM would
like to run. For example, as the PCs’ Contribution rank
starts to get higher, many large and powerful ships may
become available to them. If the GM does not want to
run a campaign focused on large-scale starship com-
bat, he may decide that the Rebellion does not have
Ships of this scale, or the crews to man them, to spare.

The same applies to equipment. The GM has final
say regarding the Rebel Alliance's available selection.


### Using Character Motivations

USING CHARACTER MOTIVATIONS

otivations are built-in story and roleplaying hooks

for both the player and the GM. The player uses
Motivations to inform his character's reactions to specific
situations, offering a level of thought or detail beyond
simply responding to the situation as presented. The
GM uses Motivations to link the characters more inti-
mately with the ongoing storyline. Players adhering to
their characters’ Motivations should be rewarded with
additional XP, as well as more interesting plot lines.

MANAGING MOTIVATIONS

The GM should track player Motivations and how they
may interact with a prepared adventure. Motivations,
especially those determined randomly, may conflict
with the Player Character goals of a published adven-
ture or GM-created story. After initial character cre-
ation, the GM should be able to better predict how
their Motivations may interact with the intended plot.

Players may keep their Motivations secret, possibly
going as far as establishing a decoy Motivation to cover
their real one. Secret Motivations add to the depth and
tension of the game, but also harbor possible story
(and party) disruption when the secret is revealed. If
the party has widely divergent Motivations, the GM
should be prepared to step in should player feelings be
hurt if opposing Motivations are revealed.

The GM may simplify the impact of Motivations on
the story by encouraging characters with similar or
compatible Motivations to combine them. Some Moti-
vations also work well with certain types of Duty. If the
characters have divergent Motivations and types of
Duty, the GM may minimize the story complexity by
de-emphasizing Motivation for those players who seem
less interested in using the mechanic, or by incorporat-
ing it into fewer adventures.

Characters should not often change their Motivation
(see Changing Motivations on page 107). When they
do so, it should be for compelling story reasons, not
because the bonus XP is too difficult to gain. Some Motiva-
tions may naturally end because the characters conclude a
story integral to the Motivation. Some Motivations change
Slowly, as a new Motivation begins to dominate the charac-
ters life or thinking. Change to Motivations should also be
reflected in the adventure or campaign.

INCORPORATING
MOTIVATIONS INTO
THE STORY

The GM should allow the player to dictate how impor-
tant his character's Motivation is to his experience.
Whereas Duty is intended to be an outside force that

permeates character interaction and the ability to
function within the adventure, Motivation focuses on
the character's internal drive and how that influences
his interaction with the galaxy. If the player wants to
focus on his personal story, or consistently gain the
XP awards from adhering to his Motivation, the GM is
encouraged to incorporate his Motivation in the adven-
ture more often.

Not all Motivations need to provide deep story hooks.
If a player is more comfortable reacting to situations
as they unfold, rather than dealing with a super-secret
background history with surprise appearances by past
enemies or events, then the GM should allow them to
play to that style. Some players are happy to simply use
their Motivation to justify their current actions.

The potential interaction of character Motivations
and Duty can inspire the GM to weave a complex story-
line, complete with surprises from the past or unknown
connections between characters. This approach works
best if the details trickle out during the adventure, fore-
shadowing larger revelations. However, an unexpected
connection or reveal can add more tension or shock to
an already climactic scene.

Motivation XP bonuses should not be automatically
achievable in every game session. If possible, the GM
should use rewards or story issues to make sticking to a
Motivation a real choice. The character gains the bonus
XP, but loses out on some other reward and causes diffi-
culty with the plot or other characters. Sticking to a Moti-
vation should be an interesting, and sometimes difficult,
choice. Otherwise, the bonus XP is too easily achievable.

REWARDING
MOTIVATIONS

Mechanically, the reward for adhering to a Motiva-
tion is simple: 5 XP per session, with a one-time 10
XP reward for an exceptional session, occurring once
in the characters life. Additional rewards are not usu-
ally necessary, as they become the regular awards nor-
mally integrated into the campaign.

Players should be rewarded for sticking to their
Motivations, especially when circumstances in the
game may cause them to stray from their ideals. Out-
side of the XP rewards given at the end of a session,
a player who adheres to his character’s Motivation
Should receive some sort of in-game reward, such as
a useful item, helpful information, or an advantageous
situation. This reward works best when the character
adamantly sticks to his Motivation, even if it runs coun-
ter to the goals of the group or otherwise impacts play.

Conversely, characters not sticking to their Motiva-
tion shouldn't receive a penalty. Motivations are there

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

to guide a character's choices, not dictate them. In
fact, there are many times in which a character must
set aside his own Motivation for the betterment of
the group or to move the story along. From a rules
sense, if a character doesn’t follow his Motivation with
conviction throughout a session, he merely doesn’t
receive additional XP at the end.

If a player consistently neglects or avoids his char-
acter’s Motivation, it’s a sign that perhaps the player
Is struggling with making the Motivation work, or
doesn’t understand how best to implement it during
the game. At the end of such a session, the GM might
consider talking to the player about altering the spe-
cific version of the Motivation to something that fits
the player's concept. For example, a character with
the Belief (Species Rights) Motivation might find him-
self more involved with fighting for freedom for every-
one and decide to change it to Belief (Freedom).

Alternatively, the GM could allow the player to
change his Motivation from one type to another—
Belief to Quest, for example. This should be avoided
if the original motivation is built into the GM's plans
or would otherwise deeply impact the story.

CREATING NEW MOTIVATIONS

The potential for character Motivations extends
beyond the three primary Motivations and their spe-
cific examples included in Ace oF REBELLION. Future
products will detail additional Motivations. GMs or
players may create their own Motivations using the
following guidelines:

e Check to see if the proposed Motivation fits as
either a Belief, Connection, or Quest. If not, it is
a new Motivation.

e Check to see if the proposed specific Motivation
fits with any of the existing specific Motivations. If
so, consider using the listed Motivation instead.

e Create a new category, if needed. Try creating a
Short list of specific Motivations with the new cat-
egory to test if it is broad enough.

e Establish the expected types of stories likely to
come from the new Motivation, so the GM and
player fully understand the intended driving force
behind it.

e Make sure the new Motivation is something the
character will have to work to maintain. Avoid
Motivations that easily give up their XP awards
every session through actions a character might
normally take in any game session anyway,
regardless of story.

e Offer the new Motivation to the other players,
unless it is intended to remain secret in the game.


### Creating Encounters, Adventures, and Campaigns for Age of Rebellion

CREATING ENCOUNTERS,
ADVENTURES, AND CAMPAIGNS
FOR AGE OF REBELLION

dventures are the core of each game session.

Creating new adventures can be one of the most
exciting and enjoyable aspects of becoming a Game
Master. Creating an interesting story that is also fun
and mechanically balanced is challenging. This sec-
tion focuses on the creation of the three elements of
any AGE OF REBELLION game: encounters, adventures,
and campaigns, and on helping the GM meld them
together in ways that are thematically appropriate for
the setting.

AGE OF REBELLION VS.
OTHER STAR WARS
ADVENTURES

AGE OF REBELLION is focused on the epic conflict between
the Rebel Alliance and the Empire, and the ultimate
destiny of the galaxy. The time frame fits into the time-
frame during the original trilogy of Star Wars movies.
The fight against the might of the Empire by soldiers,
freedom fighters, saboteurs, spies, and partisans in
battles both large and small is the central theme of the
adventures. Stories about the purge of the Jedi and the
slow reemergence of those gifted in the Force will be
addressed in detail in upcoming supplements.

USING PUBLISHED
ADVENTURES

Using published adventures gives the GM a prime
example of how an AcE oF REBELLION-Style adventure
works. It also allows players from different gaming
groups to have shared experiences, allowing them to
compare how an adventure developed and the differ-
ences in their final outcomes. However, while published
adventures are useful, the GM will likely find he needs
to create his own adventures to extend the story or
bridge gaps between official adventures.

SETTING THE STAGE

AcE OF REBELLION /S built around three types of set-
tings: encounters, adventures, and campaigns. Encoun-
ters are individual scenarios during a gaming session
that together make up an adventure. Adventures are
grouped together to put together campaigns.

Regardless of the scenario, every AGE OF REBELLION
game has fundamental! building blocks that help define

the mode of a game, along with the types of NPCs the
Player Characters will encounter and ways for the GM
to start, progress the story, and finish. This section
describes the basic points found in any game and how
to make encounters, adventures, and campaigns fun
and exciting.

MODES OF PLAY

Before the Game Master begins any AcE oF REBELLION
adventure or campaign, he and the players should
decide what mode or style of play they want in the game.
Are they looking for non-stop action, full of combat and
explosions? Are they more interested in roleplaying,
dialogue, and intrigue? Do they want to span the gal-
axy or keep things in a few key locations? Determining
the mode of play goes a long way in shaping everything
about any encounter, adventure, or campaign. There are
two main modes of play: one that focuses on roleplay-
Ing and the other concerned with combat and action.
Neither is better or worse, and most games use a blend
of the two, either within individual encounters or alter-
nating combat or intense actions with deep interaction
and plotting with other characters.

EMPHASIS ON ROLEPLAYING

These types of games put the players deep in a rich
tableau of characters, intrigue, and interaction with
other characters. The story is king, as players are more
focused on finding out the how and why of a situation
than on merely blowing it up. Players are just as likely
to banter and argue with their main villain or enemies
as they are to shoot at them.

However, even the most roleplay-intensive game
needs action to keep things from getting stale or to
ramp up the excitement. When combat happens, it’s
just as important to the story’s evolution, as it is an
injection of much-needed action. After a combat
encounter, characters may spend just as much time
interrogating prisoners or talking among themselves
about what just happened.

This mode of play encourages dialogue between
players and the Game Master. Improvisation and quick
thinking are critical, as players tend to ask questions
that are unexpected or can potentially derail the plot.
Lots of talking takes up time, as well, and particularly
chatty players could keep the story moving at a very
slow rate unless the GM makes a conscious effort to

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

move things along. Players who want to play a game
emphasizing roleplaying tend to invest a great deal of
time and effort into their character's personality and
backstory, so the GM should be prepared to craft
adventures and campaigns more around the players
than on providing set pieces for the PCs to explore.
The GM should also make it clear that all types of char-
acters are necessary for the game. Combat-oriented
characters should still be given chances to use their
impressive martial skills whenever possible and not get
“left behind” when dealing with NPCs.

GUNS BLAZING

This mode of play emphasizes everything about com-
bat. The Player Characters solve most of their situa-
tions with blasters, grenades, X-wings, and capital-
class starships. Encounters are built around moving
from one combat to the next, with enough variety to
allow characters with different fighting styles, such as
hand-to-hand or starship combat, to shine. On the
whole, the “guns blazing” mode of play focuses around
the mechanics of the game, letting roleplaying move
Slightly further into the background.

This is not to say that roleplaying is not an important
part of the game. It just means that the players spend
their time describing their actions in great detail, paint-
ing a vibrant, action-packed scene for everyone to add
to. Missions are straightforward: go assault this bun-
ker, take out the fleet's flagship, or defend a transport
convoy from attack. As with roleplay-focused games,
combat-oriented games still need a variety of charac-
ter types to move the story along. No character should
be penalized for creating a character that isn’t solely
geared toward shooting at everything.

Following the rules and the mechanics of AcE or REBEL-
LION is Critical to keeping this mode of game moving for-
ward. The Game Master should become very familiar
with both the combat rules and how talents and skills
work to keep the action from losing momentum. Stop-
ping to read about the basics of combat is guaranteed
to frustrate most players who enjoy this kind of game.

SETTING THE TONE: LIGHT AND DARK

Just as the Force has two sides, so too do roleplaying
games. Some games are light in tone, touching briefly
(or not at all) on the side effects of character action
and consequences. Other games are far darker, look-
ing at the grittier side of things. In addition to deciding
if a game will focus more on roleplaying or combat, so
too should the Game Master, along with the players,
decide how light or dark a tone they want to take.

Light-toned games move along at a rapid pace and
are usually very cut-and-dry in terms of morality and
ethics: the PCs and the Alliance are good and must
persevere. The Empire is evil and must be destroyed.
Civilians are rarely, if ever, caught in the crossfire, and

THE GAME MASTER
AGE OF REBELLION

oS

the effects of war and violence are glossed over. A
light-toned game may also be more humor-oriented,
although the GM should avoid allowing such games
to turn downright silly or outlandish. Star Wars has
its moments of humor to be sure, but the struggle
between the Alliance and the Empire lends a somber
backdrop that no one can forget.

Dark-toned games deal with more adult themes. Play-
ers find their characters in murkier territory when deal-
ing both with the enemy and with their own allies. Not
every citizen of even the most loyal Imperial-controlled
planet agrees or supports the Empire. Some members
of the Rebel Alliance are just as likely to fight on behalf
of the cause as to advance their own personal agendas.
Traitors and deserters are commonplace. Additionally,
GMs running dark-toned games should describe the
horrors of war in detail. After an encounter, civilians
may lay dead at the feet of the soldiers around them.
Historic buildings may crumble and fall after incessant
shelling. If the GM decides to pull out all the stops, then
disease, hunger, and terrible wounds may be the norm
in war-torn areas.

ASSEMBLING THE CAST

Every encounter, adventure, and campaign needs a cast
of memorable characters. Sometimes, the major charac-
ters are outlined first, with the story developing around
their goals and actions. Sometimes the plot comes first,
generating characters to fulfill certain roles or cover
aspects of the story. Usually, the characters and plot are
developed together and complement each other.

CREATING NON-PLAYER CHARACTERS

The Game Master is responsible for portraying all of
the other characters in the game. Populating the gal-
axy with a variety of unique characters may seem
daunting, but it is simpler than it appears. There are
three major types of Non-Player Characters in an AcE
OF REBELLION game, described below.

In order to keep things interesting, the Game Master
should strive to inject most NPCs with a small dash of
personality to make them memorable. Something as
simple as a strange accent or quirk will make even the
most plain of NPCs something that the PCs will remem-
ber. The GM can also use Table 9—4: Fifty Personal-
ity Traits for more ideas.

STOCK NPCS

Stock NPCs are the unnamed masses the Player Char-
acters encounter, negotiate with, and battle in most
game sessions. They are often minions or rivals (see
Chapter XII: Adversaries, page 409). Stock NPCs
in an Ace oF REBELLION adventure include the average
stormtrooper, stalwart Rebel soldier, and so on. Many
stock NPCs don't even need statistics, or can rely on
Standard stats provided in this book or others. Stock
