also enable the Rebel Alliance to work with decreased
fear for their friends and loved ones.

Secrecy is key to safe world survival. Their locations
are closely guarded and not held in normal Alliance
astrogation data. Only those pilots who must make trips
to a safe world are given data chips or astromech droids
with the coordinates. Safe worlds are often referred to
by codenames to further ensure their anonymity.

Rebel intelligence agents and scouts are always on
the lookout for suitable worlds that are unknown to the
Empire. While some safe worlds reside in overlooked
systems within Known Space, many are located in Wild
Space, and a few are near or within the edge of the
Unknown Regions. Safe worlds are almost always very
remote or difficult to travel to, in order to further pro-
tect them from accidental discovery.

Life on a safe world is typically rough and fairly
primitive. The planets are necessarily cut off from com-
munication with the rest of the galaxy, and many only
have limited access to starships, if at all. Travel to safe
worlds is minimized to further reduce the chance of
Imperial tracking or detection. Rebel Alliance Support
Services (see page 594) is responsible for maintain-
ing and supplying safe worlds. They are often short on
transports, which can affect delivery of supplies and
would-be inhabitants.

Some safe worlds have a large enough population
with sophisticated enough technology to grow food
and occasionally manufacture or provide other goods
to the Rebel Alliance. This enables the population to
participate in the war effort, even though they cannot
be on the front lines. It also reduces the Rebel’s reli-
ance on the regular or black markets for supplies.

PRISONERS OF WAR

Some safe worlds also hold Imperial prisoners of war.
As the Alliance does not have the manpower or the
money to guard, house, and feed the Imperial troops,
agents, and others taken captive, the prisoners are
dropped on isolated islands or continents far from
a Rebel settlement. There they must fend for them-
selves. Prisons initially start with enough basic supplies

THE GALAXY


### Life and Perils in Imperial Space

and shelter to get up and running. Rebel supply

ships occasionally replenish the goods and medie
cal supplies. The ships rarely land, as it is safer t@
simply air drop the containers into the area.

NEW ALDERAAN  %
/
After the destruction of Alderaan, the am .
Rebel Alliance wasted little time in sete i}
ting up a new safe planet for the offe )
world survivors. With a current pOpU=™
lation of about 15,000, New Alderaan ®
has quickly become a much-needed ©
retreat for those who have lost all faith
in the Empire and fear to be anywhere
near it. While the Alliance cannot read-
lly advertise its existence, Rebels with}
Alderaanian ties or heritage are eventually”
vetted, either covertly or openly by Sup-
port Services personnel, to determine their
interest in relocating to the world or sending
friends or relatives there. As expected, the
location is secret, but is somewhere generally
Coreward of Mon Calamari in the Outer Rim.

Though no world can replace Alderaan,
New Alderaan shares many features with its name-
sake's landscapes. It has wide, green fields, dense for-
ests, and tall mountains. The weather is very active,
and can be harsh at times. Living conditions on the
planet are very primitive, and its technology level is
very low. It is still largely reliant on imports, though the
colony is producing its own food for its own use.

T-STAR 2

T-Star 2 is the code name for a new Alliance safe world
located in a small section of Wild Space. It is spinward of
the tail end of the Corellian Run, in the Outer Rim, past
Smuggler’s Run. It is a terrestrial world, with polar con-
tinents dominated by massive, retreating glacial sheets
of a diminishing ice age. The equatorial zone is temper-
ate and free of ice. It is largely open ocean dotted with
island chains and two island continents. The temperate
weather, combined with the planet’s orbit, creates the
perfect conditions for dense forests, rather than the jun-
gle conditions one often finds on the equatorial zones
of other worlds. Massive winter storms roar down from

the ice sheets, bringing with them 1ée"s 10w, and o er
winter conditions, even in the hotter summernime,

The Rebel settlement is on the coast of the largest
continent in the massive river delta formed by the big
river flowing from the center of the continent. The settle-
ment holds about 5,000 people, mostly humans, with
some Wookiees and other commonly enslaved species.
Between their farming and fishing efforts, they are able
to feed themselves and export food back to the Alliance.

On the opposite side of the planet, the Rebels have
established an Imperial prison colony on an isolated
island chain. The largest island is about 50 square
kilometers in size and is surrounded by half a dozen
smaller islands, which extend northeast to southwest of
the main island. While the large island is best for farm-
ing and hunting, ferocious predators have encouraged
many of the Imperial prisoners to relocate to the outly-
ing islands. Most of the Imperial prisoners are captured
Imperial Navy crew members and officers, with the
occasional army trooper and even an ISB agent or two.
The current Imperial population is about 400, mainly
humans. They are all looking for a way out.

LIFE AND PERILS IN IMPERIAL SPACE

f or the average Imperial citizen, life under the Emper-
ors authoritarian hand largely depends upon where
that person lives, and often what species he is. For
humans, the Imperial age is a human-centric time. So-
called aliens, especially those who once were central

THE GALAXY
AGE OF REBELLION

=

to the Separatist movement, have been increasingly
pushed out of the halls of power. Human Imperial gov-
ernors often oversee non-human homeworlds and colo-
nies. Human-owned corporations are typically favored
over those owned by non-humans. The Commission

for the Preservation of the New Order, or COMPNOR,
and its Imperial political allies actively promote the
concept of human high culture and human superiority.
While such attitudes are prevalent in the Core Worlds
and Imperial strongholds elsewhere in the galaxy, they
often quickly diminish in other regions and on non-
human Core Worlds.

The worst of these attitudes result in the institutional
enslavement of entire species. The enslavement of the
Wookiees is the most obvious example, though there
are many others, such as the virtual enslavement of
the insect-like Verpine. Such species are typically not
allowed to travel on their own, and risk capture and
arrest for doing so. While one traveling on its own
might draw additional Imperial scrutiny, the fact is,
most citizens do not care, or cannot easily tell if an
alien they see is travelling on his own or simply carrying
out Imperial orders.

However, not every non-human species or corpo-
ration has fared so poorly under Imperial rule. Some
have made deals to keep themselves relatively free of
Imperial interference. If they benefit the Empire in tax
revenue or other areas, they often retain more auton-
omy. The Sullustan vehicle and industrial company
SoroSuub is a prime example.

IMPERIAL LAW AND ORDER

Generally speaking, life for the average citizen in most
Imperial systems is relatively quiet. Criminal activity
appears relatively low, though this is often a facade,
with corrupt officials working behind the scenes rather
than out in the open (as they did in the Republic). In
more remote regions, Imperial crackdowns on pirates
are welcome. In these areas and most Coreward worlds,
the Rebels are typically viewed as a danger to peace
and society.

With the destruction of the Death Star, the Rebel-
lion is now viewed as public enemy number one. Rebel
sympathizers are hunted down by the ISB and other
Imperial agencies. Just the threat of Rebel sympathy
is often enough to destroy reputations and result in
Imperial investigations and detention. The Imperial
propaganda machine rarely fails to cast the Rebellion
in the worst possible light, making it difficult for the
Alliance to instill the truth in the public consciousness,

While security is always strict under Imperial rule,
it has increased even further during the Empire’s
relentless search for Rebel agents and sympathizers.
Travel and commerce are affected by increased scru-
tiny. Permits, identification, and licenses are regularly
demanded and checked for authenticity. The closer to
the Core Worlds one travels, the heavier the security
and the better the Empire’s response times.

Those arrested on suspicion of Rebel activity are
transported and held in the local detention facility. Local
law enforcement may question them, but it isn't long
before the ISB or Imperial military arrives to investigate
and possibly take custody of the prisoner. If a trial is
demanded by the administrators (whether or not it is
warranted), it can be a public spectacle or closed-door
affair, depending on the subject matter. Convictions
result in long incarcerations, isolated confinement, work
camps, or even the spice mines of Kessel.

IMPERIAL SECURITY ZONES

Throughout the Empire, different planets, systems,
regions, and sectors have different levels of security.
The largest zone is the Deep Core Security Zone (see
page 350), which is also the most heavily guarded.
Other zones are much smaller, typically encompassing
a star system, planet, or less. These zones often sur-
round an area or object of strategic or military impor-
tance. Many of the smaller zones are ideal targets for
Rebel raids and attacks.

The most common zone typically surrounds Imperial
Shipyards. Imperial warships are particularly vulner-
able in shipyards, because the shipyards are a known
location, and craft undergoing repair are there for long
periods of time. Rebel raids typically target specific
starships or the repair facilities themselves, disrupt-
ing Imperial activity in a given system or sector. Ship-
yards may be as small as a single spacedock, include
a cluster or planetary ring of stardocks, or even stretch
across entire star systems, such as at Kuat.

Other zones are established around specific worlds.
Enslaved worlds like Kashyyyk are heavily defended
and patrolled, both in space and on the ground. Some
worlds are temporarily blockaded in retaliation for
clashing with the Empire, such as Yavin 4 after the
destruction of the Death Star. Some zones are secret,
and even flying into the area with improper permits or
transponders results in instant attack—such as in the
Deep Core Security Zone, or certain Imperial construc-
tion projects, like the Death Star.

PRIORITY SECTORS AND OVERSECTORS

Disregarding the traditional sector boundaries, priority
sectors encompass many areas or sectors with active
Rebel threats. Usually the equivalent of two sector mili-
tary forces operates in the oversector, commanded by
a Moff or Grand Moff. These forces operate outside
the regular sector command structure, enabling them
to respond more quickly to threats that span or cross
systems and sectors. For example, Oversector Outer
covers much of the Outer Rim Territories.

THE GALAXY


### Other Notable Locations

OTHER NOTABLE LOCATIONS

n° all galactic geographic areas are classified by
planet, system, sector, or major region. Some titles
are informal spacer slang for a hyperroute or collec-
tion of stars. Others are holdovers from past interstel-
lar empires or long-held cultural connections. As the
Republic expanded and encountered existing interstel-
lar political bodies, it often granted them Allied Region
Status. Most eventually joined or were otherwise incor-
porated into the Republic as sectors. However, a few
Allied Regions remain, although most travelers would
regard them as part of Republic or Imperial space.
some Allied Regions and other bodies still retain signifi-
cant political power. A very few are fully autonomous,
even under the Empire.

BOTHAN SPACE

Bothan Space, an Allied Region in the Mid Rim, encom-
passes over 150 inhabited systems primarily compris-
ing Bothan colonies. It is ruled by the Bothan Council
and Bothan clans. Bothan Space remains an Allied
Region under the Empire, but it is closely monitored
and still subject to Imperial decree. Despite this, the
Bothans provide aid to the Rebellion and allow them to
operate with limited protection within Bothan Space.
The Bothan Spynet helps to conceal the Rebels’ pres-
ence, as any connection between the Bothans and the
Rebellion would likely result in an Imperial crackdown
nd endanger the Bothans’ Allied Region status.

CORPORATE SECTOR

The Corporate Sector lies on the outer edge of the
Outer Rim Territories, at the end of the Hydian Way.
It borders Wild Space on the western edge. Politically,
the Corporate Sector is the result of experiments with
total corporate control over a major region of space.
The sector is administrated by the Corporate Sector
Authority and controlled by companies participating in
the conglomerate. Though the area changes over time,
it currently contains about 30,000 worlds divided into
twenty-nine territories. The CSA controls everything
about the sector, serving as government, security, and
sole employer. Imperial ships conduct Imperial busi-
ness, but the Imperial military is virtually non-existent
in the sector.

The CSA uses and exploits the resources of tens of
thousands of star systems and controls vast amounts
of wealth. Workers drawn to the promise of jobs and
Stable economic livelihoods soon learn the realities of
living under a corporate master. The CSA controls virtu-
ally everything, including paychecks, the cost of goods
and services, and access to anything anyone needs or
wants. While small independent companies and mer-
chants are allowed to operate, they are squeezed by
CSA pricing and contracts. CSA profits drive virtually
all decision-making. Abuses of power are common.
Conditions are often poor and hazardous, meeting the
barest minimum needed to keep operations running at _
low cost. The region is so remote that the Rebellion has ©
had very little impact here.

y

HERGLIC SPACE

The Herglics established their own empire well before
the rise of the Old Republic, but it failed long ago. The
Empire seized control of Herglic industry, and now mer-
cilessly rules the Herglics with an iron fist. Herglic Space
is still considered an Allied Region, and includes about
forty systems, mainly near the Rimma Trade Route.

HUTT SPACE

The Hutts are among the oldest civilizations in the gal-
axy. They control a large region of space along the Mid
Rim and Outer Rim border in the Slice. They have been
a fixture of galactic politics, criminality, and gamesman-
ship for thousands of years. The borders of Hutt Space
have fluctuated over the centuries, along with their
power. The borders that stretched Coreward during the
Clone Wars were quickly beaten back by the Empire.

The Hutts are nominally Imperial subjects, with Hutt
Space under Imperial control and ruled from the Teth
system. In reality, the Hutts largely govern their own
affairs and their worlds, and the Empire allows them to
do so as long as they remain a lucrative tax resource.
The Hutts do not support the Rebel Alliance or willingly
harbor them within their borders. They are quite willing
to make money from the Rebels, and manipulate them
to the Hutts’ advantage, but they are not in the busi-
ness of rebellion or resistance without profit.

MANDALORIAN SPACE

As the home region of the fearsome Mandalorian war-
riors and their homeworld of Mandalore, the extents of
Mandalorian Space have expanded and shrunk in line
with its fortunes. At the height of the ancient Mandalo-
rian Wars, Mandalorian Space stretched from the edge
of the Inner Rim to the northeastern edge of the Outer
Rim. While other systems and areas Coreward were also
conquered, they were occupied Republic worlds.

Even in modern times, this vast area is among the
least populated in the galaxy. By the time of the Clone
Wars and the rise of the Empire, Mandalorian Space
largely meant the Mandalore sector. Though nominally
loyal to the Republic, factions within the Mandalorian
ranks exert cultural and political dominance. By the
time of the Empire, few publicly followed the old ways.
Boba Fett is often viewed as the best-known represen-
tative of this ancient warrior culture. As the Mandalori-
ans are involved in their own affairs, most are not inter-
ested in the growing Rebellion.

SHADOWPORTS

Shadowports typically operate without any regard to
any type of government regulation. Many rely on hid-
den, remote, or mobile locations, while others bribe law
enforcement. Some ports are simply too big and busy to

be adequately policed, sustaining an ongoing mix of legal
and illegal activity, even in Imperial or otherwise-regulated
space. Shadowports may be found in every region of the
galaxy, though many are in the Outer Rim Territories.

Shadowport juggernauts Nar Shaddaa and Kwenn
Space Station are destinations in Hutt Space. Tatooine,
Terminus, Bespin, and Socorro are a few of the better-
known ports in the Outer Rim. Even the Core Worlds
are not immune, with the once-reliable Abregado-rae
sliding into the realm of shadowy dealings. In truth, no
one knows how many shadowports exist, as most rely
on some form of secrecy for their continued operation.
While the mega-shadowports benefit from their noto-
riety, there are far more smaller ports known only to
local patrons, smugglers, and criminals.

While major shadowports host a vast array of illegal
activities and contraband, smaller ports tend to spe-
cialize in local needs. At a minimum, they are excellent
meeting places for those who wish to avoid regulatory
competition. Most rely on smuggling in some form,
whether as enabler and purchaser. Some shadowports
include starship repair and upgrade services, useful for
those requiring clandestine repairs or illegal upgrades
to their ships. Though relatively safe from government
agents, shadowports are still dangerous. Owners and
operators are always on guard, protecting the opera-
tion from government agents and rivals. Large criminal
enterprises run many shadowports, posing a danger to
any who challenge their control, or simply disposing of
anyone they feel is a threat.

As the Rebels must often operate outside Imperial
law, they naturally must rely on contacts, goods, and
services offered through shadowports. Some Rebel
agents have no qualms about dealing with any under-
world operation if it gets them to their goal. However,
Rebel high commanders prefer to limit the Rebellion’s
dependence on crime lords and the lowlife of the galaxy.

THE SLICE

The Slice is the vast, wedge-shaped region of the gal-
axy between the Perlemian Trade Route and the Corel-
lian Run. Like a region, the Slice is largely a geographic
designation and not a political division. No one con-
trols the Slice.

The Slice, which extends from the Core Worlds all the
way to the edge of the galaxy, was the heart of galac-
tic exploration and development for millennia. Almost
every usable world in it has been mapped and explored,
from the Expansion Region to the Core Worlds. Beyond
the Expansion Region, the volatility of Hutt Space and
the vastness of the Outer Rim means that there are
still worlds to be discovered, developed, and exploited.
Galactic exploration flowed along the Perlemian Trade
Route and the Corellian Run before turning toward the
inner reaches of the Slice. Aside from the Outer Rim and
portions of the Expansion Region, a large network of
hyperroutes ties the area together.

THE GALAXY

ALDERAAN

Astronavigation Data: Alderaan sys-
tem, Alderaan.sector, Core region

Orbital Metrics: 464 days per year
/ 24 hours per day

Government: democracy

Population: 2 billion |
(human 95%, other 5%)

Languages: Basic
Terrain: forest, plains

Major Cities: Aldera (capital), Cre- °
vasse City

Areas of Interest: Castle Lands, Pet-
rified Mounds of the Killiks

Major Exports: wine, art, luxury goods

Major Imports: manufactured goods,
electronics

Trade Routes: Commenor Run

Special Conditions: planet destroyed, plane covers
pre-destruction information |

Background: The founding of Alderaan by human
colonists predates the Republic. However, even this
ancient heritage was not the earliest civilization on
the planet. The insectoid Killik species, once native
to the planet, departed long before, leaving behind
towering mound-like structures to become petrified
* monuments to their civilization.

Alderaan was a prominent, cultured world often
acting at or near the peak of galactic politics and
influence. It was a beautiful place, with a gorgeous
landscape that matched the Alderaanian desire for
high culture and art. The galaxy benefited signifi-
cantly from Alderaanian cultural exports For: centu-
ries, the Alderaanians avoided despoiling the world,
building their urban centers in canyon crevasses, on
stilts over the seas, or in other unusual areas.

Alderaan was a democracy but maintained its tra-
ditional royal family. The Organas served as its repre-
sentatives in the Republic and Imperial Senate. Much
like their people, they: were outwardly peaceful and
held deep beliefs about law, fairness, and morality
that drove their: politics and contributions to galac-
tic life. As Senators, whether in the Republic or the
Empire, the Organas were always influential and often
central to the Galaxy’s most important issues. After
the brutal Clone Wars, Bail Organa led the effort for
Alderaan to become a pacifist world.

Bail Organa, and later, his daughter Leia Organa
were skilled at publicly riding the line between criti-
cism and outright treason. Behind the scenes, they

THE GALAXY

374: AGE OF REBELLION

and many other Alderaanian citizens organized resis-
tance to the Empire immediately after the Emperor
took control. They carried out countless actions,
building valuable experience for the more direct and .
overt actions to come. Bail Organa was.a key founder
of the Rebel Alliance, and Leia participated directly in
operations against the Empire.

These attitudes and actions eventually caught up
with Alderaan when the Empire made it the first tar-
get of their brand-new weapon of galactic terror, the
Death Star. With the destruction of Alderaan, the
Empire made it the prime example of the fate of trea-
sonous worlds. No world was safe if it stepped out of
line. With one shot, any troublesome planet could be
removed from the equation.

THE GRAVEYARD OF ALDERAAN

he blasted remains of the planet Alderaan

became known as the Graveyard. It is an
unpredictable, extremely dense asteroid field
that is hazardous to approach or navigate.
Tales of ghost ships, surviving structures, or
lost treasures periodically draw people to
search the ruins, and have been used as politi-
cal and intelligence ploys to draw out Rebels
and their supporters.

Not long after the planet’s destruction, the
surviving Alderaanians began making trips to
the Graveyard to pay their respects. This soon
became a tradition called the Return.

BYSS

Astronavigation Data: Beshqek system, Deep
Core Security Zone, Deep Core region

Orbital Metrics: 207 days per year:/ 31
hours per day

Government: dark side theocracy

Population: 19.7 billion
(human 98%, other 2%)

Languages: Basic
Terrain: lakes, plateaus
Major Cities: unknown

Areas of Interest: Imperial Citadel, Impe-
rial Freight Complex, Desolation Peak

Major Exports: none

Major Imports: high. technology, processed
foods, weapons

_ Trade Routes: Byss Run
Special Conditions: none

Background: Byss is the Emperor’s personal, pri-
vate, and secret retreat, hidden at the end of the
treacherous Byss Run in the Deep Core. It was once
a lush world, rich in life, with chains of lakes stretch-
ing across the landscape. Unfortunately, the Emperor
has corrupted this world, much as he corrupted the
galaxy, with his devotion to the dark side of the Force.

Over time, much of the world has become despoiled

by industry and by the twisted nature’ of the dark °

side. The Emperor recruited and attracted nobles
and others loyal to his cause, sending them to live on
Byss to carry out his darkest plans. Some he taught
the basic tenants of the dark side: dominating the
weak, empowering the strong, and enslaving all.

THE EMPEROR'S DARKEST SECRETS

he Emperor denies the existence of Byss

publicly. That is, he does so until he deems
someone worthy of the dark side of the Force.
Then he masterfully manipulates the situation,
seemingly bringing the individual into his con-
fidence, enticing the individual with promises
of power and influence to convince his target
to apply for relocation to Byss.

Once there, the subject soon finds it is likely a
permanent arrangement. It is almost impos-
sible for anyone to leave without permission,
and there is no offworld communication.
Worse, the dark side energies on the world,
among other things, keep most people in a
strange, dream-like state.

When on Byss, the Emperor resides and operates.

from the towering, ominous Imperial Citadel. He has
dark side guardians who never leave Byss and remain
undetected by the rest of the galaxy. Among them

~ are the Imperial Sentinels, giant humanoids devoted

to Palpatine and corrupted by the dark side.

As ghastly as the planet is, Palpatine holds an even
greater secret. He is growing clones of himself for
future purposes not yet revealed. Perhaps his pow-
erful visions have given him the foresight needed to
escape death. As with all things, he certainly has a
plan to outwit his foes.

Byss is phenomenally well-protected. Just locating
astrogation data for Beshgek and the Byss Run is dif-
ficult and dangerous, as they officially don’t exist in
the Imperial databases. Searches draw the. attention
of the ISB or the feared Inquisitors. For those caught
looking, arrest is the least of their worries. Even if a
ship manages to arrive at Beshgek, an entire fleet of
starships, suitable to protect an entire sector, patrols
the system.

Of course, regular flights keep the world supplied
with goods, and those ships come from somewhere.
Many are Imperial military or intelligence craft. When
Specialized ships or additional labor is needed, well-
compensated smugglers are occasionally hired. Few
ever speak of the experience, on pain of death.

THE GALAXY

“375

Astronavigation Data: Chandrila sys-
tem, Bormea sector, Core region

Orbital Metrics: 368 days in a year
/ 20 hours in a day

Government: Imperial governor-
ship (limited democracy)

Population: 1.2. billion
96%, other 4%)

Languages: Basic

(human

Terrain: plains, forest

Major Cities: Hanna City (capital),
Emita, Nayli

Areas of Interest: Hanna Wild Game
Reserve, Gladean State Parks, Lake.
Sahot, Brionelle Memorial Military
Academy, Silver Sea

Major Exports: foodstuffs

Major Imports: high technology

Trade Routes: Perlemian Trade Route _
Special Conditions: none

Background: Chandrila is a lightly populated Core
World with a thriving agrarian society, which is uncom-
mon among powerful Core systems. It is a beautiful

world, with grassy plains, dense forests, and protected ©

nature areas. Chandrila is much less industrialized, but
no less influential, than many of its political equals.

_ Chandrila’s traditional democratic governing body
has long encouraged intense discussion and lively
debate. It has proven to be an effective training ground

for the world’s Republic and Imperial Senators. It also °

helped train once-Republic and Imperial Senator Mon
Mothma ‘to be an effective foil and adversary to’ the
Imperial government. Now, Mon Mothma openly
leads the Rebel Alliance from its secret bases.

Chandrila has suffered for Mon Mothma’s actions.
The Empire retaliates in many ways, from arresting
Chandrila’s: politicians to placing costly tariffs on
agrarian goods from the Bormea sector. Despite this,

‘the Imperial Governor still allows and tolerates the
world’s democratic government to operate, though
the Governor retains ultimate control over the world.

Mon Mothma’s example has led others to follow -

her path of resistance and Rebellion. The planet has
become a decent recruiting ground for those opposed
to the Empire, and individuals with interstellar influ-
ence export these ideas to other worlds. The destruc-
tion of Alderaan suppressed this activity out of fear
of Imperial reprisals. So far, the world has avoided
the worst of Imperial retaliation, and does what it can
to stay out of the Imperial crosshairs.

THE GALAXY

376" AGE OF REBELLION

Despite the political difficulties, life on Chandrila
continues much as it did under the Republic. Impe-
rial interference, blockades, and non-military retalia-
tion affect some day-to-day activities, but some of the
worst Imperial institutions have had difficulty estab-
lishing themselves. COMPNOR finds little support,
and tires of the never-ending accusations and debate
that come naturally to many Chandrilians. While the
world’s leaders oppose the Empire on many fronts,
many of their citizens still support the regime, and
are just as adept at arguing their points for it. Such
attitudes may have saved the world from even stricter
Imperial crackdowns, or even the fate of Alderaan.

RECRUITING AND DEBATE

handrila serves as a recruiting ground for

both sides of the Galactic Civil War. With
debate a popular pastime among the politi-
cians and citizenry, concealing one’s political
views completely is rare among Chandrilans.

Recruitment comes not only from the general
population, but from Chandrila’s impressive
Brionelle Memorial Military Academy, which
trains officers for the home defense fleet. Agents
from both sides keep a watchful eye on the best
recruits and their apparent political leanings.

DAC
(MON CALAMARI)

Astronavigation Data: Calamari system,
Calamari sector, Outer Rim region

Orbital Metrics: 398 days per year / 21
hours per day

Government: representative council

Population: 27.5 billion (Quarren 60%,
Mon Calamari 39%, other 1%)

Languages: Mon Calamarian, Quarren-
ese, Basic

Terrain: oceans, reefs

Major Cities: Aquarius, Coral City,
Coral Depths, Foamwander, Heurkea

Areas of Interest: Mkbuto Seatree Preserve,
Knowledge Bank

Major Exports: seafood, high technology, starships,
weapons

Major Imports: foodstuffs, medicine, technology
Trade Routes: Overic Griplink
Special Conditions: none

Background: Known as Mon Calamari in official
Republic and Imperial records, or simply Mon Cala,
the ocean-covered world is called Dac by the native
Mon Calamari and Quarren species. Mon Calamari
is a relative newcomer to the Republic. Its remote
location in the Outer Rim, away from the traditional
major hyperroutes, deterred early exploration by
ancient Republic scouts.

It was out in space where the Republic and Mon
Calamari ships first made contact. The Mon Cala-
mari were encouraged, and sought further study and

ORBITAL SHIPYARDS

efore embarking on interstellar explora-

tion, the Mon Calamari and Quarren built
an orbital ring of labs and construction facili-
ties. From there, they designed and developed
the starships and other equipment needed for
exploration and potential colonization. The ring
has since become an enormous facility with mil-
lions of designers, workers, and builders.

Mon Calamari ships, especially large and
important ships, are typically built as pieces of
art. While specific classes hold the same gen-
eral shape and capabilities, every ship is cus-
tomized and specialized according to the task
and artistic whims of the builders.

communications with their new, fascinating neighbors.
The Quarren were much more cautious. They feared
the Mon Calamari were moving much too quickly. This
division marked the end of a period of cooperation
between the species. The division only got worse as
the Republic deteriorated into the Empire.

The Empire wasted no time in taking control of
the system and the shipyards. The Mon Calamari
attempted to negotiate. When that failed, the Empire
attacked, benefiting from friendly Quarren who
believed Imperial control was necessary, especially at
the expense of the Mon Calamari.

The Imperials proved unable to convert the ship-
yards to produce Imperial standard craft. While they
tried, Mon Calamari resistance groups organized and
began fighting back through nonviolent means. Even-
tually, the Imperials retaliated with a devastating
orbital bombardment against three floating cities.

Instead of buckling under the pressure, both the
Mon Calamari and the Quarren openly fought back.
They secretly modified many ships for combat and
harassed the Imperial fleet incessantly. Eventually, —
the Imperials concluded that holding Mon Calamari
wasn't worth the effort or losses due to the remote
location. Finally, after the Battle of Yavin, the Mon
Calamari openly and formally joined the Rebellion,
one of the few worlds to openly do so. The system is
now protected by Rebel starships, often coming right
off Mon Calamari’s own production lines.

THE GALAXY

AGE OF REBELLION ‘377

HOTH.

Astronavigation Data: Hoth ‘system,
Javin (Anoat) sector, Outer Rim region

Orbital Metrics: 549 days aa year
/ 25 hours per day

Government: none

Population: no permanent
population

Languages: none —

Terrain: frozen plains, mountains,
snow .

Major Cities: none

Areas of Interest: none
Major Exports: none
Major Imports: none

Trade Routes: Ison Corridor

Special Conditions: harsh cold, blizzard and
winter storm conditions common

Background: Hoth is an icy, frozen world with. brutal
weather conditions at the best of times. The planet
is so remote and isolated that it doesn’t appear on
many star charts. While this would appear to make
it a prime location for pirates, smugglers, and those
who don't wish to be found, the few who try don't
Stay very long. Hoth’s taxing conditions don’t stop at
the edge of the atmosphere, either. Meteors originat-
ing from a nearby asteroid field regularly strike the
_ Surface, posing a hazard to starships and. potential
settlements alike.

Hoth’s massive ice sheets cover most of the plan-.

et’s surface, concealing an enormous ocean. Natural
tidal and geological pressures form spectacular land-
scapes of deep fissures, mountain-like ice formations,
and translucent white-blue canyons. The mix of phys-
ics, water, and cold produces unexpected geysers
that freeze into towering, icy columns and arches.

Surprisingly, life exists on this frozen ball of ice.
Fungal and algae growths in certain areas beneath
the ice provide a limited food source to some animal
lifeforms. Tauntauns live on the snowbound plains,
and herds rely on one another to find food and pro-
vide communal warmth. Not all life is so docile. Huge,

powerful bipedal creatures called wampas hunt virtu-:

ally every other living thing that might make a good
meal. A wampa that finds a herd of tauntauns shel-
tering in a cave is in for a meal, fight, and stampede.
Among the few rumors and stories of Hoth are tales
of fire-breathing dragon creatures living in some of
the deeper ice fissures.

THE GALAXY

378" AGE OF REBELLION

The Rebel Alliance has taken a recent interest in the
world as a possible location for a new hidden base.
If viable, the Rebels have much work to do to build a
base and adapt their equipment to the severe cold.

HIDING OUT

pits. smugglers, and fugitives occasion-
ally use Hoth as a temporary hideout. The
remote conditions usually make any such stay
short-lived. There are no permanent settle-
ments or outposts on the planet, and those
who know of the world find no economic value
in the ice, snow, or even the creatures they
find. Tauntauns make good mounts, and are
occasionally captured and exported, but are
difficult to build a successful business around.

Those who do find temporary shelter in the ice
usually bring their own living quarters. While
this is typically a starship, it might also be pre-
fabricated structures set on the ice or hidden
within the caves. Such activity usually frightens
off more timid creatures, but small groups or
individuals wandering alone eventually attract
the attention of any wampa in the area. Wam-
pas are always dangerous and may attack indi-
vidually or in small packs.

IMPERIAL CENTER
(CORUSCANT) ©

Astronavigation Data: Coruscant system,
Corusca sector, Core region

Orbital Metrics: 368 days per year / 24
hours per day

Government: Imperial dictatorship

Population: 1 trillion (human 78%,
other 22%)

Languages: Basic

Terrain: dense,
urban cityscape

multi-level, planetwide

Major Cities: Imperial City

Areas of Interest: Imperial Palace (formerly the
Jedi Temple), Galactic Museum, Imperial Senate,
Column Commons, Monument Plaza, The Works

Major Exports: culture
Major Imports: foodstuffs, medicinal goods

Trade Routes: Corellian Trade Spine, Perlemian
Trade Route, Namadii Corridor

Special Conditions: none

Background: Coruscant is among the most ancient
of the galaxy’s civilized worlds. Coruscant’s life and
civilization predate the Republic, as well as. the
-ancient Rakata Infinite Empire, by tens of thousands
of years. As the system of hyperspace routes grew
across the galaxy, Coruscant benefited significantly
from its location. Coruscant’s political and economic
power and influence became dominant. It grew to
become the capital of the Republic, a world that
every galactic government must ultimately control to
be considered legitimate.

CORUSCANT UNDERWORLD

is] Ss much as the Imperials would like to elimi-
nate or forget about the planet’s lower lev-
els, Coruscant’s ancient underworld operates
under Imperial rule much like it did under
the Republic. Poor and often dangerous, the
underworld conveniently hides criminals,
crime lords, and even the occasional Rebel
agent, dissident, or instigator.

The Imperial government has not abandoned
the lower levels. Instead, ISB, Underworld
Police operating out of specially designated
Federal Zones, and other agents monitor the
area and use informants to keep tabs on recent
developments. Any problems associated in any
way with Rebel activity receive first priority.

As the planet developed, the needs of the economic
powerhouse and galactic government brought with it
a massive boost in population. The world was already
covered by layers of urban cityscape, and it kept grow-
ing. As the city stretched higher into the sky, the popu-
lation stratified. The wealthy and. powerful literally
rose to the top, leaving the lower levels to the poor.

As the center of galactic political power, Coruscant
held two of the galaxy’s most important entities—the
Republic Senate, and the Jedi Temple. Though typi-
cally allies, the two groups had their own agendas
and their own purposes for being. Both ultimately
failed. The Senate fell to corruption, while the Jedi
became arrogant and set in their ways.

The Empire renamed the world Imperial Center as a

~ powerful symbol that the Old Republic was gone for-

ever. The governing district became Imperial City. As
the seat of Imperial power, the planet is protected by

formidable fleets of Star Destroyers, numerous orbital

defense platforms, and powerful planetary shields.

In general, the citizens of the mid and upper levels.
are among the staunchest Imperial supporters in the
Empire. Most benefit daily from Imperial economic
activity, from the largest of suppliers to maintenance
workers and others living on Imperial wages. Rebel
activity is to be feared, and associating with Rebels
risks damage to reputation as well as Imperial arrest.

THE GALAXY
AGE OF REBELLION

w379

SULLUST

Astronavigation Data: Sullust. system,
Brema sector, Outer Rim region

Orbital Metrics: 263 days per year /
20 hours per day

Government: corporate

_ Population: 18.5 billion (Sullustan
96%, human 2%, Bith 1%,
other 1%)

Languages: Sullustese, Basic
Terrain: mountains, volcanoes, caves
Major Cities: Byllurun

Areas of Interest: SoroSuub Headquar-
ters, Piringiisi

Major Exports: starships, computers, droids,
hyperdrive and astrogation technology

Major Imports: foodstuffs, water
Trade Routes: Rimma Trade Route
Special Conditions: breath mask or similar appara-

tus often required outside when volcanoes are active .

Background: Sullust is a volcanic world. Its atmosphere
is filled with dangerous and poisonous gases, making
life on the surface difficult. To escape this reality, the
native Sullustan species quickly learned to live under
the surface in natural caves and carved-out caverns.

The atmosphere is a roiling mix of noxious clouds
and gases. The gas mix in a given area may change
radically depending on the winds and the density of
the substances involved. Those who venture onto its
Surface are advised to carry breath masks at the very

least, and fully. enclosed environmental protection’

Suits in the worst areas.

Beneath the surface, pools of water and other for-
mations allow the Sullustans to thrive. The Sullustans
have created a technologically advanced society, and
have engineered pressure relief vents, diversion tun-
nels, and other structures to help quell some of the
volcanic activity and stabilize their homes and cities.
Their aptitudes for piloting and navigation helped
~ them to explore, create, and develop the Rimma Trade
Route. Their efforts ultimately benefited one company
the most—the powerful SoroSuub Corporation.

While SoroSuub long dominated Sullustan society, :

by the time of the Clone Wars it was powerful enough
to mandate joining the Separatist cause over the
objections of the government, known as the Sullustan
Council. When the Separatists collapsed, the company
quickly reformed itself, becoming a staunch supporter
and ally of the Empire. As many Sullustans opposed
the Empire, SoroSuub dissolved the Sullustan Council
and took direct control of the government.

THE GALAXY

380" AGE OF REBELLION

A significant percentage of Sullustans continue to
oppose the Empire. The human-centric attitudes and
actions irritate and anger the Sullustans. Their oppo-
sition to these attitudes and totalitarian policies is
slowly influencing SoroSuub decision-making. How-
ever, some are unwilling to wait for corporate minds
to change and are establishing resistance groups of
their own, if not joining the Rebels directly. There is
hope that if the Rebels continue to make progress,
and their support among the Sullustan people contin-
ues to grow, the world might someday change sides.

Sa Corporation is an industrial pow-
erhouse and one of the galaxy’s preeminent
interstellar corporations. Although capable of
producing starships of all makes and types, it
primarily sells its warships to planetary gov-
ernments and corporate clients, having lost
out to other manufacturers for Imperial con-
tracts. SoroSuub produces an enormous array
of planetary vehicles, which can be found
throughout the galaxy. It has a highly suc-
cessful weapons division, which broke into the
Imperial market by creating the Stormtrooper
One clone of the standard BlasTech E-11
blaster carried by stormtroopers. They build
highly specialized droids, and have very suc-
cessful tool and technical equipment divisions.

YAVIN 4

Astronavigation Data:
Yavin system, Gordian Reach
sector, Outer Rim region

Orbital Metrics: 4,818 days
per year / 24 hours per day .

Government: none |
Population: no permanent population
Languages: none

Terrain: jungle, rainforest

Major Cities: none

Areas of Interest: Great Temple (aban-
doned Rebel Base), Temple of Exar Kun

Major Exports: none
Major Imports: none
‘Trade Routes: none
Special Conditions: none

Background: Yavin 4 is best known for foie the
Rebel Alliance’s secret base and, not coincidentally, ts
the location of the Rebellion’s first major victory with
the destruction of the Death Star. Though remote, this
wasn’t the first galactically important event to occur on
the world. Over the centuries, Yavin 4 has been impor-
tant, if long forgotten, to both the Jedi and Sith causes.

The fourth moon of the-gas giant Yavin, Yavin 4 is

a lush, green world of dense jungle and extraordinary

forms of life. It is covered with enormous Massassi trees,

with huge branches and crowns. The jungle holds amaz-
ing life forms, including woolamanders and runyips.

The Sith Lord Naga Sadow first settled the jungle
moon with a Sith warrior species known as the Mas-
sassi. Under Sadow’s command, they built the great
temples that the Rebel Alliance would someday
repurpose for their main base. Centuries later, Yavin
was rediscovered by the Jedi Knight Freedon Nadd,
who learned the Sith arts there. 7

THE FORCE AND YAVIN 4

here is a strong connection between the

Force and Yavin 4— particularly the dark side.
After the Rebels finally abandon their base, the
Empire installs an outpost to analyze the Death
Star and base remains. However, Force-using
characters might find the stories about Yavin’s
past—even its recent past—interesting enough
to check out. While it is unlikely, or undesirable,
for them to learn too much about the ancient
past, they could certainly get hints about the
dark side that might teach them something
new, if potentially undesired.

Nadd was eventually entombed on Onderon, but
another Jedi named Exar Kun awakened his spirit
centuries later. Nadd guided him to Yavin 4, where

the Massassi captured Kun. Nadd underestimated
Exar Kun, and the fallen Jedi defeated him. Exar Kun
launched the Sith War, but the Jedi.eventually chased
him to Yavin 4 and obliterated the jungle in an orbital
bombardment. Exar Kun used Sith sorcery to drain
the Massassi’s life energies to save his own spirit,
while the Jedi erased the system from the galactic
charts to prevent future contact.

Eventually, the system was rediscovered by pede

lic scouts. The moon and jungle had recovered, with

life once again covering the world. Centuries after
the end of the Sith War, the Rebel Alliance selected

* the moon as their new main base after abandoning

the Dantooine system. They moved into the long-
abandoned Great Temple, not knowing its ancient

~ history, and made the place safe to inhabit. See page
398 for more on the Rebel Base.

Their secrecy was short lived. The Death Star tracked
Princess Leia and the Millennium Falcon to the moon.
With the Death Star maneuvering into firing position
to unleash its planet-killing superlaser, Rebel fighters
desperately attacked the station. Luke Skywalker fired
the fatal shot, securing the Rebellion’s first victory and
saving the Alliance headquarters staff and leaders in
the process. See page 388 for more on the Battle of
Yavin and the aftermath.

THE GALAXY

AGE OF REBELLION “381

i
4
—_ ah

ar


## 11: The Rebellion

REBELLION

he-Rebellion is both an idea and an organization.

The idea-of rebellion appeals to all who are dissat-
isfied, disenfranchised, and outright horrified by the
actions and ideals of the Galactic Empire. Resistance
in many forms can be considered rebellious, but dedi-
cated and consistent dissent combined with regular
acts of public, targeted opposition moves one quickly
toward formal rebellion:

Resistance against the Empire began the day the
Emperor declared its existence, though it would take
decades for the unrest to grow widespread, orga-
nized, and strong enough to become a true threat to
Imperial power. Brave individuals fought against the
spreading evil, finding others of-like mind and heart.
Small groups became larger groups. Political dissi-
dents, both public and private, influenced those they
could reach by word or by action. Setbacks, arrests,

“E Ve | C [ | a | oy j i }

| think you overestimate their chances. -

our moment of triumph?

~Grand Moff Tarkin

public ridicule, and propaganda slowed the anti-lmpe-
rial cause until finally the would-be Rebels were strong
enough to strike militarily, socially, and politically.

The Rebellion is known by many names. Its formal
title is the Alliance to Restore the Republic. It’s more
often called the Rebel Alliance, the Rebels, or the
Rebellion. It is the largest Imperial opposition group
in the galaxy, fighting to topple the Emperor, disman-
tle the Empire, and restore the Republic that once
stood for thousands of years.

Not all of those who fight the Empire are part of
the Rebellion itself. Not every resistance group or
military opposition unit is part of the Rebel Alliance.
Some stand apart through choice, others are too
remote or otherwise-isolated by culture, politics, or
Imperial barriers. As the Rebellion grows, it absorbs
many groups into its fold.

THE REBELLION

AGE OF REBELLION “383


### History of the Rebellion

HISTORY OF THE REBELLION

Fo much of the galaxy, an active, fighting Rebellion is
still a relatively recent development on the galactic
stage. Reports of skirmishes and battles were typically
few in number and seemingly restricted to the remote
worlds of the galaxy. Thanks in part to an effective Impe-
rial propaganda machine, early Rebel victories were
often covered up, minimized, or misreported altogether.
To most, the Rebellion was filled with foolish idealists
who had no hope of changing or defeating the Empire.

Then the Rebels destroyed the Empire’s mightiest
weapon, the Death Star.

The Rebellion instantly became feared or admired,
depending on one's view of the Empire. The show of
military might encourages dissidents and opposition
forces. Imperial loyalists were angered, startled, scared,
and more determined than ever to defeat those who
would bring war and chaos not seen in the galaxy since
the Clone Wars. While the Rebellion and the Death Star
simultaneously exploded into public consciousness
seemingly overnight, both were planned, developed,
and built throughout the preceding decades.

THE DARK TIMES

In truth, the Rebellion began in the midst of the Clone
Wars. As Supreme Chancellor Palpatine established the
Grand Army of the Republic and consolidated more

and more power, some Republic senators watched with
ever-growing concern. As they introduced measures or
promoted actions that would slow Palpatine’s methodi-
cal power grab, they were stymied by the Chancellor's
skillful use of the bureaucracy and manipulation of
public opinion.

Most of these senators signed the Petition of 2,000.
They called for the Chancellor to relinquish some of
his emergency powers and seek peace with the Sepa-
ratists. Palpatine declined. When he declared himself
Emperor and announced the creation of the Galactic
Empire, many withdrew their support of the document.
Nevertheless, all 2,000 were investigated by the newly
created Imperial intelligence agencies, including the
Imperial Security Bureau. Some were publicly cleared,
while others were arrested; some disappeared forever.

Of those who remained, three powerful Core World
Senators formed the nucleus of what would someday
become the central leadership of the Rebellion. Mon
Mothma of Chandrila, Bail Organa of Alderaan, and,
later, Garm Bel Iblis of Corellia met privately to strat-
egize and develop political and other opposition to the
Emperors schemes. Throughout the dark times, they
used their positions to counter the Imperial plans,
both politically in the Imperial Senate, and practically
through opposition groups and proxy battles.

SECRET MEETINGS

Mothma, Organa, and Iblis were not above Imperial
Suspicion. Organa publicly played the part of con-
cerned loyalist. Mothma used similar tactics, but also
used her homeworlds reputation for fierce political
debate to press the Imperial government. Garm Iblis,
too, benefited from Corellia’s standing as contentious
and independently minded, allowing him to be a sur-
prisingly harsh critic of Imperial rule. Cleverness in
word and deed, as well as political power and games-
manship, was enough to keep the three out of Imperial
detention centers.

Organa’s Cantham House residence became their
primary meeting place on Coruscant, as the world
transformed into Imperial Center. The Cantham House
meetings developed means and methods for public and
private resistance. Though they opposed the Emperor,
the three Senators had differing opinions about how to
defeat and depose him. Bail Organa, having personally
seen the vicious assault on the Jedi Temple at the end
of the Clone Wars, favored political solutions, even in
the face of increasing support for the Emperor among
the Senateand the citizenry. Alderaan publicly became
a pacifist society, and Organa both led the way and
used it politically. Mothma pressed for more direct
action, fearing the Empire would only solidify its posi-
tion over time. Garm Iblis's harsh manner and criticism
extended to both the Senate and the private meetings.

Organa’s loyalist facade landed him on important
Imperial Senate committees, granting him access
to intimate details the group used to effectively dis-
rupt Imperial plans and actions. He and the others
even played the parts of so-called rivals in the Senate
chamber. For years, stretching into decades, immedi-
ate overt action was difficult and very dangerous to
pursue. Slow and careful planning and building were
deemed necessary.

GROWING RESISTANCE

Outside of the halls of power, some brave and moti-
vated people of the galaxy were also plotting, planning,
and fighting Imperial rule on their own terms. Though
much of the Coreward regions were adamant Imperial
Supporters, this was not universal. Support diminished
greatly in some areas, especially in remote parts of
the Outer Rim. Outright secession, insurgencies, and
attempts at open rebellion were quickly crushed by the
Imperial military. Anti-Imperial leaders and supporters
were ruthlessly pursued, arrested, and imprisoned.
Some simply disappeared. Such worlds received harsh
punishment and were made examples of in order to
deter them and anyone else from opposing the Empire.
Though the Empire successfully cowed many citizens
ana worlds into submission, others tried harder by con-
cealing their efforts and picking their targets carefully.

THE JEDI ARE EXTINCT, THEIR FIRE
HAS GONE OUT OF THE UNIVERSE...

Imost. Nearly all of the Jedi were killed by
Darth Vader and Order 66. Of the few who
survived, most went into hiding to avoid suffer-
ing the same fate. During the dark times, Vader
continued the purge of any remaining Jedi he
discovered, seeking them out and destroying

them himself. Most Jedi went deep under-
ground, hiding themselves on the fringes of
the galaxy. Occasionally, one would resurface
to right a terrible wrong, or protect the inno-
cent, or even seek vengeance after losing all
discipline once instilled by the now-dead Jedi
Order. These lone Jedi occasionally helped
early dissidents, often at the cost of their own
lives as Vader mercilessly hunted them down.
By the time the Rebel Alliance engaged the
Death Star, Obi-Wan Kenobi and Luke Sky-
walker were the Alliance’s only known Force-
using allies. Like the Jedi before him, Kenobi
didn't survive his final encounter with Vader.

Armed resistance fighters first appeared very early
in Imperial history. However, their isolation and small
size made them easy for the Imperial military to sup-
press or destroy. Bail Organa secretly supported Alder-
aanian resistance forces, some of which were highly
successful at disrupting Imperial military weapon and
ship development. Though helpful in specific instances
or situations, such efforts failed to inspire the public
at large and ultimately failed to significantly influence
Imperial power, either politically or militarily. A larger,
more organized and better-supported group was
needed, and it took years to develop and assemble.

In some ways, time was on the resistance groups’
side. Once the Imperials had consolidated power and
control over much of the galaxy, the initial backlash
against Imperial rule was minimized by choice or by
force. However, as the Emperor put his plans into
action, and as the Imperial military, bureaucracy, and
security efforts took hold, conditions worsened sig-
nificantly for some areas and some species. The more
species that were enslaved, the more worlds that were
Subjugated or retaliated against, and the more abuses
of Imperial power became public, the more citizens
were likely to resist in extreme ways.

Bel Garm Iblis drew enough of the Emperor’s ire
that the he and his immediate family were targeted
by Imperial assassins. His wife and daughters were
killed, but Garm Iblis managed to escape into hiding.
As Corellia mourned his apparent death, he worked
behind the scenes to further the anti-lmperial cause
and build a militia.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

Bail Organa’s loyalist front paid off in another
way, when one of the Emperor's truly loyal Senators
informed Organa of Mon Mothma’s impending arrest.
Organa tipped her off, and she escaped. On the run
and unable to continue in any legally official capac-
ity, Mothma took the lead in personally organizing the
resistance forces by seeking out the disparate groups
and convincing them to work together.

THE CORELLIAN TREATY

Despite a loss of reputation due to increasing Imperial
scrutiny and surveillance, Organa continued as best he
could in the Imperial Senate. Out in the galaxy, three
large resistance forces became organized and active
enough to form the beginnings of a significant military
fighting force. Mon Mothma negotiated with them,
sharing the idea of a larger rebellion and how it could
be organized and accomplished using plans developed
in the Cantham House meetings.

Two years before the Battle of Yavin, a completely
unforeseen and unexpected player arrived on the scene.
A man powerful in the Force and calling himself Starkiller
saved Senator Organa while on the planet Felucia. Hav-
ing gained Bails trust, Starkiller fulfilled Princess Leia’s
request to disrupt the Empire’s Wookiee slave opera-
tions. The Emperor had ordered her to oversee con-
struction of the operation as retaliation for her contin-
ued dissent. Starkiller tried to convince the Organas to
join him in overthrowing the Emperor. Having proved
his power with the Force, the trio of main conspirators
became convinced that he might be able to do just that.

After a very risky Cantham House meeting, Bail
Organa, Mon Mothma, and Garm Bel Iblis decided
the time had come to formally create the “Alliance to
Restore the Republic.” The Corellian Treaty formalized
the relationship between the resistance groups and
established the Rebellion. Meeting personally on Corel-
lia, the three original conspirators were joined by Prin-
cess Leia via holographic link, as well as Starkiller and
his pilot, Juno Eclipse. What should have been a joyous
occasion suddenly turned terrifying when Darth Vader
and Imperial forces arrived to arrest them. Bail Organa
managed to cut the link to preserve Princess Leia’s
secret involvement, but Vader captured him and the
heart of the would-be Rebel leadership. Vader exposed
Starkiller as his so-called apprentice and then revealed
he and the Emperor had manipulated Starkiller’s entire
existence for their own purposes. Since he was no lon-
ger useful to them, Vader tried to kill Starkiller.

Vader failed. Starkiller survived and pursued Vader
to the unfinished Death Star, where the Emperor and
Vader intended to personally oversee the conspirators’
execution. Starkiller proved his worth and his ideals by
freeing all three of them. This time, he didn’t survive.

The conspirators escaped and traced Starkiller’s
story back to Kashyyyk. Using their long-discussed

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

a

THE DECLARATION OF REBELLION

he Declaration of Rebellion includes the

following major tenets which help form the
ideals of the Rebel Alliance, and should also
guide GMs and players as to the manner in
which the Rebellion aspires to operate:

e It acknowledges the necessity of
galactic government, but one sup-
ported by the people it represents.

e The Empire’s subjugation of species,

abuse of its citizens, and its many

atrocities against its own people
gives the citizens the right to remove
it from power.

It summarizes the Empire’s many
abuses of power and people, includ-
ing the overthrow of planetary gov-
ernments, wrongful imprisonment,
improper executions, genocide, insti-
tutionalized slavery, and repression of
those who oppose the Emperor.

It declares the Rebel Alliance will bat-
tle the Emperor and his forces, refuse
any Imperial law against the rights of
free beings, fight for the destruction
of the Emperor and the Empire itself,
and free all beings in the galaxy.

plans from Cantham House and beyond, the Corellian
Treaty became a reality, and the Alliance to Restore the
Republic became a new force in the galaxy.

DECLARATION OF
REBELLION

Following the signing of the Treaty, the conspirators
returned to their separate duties. Bel Iblis returned to
his growing militia forces at Corellia. Bail Organa could
no longer effectively serve as an Imperial Senator. He
resigned, and his daughter Leia Organa took his place.
Bail returned to his position as Viceroy and First Chair-
man of the Alderaan system. Both Organas remained
suspect in Imperial eyes and under Imperial surveillance,
but they were temporarily protected by their power-
ful political positions. Mon Mothma became the Com-
mander in Chief for the Rebel Alliance, and its public face.

In order to legitimize the Rebellion as a true political
and fighting force for liberty and rejection of Imperial
ideas and laws, Mon Mothma personally wrote the Dec-
laration of Rebellion. Though addressed to the Emperor,
it was released to the public to clearly announce the
Rebels’ intentions and to minimize the effectiveness of
the never-ending Imperial propaganda campaigns.

The Declaration of Rebellion initially worked too well.
Several star systems immediately declared their sup-
port and seceded from the Empire. Mon Mothma tried
to warn and deter anyone from such immediate and
rash action, as no Rebel or resistance forces were yet
up to the task of opposing a significant Imperial mili-
tary force. As predicted, memories of similar Separatist
actions before and during the Clone Wars spurred on
an immediate Imperial military reaction. The Secession
Worlds were quickly retaken and received harsh politi-
cal and economic punishment for their defiance.

However, in some cases, it took the Imperials days
or weeks to retake the systems. This gave the seces-
sionists enough time to transfer cash, materials, man-
power, equipment, and starships directly to the Rebel
Alliance. Though those worlds paid a heavy price for
their resistance, their support and materiel bolstered
the Rebel cause immeasurably.

A SLOW START

Following the Declaration of Rebellion and the pre-
dictable-but-unfortunate fate of the Secession Worlds,
the Rebel Alliance enjoyed an initial surge of people
and materiel into the existing military units. Beyond
those transferred from the Secession Worlds, many of
these people were already active dissidents or simply
individuals who had tolerated Imperial rule for long
enough. In essence, it released a pent-up demand for
active rebellion and resistance.

Existing resistance groups eyed the situation care-
fully. Some were initially fearful of an Imperial trap.
Others needed to be convinced that the new Rebel

om z

Alliance could deliver on its promises, and not compro-
mise their own positions if they joined. A few groups
disagreed on the specific tactics used, or had differing
political views due to their culture. Mon Mothma con-
tinued to negotiate with suitable groups to bring them
into the fold.

For roughly two years after the Declaration of Rebel-
lion, both individual and group recruitment was slow but
steady. Growing public support after the initial surge
proved to be a long, slow process. The Rebellion faced
a formidable Imperial propaganda machine able to twist
Rebel victories into apparent criminal actions that were
a detriment to the peace, order, and stability estab-
lished in the years following the devastating Clone Wars.

Politically, those in the Imperial Senate who opposed
the Empire began to slowly support and suggest posi-
tions that incrementally aided or reinforced the Rebel
cause. Arrest and detention of Rebels and Rebel sympa-
thizers soon made it crystal clear that anyone support-
ing the Rebel Alliance was considered a traitor and an
enemy of the state. Even to be accused of associating
with the Rebels could seriously damage the reputation
of anyone at any level of society or government position.

While such conditions made it difficult to recruit, the
Rebels continued to bring in new members from every
corner of the galaxy and level of society. Entire star-
ships disappeared and defected to Rebel forces. Mili-
tary officers, soldiers, and naval crewmen individually
sought out Rebel forces, or were successfully recruited
when a Rebel agent deduced or discovered their hatred
of the Empire. Imperial propaganda sometimes failed
to discredit Rebel victories or cover Imperial impropri-
eties, atrocities, and abuses of power.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

EARLY LESSONS

Initially, the Rebel Alliance tried building strong Sec-
tor Force armies to conquer key planets and hold
them using planetary shields and formidable defenses.
Called Operation Domino, this plan was short-lived.
The few attempted revolutions failed to take control
of their planets, or Alliance ground forces failed to
hold them. In short, the Alliance army was not large or
strong enough to hold territory. Worse, every Alliance
Sector Force had very different assets at its disposal.
It was not a cohesive, well-rounded, or well-equipped
military operation.

Militarily, the Rebels were on the run or forced under-
ground. Even keeping their main base hidden and in
operation was problematic, as the Imperials scoured
the galaxy for any Rebel activity. (See Operation Strike
Fear and Rebel Battles on page 403 for more infor-
mation.) Far from giving up, the Rebels adapted, learned
new tactics, and implemented new strategies.

FORMER SEPARATISTS:
ENEMIES OR ALLIES?

ven decades after their defeat in the Clone

Wars, ex-Separatist factions still exist in the
galaxy. While these would seem to be ready-
made enemies of the Empire and allies of the
Rebellion, things are not that simple. Some Reb-
els see a distinction between rebelling against
the Empire and turning against the Republic,
and still consider the Separatists as enemies.
Some Separatists were war criminals by any
Standard. For Mon Mothma, Garm Bel Iblis, and
Bail Organa, the issue was not clear-cut and was
the source of much discussion and argument.

Even among the former Separatists, the issues
are muddled. The goal of the Rebellion is to
reestablish the Republic in some form, a gov-
ernment the Separatists wanted to escape or
eliminate. As much as they hate the Empire,
many seek a different path than a return to
what they regard as a corrupt and ineffectual
system. Some ex-Separatists—mainly those
with heavy corporate involvement—have since
benefited significantly, becoming rich under
Imperial rule. In the end, some ex-Separatists
side with the Rebels, some fight the Empire
on their own terms, some remain allied with
their benefactors, and some remain neutral.
The political situation remains as complex and
intertwined with local politics as it did during
the Clone Wars themselves.

POLITICAL CHANGES

When the Emperor disbanded the Imperial Senate
just prior to the destruction of Alderaan, it silenced
a source of growing Rebel sympathy from certain
Senators. With the Senate out of the way, the gal-
axy'’s most-watched forum of political discussion and
discourse—however hollow it might have often been
under the Emperor—was silenced. Imperial Governors
assumed direct control of their holdings and took their
orders from the Emperor, not their citizens. The Empire
strengthened its hold on its systems, but the move also
convinced even more people to sympathize or join with
the Rebellion.

Some local and planetary governments chafed at
the idea of being under the control of an Imperial Gov-
ernor. Humans were often appointed to oversee non-
human worlds. Sometimes, the Imperial Governor kept
the local traditional government running, as long as it
did not interfere in his authority. Worsening political
and societal positions opened the way for more politi-
cal dissent and ultimately, Rebel recruiting.

THE BATTLE OF YAVIN

The Death Star was the Empire's ultimate weapon of
terror and mass destruction, designed to intimidate
worlds into obedience in the face of total annihilation.
The destruction of Alderaan did exactly that, and the
shockwaves rippled through galactic society. The willful
destruction of an ancient world important to the galaxy
for SO many centuries was difficult to stomach for even
the most loyal Imperial citizen. While some worlds were
Surely cowed into compliance, for others, the veil was
finally lifted. Nothing would stop the Emperor, or the
Empire, from dominating any system anywhere.

Fortunately for the galaxy, a newcomer to the Rebel
Alliance named Luke Skywalker destroyed the station
a short time later, ending its reign of terror before it
could do more damage. The Battle of Yavin became
the first major Rebel military victory on a galactic scale.
While the Alliance had managed to achieve some suc-
cess against elements of the Imperial fleets, this was
a landmark victory that could not be easily denied.
See page 404 for more on the Battle of Yavin and its
immediate aftermath.

The Rebels enjoyed a new influx of recruits and allied
groups in the time that followed. Even planetary gov-
ernments and star systems gave new support, in secret
if not in public. A few, like Mon Calamari, were strong
enough to take a more public stand.

The aid was welcomed and needed. Rebel forces
were eventually chased from the Yavin 4 base and
sought a permanent base elsewhere in the galaxy. In
other locations, smaller Rebel facilities and forces con-
tinue to grow.


### Rebel Alliance Organization

CURRENT STATUS

he Rebels currently do not have a primary

secret base. They abandoned Yavin 4 imme-
diately after the destruction of the Death
Star. High Command operates from the Rebel
Fleet and the occasional temporary base, as
needed. The search is on for a suitable world
for a new Rebel base, a search in which the
PCs could take part. Even if the location the
PCs find does not the needs of Alliance High
Command, it might always be turned into a

smaller secret facility or starfighter base.

INSPIRED ACTIONS

The Battle of Yavin became a major turning point for
the Rebels. Resistance fighters across the galaxy were
emboldened by the Rebel victory. New resistance
groups emerged, some on the staunchest of Imperial
worlds. Across the galaxy, beings found new hope and
the courage to stand up to Imperial rule. Against this
surge of Rebel support came increasingly harsh Impe-
rial reprisals and security responses, which only further
incited the citizenry and pushed them ever closer to
the Alliance. The galaxy was fighting back.

REBEL ALLIANCE ORGANIZATION

hough nowhere near the size or scale of the Imperial

government, the Rebel Alliance still commands thou-
sands of individuals spread across an entire galaxy. It
must wage a widespread political battle while simultane-
ously planning and coordinating military strikes against
a much larger and better-equipped enemy. It must logis-
tically Support all civil and military operations in virtual
secrecy. Even minor security breaches can be devastat-
ing to a significant percentage of the Alliance's available
resources. To handle these tasks, the Rebel Alliance is
divided into the Alliance Civil Government and the Alli-
ance Military. Mon Mothma heads both divisions as
Chief of State and Commander-in-Chief,

As organized as the command structure appears
here, the Rebellion is truly an alliance of separate
groups. Though they tend to act more and more in
concert with each other and begin to develop proce-
dures and tactics to match, they can still be wildly dif-
ferent in makeup, culture, and ambition. Appearing as
a cohesive unit is useful for morale, public support, and
easier recognition between unfamiliar Allied groups,
but there are natural variations between appearance,
equipment, and tactics.

ALLIANCE CIVIL
GOVERNMENT

The Alliance Civil Government mirrors the functions of
a typical large-scale civilian government. It provides
direction and support for all functions of the Alliance. It
collects taxes from member organizations and worlds,
and handles internal and external diplomatic relations.
It primarily exists to fund and support the Alliance Mili-
tary, but many of the Civil Government’s functions are
actually fulfilled by the local Allied Commands. As the
political wing of the Rebellion, the Civil Government
represents the Alliance as a whole in public and dip-
lomatic matters.

Chief of State: The Chief of State is the Alliance’s
primary diplomat, negotiator, and leader. Though
elections are held every two years by member govern-
ments, the Chief of State otherwise has complete dic-
tatorial power over the Alliance. Imperial propaganda
often calls attention to this, and even Bel Iblis feared
Mon Mothma’s ultimate ambition. However, in order
to prevent a Rebel dictatorship, this position auto-
matically terminates with the fall of the Emperor. Mon
Mothma is the only person to have held the position.

The Advisory Council: The Advisory Council's author-
ity is limited to approval or disapproval of the Chief of
State's actions and activities, but includes the power to
remove the Chief of State. It counterbalances the Chief
of State’s broad powers and advises on any subject
in need of discussion and contemplation. The Council
members come from the seven worlds that have given
the greatest number of lives to the Rebellion, which
changes as the war progresses. Though Princess Leia
should have a seat representing Alderaan, she elects
to serve in other roles.

The Cabinet: The Cabinet carries out the Chief of
State's orders and decisions. It is comprised of six
ministries, each with its own minister. Meetings are
conducted by the Secretary of the Cabinet, who also
Stands in when the Chief of State is absent.

Education Ministry: This ministry is responsible for
Rebel propaganda and public information campaigns,
as well as comprising the Alliance Intelligence group for
the Civil Government. The ministry coordinates heavily
with Military Intelligence and monitors the Civil Gov-
ernment for Imperial infiltration and leaks.

Finance Ministry: This ministry is responsible for
financing the Rebel Alliance. It collects taxes and other
funds from member and sympathetic governments
(covertly). They also issue the Alliance Credit, which
has, at best, twenty-five percent of the value of the
typical credit outside of the Alliance.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

Ministry of Industry: This ministry controls all of the
Alliance's heavy industry and manufacturing. Facilities
on Rebel-controlled worlds, like the shipyards of Mon
Calamari, are few in number. The Alliance also benefits
from secret deals with sympathetic corporations and
worlds that clandestinely ship goods into the Rebels’
secret supply network.

Ministry of State: This ministry negotiates with non-
member governments and oversees the Diplomatic
Corps. The Minister of State takes over the Civil Gov-
ernment if the Chief of State is ever removed or killed.

Ministry of Supply: This ministry is responsible for
feeding and supplying the entire Alliance military and
civilian efforts. Supplies are limited by Alliance funds
and the fact that most heavy military gear and equip-
ment isn't available to the Alliance through the open
market. Food primarily comes from Alliance safe
worlds, which saves on cost, but is difficult to transport
due to chronic transport starship shortages.

Ministry of War: This ministry oversees the Alliance
military forces, translating the Chief of State’s policy
and specific orders into practical commands. Opera-
tional decisions are left primarily to the Chiefs of Staff.
This ministry also coordinates the war effort with the
rest of the Civil Government.

ALLIANCE MILITARY

The Alliance Military is charged with defeating Imperial
military forces and toppling the Emperor. It is chroni-
cally outgunned and outmanned by enemy forces, and
further hindered by training limitations and equipment
issues. The Alliance Military is split into Alliance Forces
and Sector Forces. Alliance Forces are not permanently
attached to any particular sector, but are sent where
needed. Sector Forces serve specific sectors (see below).

Alliance High Command: High Command comprises
the Supreme Allied Commanders of each individual
command. They oversee and coordinate all fleet and
army operations, disseminating the orders of the Com-
mander-in-Chief and Minister of War. High Command
operates aboard the Rebel Fleet or from the main rebel
base as required. They oversee both Alliance and Sec-
tor Forces.

Fleet Command: Fleet Command oversees the orders
and operation of the Alliance starship fleet, which is
the single most vital military asset in the Rebellion.

Intelligence: Intelligence is responsible for gather-
Ing vital military and civilian intelligence data for use
by all Rebel organizations. It reports directly to the
Commander-in-Chief. It is among the largest of the
commands, with agents spread throughout the galaxy
operating individually or in teams. See page 394 for
more on Alliance Intelligence.

Ordnance and Supply: This section acquires ships,
equipment, and supplies for the military, coordinating

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

me

with the Supply Ministry as needed. Support Services
carries out the actual transportation of goods.

Sector Command: Sector Command loosely oversees
and, when necessary, coordinates disparate Sector
Forces in larger operations. They also coordinate the Sec-
tor Forces with Alliance High Command. Sector Command
does not take command of individual Sector Forces.

Special Forces Command: The approximately ten divi-
sions of Special Forces units protect and work directly
with Alliance High Command. They are occasionally
loaned to Sector Commands for specific missions.

Starfighter Command: Starfighter Command_ is
in charge of the large and vital starfighter fleet. It is
responsible for pilot training, and assigns wings to
Alliance High Command and the Sector Commands.
Starfighters are a scarce resource and their reassign-
ment is often the source of friction between the various
commands. It also provides orders for roving squad-
rons without permanent assignments, such as Rogue
Squadron.

Support Services: Support Services covers the logisti-
cal support of ships, bases, safe worlds, and personnel
In general. This includes support staff of all types, from
droids to doctors.

ALLIED COMMANDS

When an existing planetary or regional government
or similar large-scale organization joins the Rebel Alli-
ance, it becomes an Allied Command. Sectors with
significant Rebel military units but no major Rebel
governmental allies or sympathizers have Sector Force
commands instead. Both command types hold exten-
sive semi-autonomous control over their own forces
and regions. In sectors with multiple Allied commands
(for political or other reasons), they may coordinate
through the local Sector Force. Existing Allied govern-
ments and military forces typically keep their own
command structure, though they may adopt or adapt
to the Alliance’s typical military and political structure.
This might mirror the positions of the Civil Government.

THE ALLIANCE FLEET

The Alliance Fleet is the Rebels’ most important mili-
tary force. The fleet gives the Rebels the mobility and
firepower it needs to legitimately challenge Imperial
military forces. However, as the fleet is only a small
fraction of the size of the Imperial Navy, it must pick
and choose its battles very carefully.
