The Admiral of the Fleet is the Supreme Commander
of the Alliance Fleet. Under the Admiral are the line
admirals, Fleet Intelligence, Ordnance and Supply, and
Starfighter Command. Starfighter squadrons, Special
Operations, and SpecForce units attached to the Fleet
typically report to their individual ship captains or
admirals for the duration of their assignment, rather

than their individual commands. Individual department
heads oversee various functions such as medical and
engineering support. They report to their ship captains.

Mon Mothma heavily restricts the use of the Fleet.
With the Rebel Main Base chased off Yavin 4, the loss
of the fleet would be a severe (if not fatal) blow to
Rebel firepower and credibility. Clashes with the Impe-
rial Navy prove it seeks a decisive victory. The fleet
remains a fighting force by detaching up to twenty-five
percent of its ships on a rotating basis for specific raids
and other missions.

Ships of the Fleet come from numerous sources,
Including defections, Allied Commands, occasional
purchases, outright theft of Imperial military assets,
and Allied manufacturing facilities like the Mon Cala-
mari shipyards. Due to this, there are no standardized
Rebel capital ships or similar classes of vessels. Rebel
engineers are adept at upgrading or adapting star-
Ships for military use. Starfighter Command handles
the starfighter squadrons.

Alliance task forces typically consist of frigates,
corvettes, and gunships. A cruiser or two may be

~)

\

deployed for extra firepower. With warships at a pre-
mium, heavily-armed and upgraded freighters serve
many roles in the fleet that would normally be handled
by military-grade craft. Some freighters, container
ships, and bulk cruisers are much better suited to this
task than others.

ALLIANCE FLEET ORGANIZATION

Subdivisions and fleet detachments are handled on an
ad-hoc basis as required for the mission at hand. At
any given time, task forces and detachments may be
under the direct orders of Alliance High Command, an
Allied Command, or a Sector Force.

Element: A single starship under the command of a
ship's captain.

Section: A small number of starships, their exact type
determined by mission requirements. Typically, a sec-
tion includes corvettes, frigates, or other light capital
Ships. Larger vessels are rarely included, however.
When a combination of capital ships and close support
vessels is used, the section may be called a flotilla. The
section typically includes three to twelve vessels under
the command of the most senior ship's captain.

»_ Squadron: A squadron includes three or four sec-

tions commanded by a senior captain or line
admiral. Some Allied Commands use the
rank of commodore. The squadron is
the most common Fleet detachment,
and is often a line of capital ships
escorted by smaller vessels and
using armored freighters as a
Meee picket line.

. =e Battle Group: Battle groups

Seem, Qeinciude two to four squad-
| rons under the command
BOf a line admiral. Only the
. largest Allied Commands
deploy battle groups, as
fleet detachments are
@ rarely this large.

=

yFleet: A fleet includes
three to six battle groups.
The main Alliance Fleet is
» under the direct command
bof the Admiral of the Fleet.
Me Others are led by fleet
™ admirals. Fleet operations
are always major battles
Wrequiring approval of Alli-
ance High Command.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

SECTOR FORCES

Sector Forces comprise the primary military units of
the Rebel Alliance. Each Sector Force contains every
military and support unit that is available, includ-
ing ground troops, special units, intelligence forces,
Starfighters, and fleets of starships. They also control
any assets assigned to them by Alliance High Com-
mand. Sector Forces contain the bulk of the Alliance
ground forces as regular and irregular troops.

Individual resistance groups that join the Rebel Alli-
ance either become the core of a new Sector Force, or
join an already existing Sector Force. Although the Sec-
tor Force is a military classification, it typically fights
using guerrilla warfare and other resistance tactics. Few
Sector Forces have the personnel or machinery to take
on the Empire head-on for long.

Sector Forces use their own organizational struc-
tures, or integrate or adopt a version of the Rebel
Alliance structure. They handle all operational duties
within their sector, coordinating as needed with Alli-
ance High Command. Sector Forces vary widely in
personnel, facilities, and abilities. Some sectors have
a full range of military assets and units, while others
struggle along with what personnel, equipment, and
intelligence they have at hand.

SECTOR FORCE AND TYPICAL
ALLIANCE ARMY ORGANIZATION

Ground force units typically use a version of the orga-
nization, Outlined below. Special Forces, Starfighter
Command, and Alliance Navy units use their respective
structures, even as part of a Sector Force.

Squad: A squad typically includes eight troopers and
a sergeant.

Platoon: The platoon includes four squads com-
manded by a lieutenant and master sergeant.

Company: A company includes four platoons plus
their support personnel under a captain.

Battalion: Battalions include four companies and their
support personnel, commanded by a major.

Regiment: The typical regiment includes four battal-
ions and their support personnel, commanded by a
regimental commander, typically a colonel.

Brigade: Brigades, sometimes called Legions, include
four regiments and their support personnel under a
colonel or general.

Corps: Generals command corps of four brigades plus
their Support personnel.

Sector Army: No sector is able to field a true Sec-
tor Army at this stage of Alliance development. Sector
Armies consist of several Corps under the command of
a general or other officer who might be the local Force
Commander or Allied Command leader.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

=

SPECIAL FORCES

Alliance Special Forces, commonly called SpecForce
units, are the most highly trained and competent sol-
diers in the Rebel Alliance. Though few in number, they
take On many important tasks and missions, includ-
ing major commando raids, protecting Alliance High
Command, protecting the Main Base, training Sector
Forces, and other special missions. SpecForce units are
typically under Alliance High Command, but may be
assigned to specific starships, Sector Forces, or Allied
Commands as needed. Emergency deployments to
critical battlefields are common.

SPECFORCE REGIMENTS

Each SpecForce soldier is assigned to a regiment associ-
ated with their specific role in combat or the combat
environment. Task forces are created with personnel
from one or more regiments as dictated by the mission,
and often include individuals with a wide range of skills.

Ist Regiment, Marines (SpaceOps): Marines, or
SpaceOps, are trained in zero-g combat, offensive and
defensive boarding actions, shipboard fighting, envi-
ronmental suits, ships’ systems, and ship security. They
serve aboard the Fleet.

2nd Regiment, Pathfinders: Pathfinders are masters
of covert operations and are often the first SpecForce
units deployed to a battlefield. They are scouts, provide
reconnaissance in force, and establish beachheads.

3rd Regiment, Urban Guerrillas: The Urban Guer-
rillas specialize in urban combat and street fighting.
They typically do not fight pitched battles or use heavy
weaponry, but instead use stealth, extreme mobility,
demolitions, light weapons, and hand-to-hand fighting.

4th Regiment, Wilderness Fighters: Wilderness
Fighters are trained for combat in specific wilderness
types, but are all well-trained in survival methods. They
also use or commandeer light vehicles , such as speed-
ers or speeder bikes, as needed in the field.

5th Regiment, Infiltrators: The Infiltrators are the
most feared and ruthless soldiers, trained in stealth,
infiltration, and enemy disruption methods. Most are
recruited because of their personal reasons for hating
the Empire.

6th Regiment, Heavy Weapons Specialists: The
Heavy Weapons Specialists train on every piece of
heavy weaponry available to the Rebel Alliance, includ-
ing starship turbolasers, vehicle cannons and missiles,
artillery, and repeating blasters. They also sustain the
highest casualty rates.

7th Regiment, Technicians: Technicians typically oper-
ate in support of other units, and all other SpecForce
specialists are included in this regiment. This includes
drivers, pilots, procurement specialists, and mechanics,
as well as computer and droid technicians.

REBEL ALLIANCE MILITARY RANKS

ach of the four major divisions of the Alliance

Military has its own rank structure, detailed
in the below table. Allied Commands may retain
their own traditional structures.

Army

General

Navy

Admiral

Captain

7 | Colonel

SpecForce
General

Colonel

The system is flexible to make assimilation
of new forces easier. Local commanders are
allowed to make adjustments for traditional or
political purposes.

Starfighter Command

General

Commander

| Major

Colonel (Wing Commander)

| Commander

Lieutenant Captain

Lieutenant Lieutenant

Ensign

) Lieutenant

Senior Lieutenant

Senior (1st) Trooper

Deckman | Trooper

SPECFORCE ORGANIZATION

SpecForce units use a rank structure similar to those
of other to normal Alliance units, but their tactical unit
Structure differs somewhat.

Fire Team: The basic unit is the fire team, consisting
of a team of two to four troopers, one of whom is a
senior trooper.

Squad: Squads of five to fifteen troopers typically
focused on a specific SpecForce soldier type, some-
times reinforced by fire teams of other types. Squads
are typically referenced in combat by a color of their
own selection, such as Red Squad.

Platoon: Platoons are designated by number and con-
sist of four squads under a lieutenant or senior lieu-
tenant. They can operate independently, or as part of
larger commands.

Company: Companies are designated by letter and
typically include five platoons plus support personnel
Each platoon is typically a different SpecForce type.
Companies are commanded by a captain or major.

Regiment: Regiments are not normally fielded as a unit,
but operate as an organizational unit within the Alliance
military structure. They are divided into individual com-
bat roles (see above). On the rare occasions they are
deployed, it is under the command of a colonel or major.

Division: Generals command the ten SpecForce divi-
sions and their support personnel. The Command
Division stays with Alliance High Command, covering
the fleet and main base. Five divisions are typically
deployed under their own general to Sector Commands
as needed. The rest are assigned to smaller operations
as needed throughout the galaxy, either under the
orders of Alliance High Command or a local commander.

Trooper

STARFIGHTER COMMAND

Starfighters are arguably the Alliance’s most useful and
sought-after resource. They are deployed throughout
the galaxy, serving as the primary strike force and pro-
tector of Rebel operations. Internal political wrangling
between the various commands is common when it
comes to starfighter deployment and assignment. As
fleet operations are limited, starfighter squadrons are
often called in to fill the gap and carry out missions of
much greater importance.

Starfighter Command, or StarCom, handles pilot
training, the assignment of starfighter wings, and
operational control over specialty squadrons, such
as Rogue Squadron. Starfighter wings are typically
assigned to specific Allied Commands, Sector Forces,
or starships. Wings are often broken up into their indi-
vidual squadrons in order to spread as many fighters
out to as many bases, starships, and other assignments
as possible. Once assigned, the local leaders typically
take command, though Starfighter Command reviews
the unit's use and results. Large operations may use
starfighters from multiple sources.

STARFIGHTER WINGS

Starfighter wings are organized as follows. Units of
Squadron size or smaller typically contain the same
Starfighter type and model.

Element: [he element includes a leader and a wingman.
Flight: A flight is two elements under the most senior pilot.

Squadron: A squadron includes three flights under a
squadron commander. Squadrons receive a color or
name designation and can be referred to as a group,
such as Red Squadron.

THE REBELLION
ACE OF REBELLION

Wing: A wing includes three squadrons and their sup-
port personnel. They are under the command of a wing
commander, which Is a colonel or sometimes an officer
of lesser rank. The three squadrons may be of different
Starfighter types, and one is typically a recon unit.

Group: A group includes two or more wings under
a wing commander or general. Groups are rarely
deployed by the Alliance.

ALLIANCE INTELLIGENCE

In a civil war filled with covert operations, secret alli-
ances, hidden bases, and military defections, Alliance
Intelligence is enormously important to the day-to-
day operations of the Rebel Alliance. It monitors all
aspects of the Empire to discover weaknesses and
gauge future intentions toward the population and
the Alliance. It provides raw data and analysis for both
the civil government and the Alliance Military. Alliance
Intelligence also provides counterintelligence and
offensive capabilities against Imperial political, intel-
ligence, and military agencies.

The Chief of Intelligence oversees Alliance Intelli-
gence's three main divisions: Operations, Intentions,
and Counterintelligence. Operations is the largest sec-
tion, as its field operatives gather data through missions
of infiltration, sabotage, blackmail, subversion, spying,
Slicing, and more. They run intelligence networks on
planets and stations, and through myriad sources.
Intentions takes that data, analyzes it, and reports it
to the Alliance civil and military chains of command
who act on it. They also handle interrogations of cap-
tured personnel, try to crack Imperial codes, and cre-
ate amazing spy gadgets to aid in specific missions.
Counterintelligence focuses entirely on Imperial infil-
tration efforts throughout the Alliance. They have their
own secret agents embedded in Rebel units and in
departments at all levels of command. They monitor,
question, arrest, and otherwise deal with those they
suspect of being Imperial spies.

Al also runs background checks and other opera-
tions to confirm the sincerity of a potential defector,
whether it be an individual, starship crew, or com-
plete political unit, such as a planetary government.
Even when the defection is real, the defector may be
unaware of Imperial surveillance efforts on their person
or organization. It is up to Alliance Intelligence to root
Out any infiltration efforts associated with defectors
and Rebel recruits.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

SUPPORT SERVICES

Support Services is responsible for transporting Alli-
ance supplies to all operational units and starships:
providing medical technicians, mechanics, and other
Support personnel to all commands; and running and
maintaining Rebel safe worlds. As there is a chronic
transport shortage, Support Services is always on the
lookout for freighters and bulk transports to recruit
into service. Though hardly the most popular division
in the Rebel Alliance, it is vital and has among the riski-
est assignments in the day-to-day operations of the
Rebellion. Rebel transports must brave shady star-
ports, deal with criminal partners, and dodge Imperial
customs inspections and patrols in order to success-
fully complete their missions.

Alliance medical needs continue to grow along
with the Rebellion. Increasing force size and combat
operations require corresponding recruitment and
acquisition of medical supplies, doctors, technicians,
and medical droids. Support Services provides these
efforts, and also runs and maintains Rebel hospital
ships, such as the medical frigates.

Rebel safe worlds provide food and a much-needed
refuge from Imperial agents or other pursuers. Sup-
port Services maintains and protects these worlds. See
page 569 for more on safe worlds.

REBEL AGENTS AND
OPERATIVES

“Rebel agent” is a generic term used by Imperials and
Rebels alike to describe virtually any covert Rebel
personnel. Rebel agents , =o
might be recruiters, geal

saboteurs, spies, infiltra- & x
tors, intelligence agents, it
procurement special-
ists, scavengers, or even
the occasional assassin.
Agents might belong to
virtually any part of
the Alliance com-
mand structure.

USING THE ALLIANCE COMMAND STRUCTURE

D etermining where a PC or group of PCs fits
into the Rebel Alliance command structure
adds layers of roleplaying and storytelling oppor-
tunities to the game. While the PCs might generi-
cally be called Rebel agents, deciding where they
fit automatically builds in opportunities for tan-
gible rewards through possible promotion and
advancement. When the PCs join the Alliance,
whether that is in character creation or as part of
an adventure or campaign, the GM should deter-
mine where they fit into the command structure.
In part, this is determined by the actual adven-
tures the GM expects to run. An adventure
where the PCs are all starfighter pilots is vastly
different than if they are all secret intelligence
agents or so-called procurement specialists.

e lf the GM has a Rebel adventure or
campaign in mind, he should use that
as a Starting point to determine in
which Command the PCs should reside.

If the campaign is more freeform, the
PCs should discuss what part of the
Rebellion they wish to participate in,
and let that guide the GM to a particu-
lar branch of service.

Not all of the PCs need to be part of

the same Command, as the Rebels form

specialized task forces or groups when-

ever needed. However, certain combt-

nations may require more thought and
explanation than others.

The only commonality is that they are operating
on their own or in small groups in the field. Captured
agents can expect harsh treatment from the Imperials,
though the exact form of detention, treatment, and
Incarceration varies by which Imperial organization
captures them.

Rebel sympathizers who are not formally part of the
Alliance Military can be considered agents, and usually
are considered so by the Imperials. Members of Rebel
cell networks, which comprise three- or four-person
groups with secret and normally blind connections to
their handlers or superiors, are also considered agents.
These brave souls expand Rebel influence, intelligence
gathering, and power on Empire-dominated worlds
with minimal Rebel assistance.

e Next, the GM should determine what
each PC’s military rank is. Most Rebel
PCs fall somewhere in the Alliance Mili-
tary side of the organization. Most of
the PCs should be of similar rank. Since
lower-ranked individuals are expected
to follow the orders of those of higher
rank, this can occasionally create issues
within the gaming group if players do
not handle the concept of being ordered
around by another player very well. The
GM may be able to consider this and
discuss it before the game. Inevitably,
the PCs will be subordinate to a higher-
ranked NPC, and this may be enough to
resolve the situation.

As the PCs succeed or fail in their mis-
sions, their Rebel comrades should
respond in kind. The PC might eventu-
ally rise in rank or receive commenda-
tions for successful operations, though
such promotions should be saved for
very special occasions, or take a long
time to achieve. They might be demoted
or even kicked out of the Rebellion for
poor judgment or scandalous or despi-
cable actions.

The PCs should receive orders, informa-
tion, equipment, or other benefits from
their Command. They also receive regu-
lar pay, typically in Alliance Credits.

Special Operations personnel are agents who don’t
easily fit into the normal Alliance Command structure.
They are autonomous agents, and are given freedom
by their immediate superiors to choose the means
by which they achieve unusual mission goals. The
Alliance attempts to harness the free-spirited nature
and improvisational skills of these agents, rather than
try to lock them into a rigid military command. While
these agents are more likely to draw unwanted atten-
tion to themselves or the Alliance, they're also just as
likely to produce spectacular results with limited sup-
port. However, they do not have completely free reign,
and agents who go too far receive severe punishment,
especially if they make the Alliance look bad in the
eyes of the public.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION


### Rebel Bases

REBEL BASES

he Rebel Alliance uses a network of secret bases to

escape Imperial scrutiny while they organize, train,
strategize, and recover... These installations are essen-
tial to the success of the Rebellion, and their loss is
always a blow to Rebel efforts and security. The Rebel-
lion plays a constant game of hide-and-seek with the
Imperial military and intelligence services when it
comes to base locations.

Rebel bases come in a vast array of forms. Their
services and layouts are designed around the needs
of their local Rebel operation and the physical con-
straints of the surrounding environment. Bases range
from secret compartments or rooms to sprawling struc-
tures complete with defensive emplacements, shields,
starfighters, and ground troops ready to respond to any
attack or external threat. Most bases are built and con-
trolled by the local Allied Command or Sector Force.

COMMON BASE TYPES

Rebel bases typically support one or more of the fol-
lowing functions and personnel. Each type listed below
increases in size and complexity, and often includes
the services of the preceding, smaller bases.

Recon Field Base: Reconnaissance field bases are
used by individual Rebel agents and SpecForce Path-
finders for extended surveillance missions against spe-
cific installations. The base may consist of temporary
Structures, such as tents, or make use of natural ter-
rain, such as caves, massive trees, sinkholes, or other
landforms that provide shelter and concealment. They
may also serve as rally points and defensive positions
Should the unit be detected.

Cache: Caches are hidden supply storage locations.
They may be as simple as a hidden compartment in
a starship or structure. Some are buried in concealed
locations, such as urban parks or any stretch of wilder-
ness. Larger caches might be small structures, contain-
ing enough weapons, food, medicine, and other sup-
plies for a tactical team. The largest may even hold a
vehicle or small starship. Caches often serve as drop-
off points between agents or other Rebel groups.

Basic Meeting Location: Rebel agents and cell net-
work members often need a simple secret meeting
place to discuss issues, relay orders, and plan new
operations. The location might also serve as a cache
for weapons, equipment, medications, or other gear
that is illegal or difficult to obtain or carry on the world
in question. Virtually any small room where one can
enter and leave without detection may be used. This
can include abandoned buildings, secret rooms, and
otherwise public places, such as long-wrecked and
abandoned vehicles or starships, or the house of a
Rebel sympathizer.

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

oe

Safe-house: Safe-houses are bolt holes and hide-
outs for Rebel agents on the run. Some may be used
as meeting locations, but usually safe-houses provide
the best protection if they are completely unknown
to any pursuers or even most allies of the person
seeking safety. Since they are used to lay low for an
extended period of time, a safehouse is often an actual
residence, such as a standalone building, apartment,
hotel, or even something extraordinary, like a noble’s
palace. Safe-houses typically contain a small cache
of basic weapons, medical supplies, communications
gear, and food.

Medical Station: While most large bases have their
own medical facilities, smaller operations may not
have much more than the equivalent of a starship's
basic medical kit and bunk. Medical stations are hid-
den treatment facilities where sympathetic doctors and
medical technicians can treat injured Rebels for longer
periods of time. These stations are vital on Imperial
worlds with active Rebel cell networks or other activi-
ties, where treatment at a commercial or public facility
might draw unwanted attention from Imperial agents.
The level of treatment varies based on the availability of
Skilled medical personnel, medical droids, surgical facil-
ities, and bacta tanks. Medical stations are usually iso-
lated or concealed standalone clinics, but a few oper-
ate nearly in plain sight. Though risky, these stations
are purposely lost amid the chaos of extremely large
hospitals in major cities. Some manipulate the admin-
istrative systems to take over treatment rooms within
the regular facility, and others are hidden in basements
or maintenance levels of sprawling medical complexes.

Outpost: Outposts are the most common and versa-
tile Rebel base type. Scattered throughout the galaxy,
they are created and customized to fulfill the needs of
specific mission types. Few operate as general-purpose
bases, but some grow and develop as the situation war-
rants. Many are temporary and cheaply constructed,
and use local landforms and available equipment and
materials in their construction and outfitting. Others
are fortified, permanent positions with minor defen-
sive capabilities. Common missions include: listen-
ing, surveillance, intelligence, ordnance and resupply,
Starfighter base, medical support, Special Operations
base, training facilities, and maintenance. Outpost
populations range from 25 to 500 people, with the
Staff tailored to the outpost’s specific role.

Starfighter Outpost: Starfighter bases are outposts
established solely to Support one or more squadrons
of starfighters. Some bases support a specific mission
type, such as attack, intercept, or reconnaissance.
They have a good medical bay and repair facilities,
but a very limited crew. Starfighter missions tend to
be far away from the base's location in order to avoid

drawing suspicion as to its location. Imperials discover
Starfighter bases the most often, due to their neces-
sary traffic and extensive use.

Local Operations: Local bases are relatively perma-
nent facilities that are manned around the clock. These
bases are typically subsidiary to a larger Sector Force,
with rotating personnel between 10 and 1,000 people.
They seldom have permanent landing facilities for star-
Ships but are easily accessed by ground vehicles and on
foot. They may contain a garage. These bases usually
serve local Rebel operations in a given city or geographic
region. They include decent medical facilities, such as a
doctor or medical droid with a basic surgical suite.

Planetary Operations: Planetary bases are larger
versions of the local operations base. They have more
personnel, better defenses, and more-advanced medi-
cal facilities. Training rooms and equipment may also be
available. This base type typically includes one or more
landing facilities for starships, and may be defended by a
Squadron of airspeeders and/or starfighters. Due to their
activities, these bases are often located in remote loca-
tions, to avoid raising suspicion from the local population.

Sector Force Headquarters: Sector Force Headquar-
ters facilities vary widely by the strength of the local
Rebel forces. The smallest may be similar to a local or
Starfighter base. The best are often larger and more
complex versions of a planetary operations base. Such
a base includes extensive communications gear and is
the hub for sector intelligence operations and report-
ing. While the base is heavily defended by ground
troops and starfighters, and possibly even capital
ships, it usually has the means to evacuate personnel
very quickly, as well as a self-destruct mechanism to
prevent sensitive data banks and computer systems
from being captured.

Allied Command Headquarters: Allied Command
Headquarters Is similar to Sector Force Headquarters,
but may be located in the Allied Command's own mili-
tary facilities. As such, this base may have planetary
defense systems at its disposal, including powerful
local or planetary shields and small fleets of capital
Ships. The base may be very large, incorporating many
facilities over a wide area.

Main Rebel Alliance Base: The Alliance Main Base
is among the best-defended in the galaxy. SpecForce
units guard the facility, and some of the best starfighter
pilots are housed there. The base's location is a closely
guarded secret, as it is always the top target for the
Imperials. The facility is designed to be evacuated
extremely quickly, though Alliance High Command
may decide otherwise, depending on the situation. It
typically has the same facilities as a major Sector Force
Headquarters, with additional defenses and better per-
sonnel. See The Rebel Main Base and Yavin 4 on
page 498 for more information.

COMMON BASE
ENVIRONMENTS

Rebel bases may be found in almost any habitable,
barely-habitable, or virtually uninhabitable environ-
ment. Locations are selected by base type, proximity
to Imperial routes and installations, and suitability of
the environment for a particular role. Rebel engineers
are especially skilled at creatively adapting unusual or
dangerous locations to their purposes.

Abandoned structures: Abandoned structures may
be repurposed into a base of almost any type or size,
if the building is large enough. Ancient structures, such
as Yavin 4s Massassi temples, are occasionally useful
but are rarely suitable. However, abandoned mines,
industrial facilities, failed colonies, and ghost towns
might provide extensive structures that can be con-
verted to the desired accommodations.

Arctic: Arctic bases are found in the frozen wastelands
of planetary poles, glaciers, or entire frozen planets.
The cold plays havoc with equipment and puts a strain
on personnel.

Asteroid: These bases are built into natural or artificial
caverns, or attached to the sides of selected asteroids.
These hunks of rock are typically selected for their
internal stability and for the predictability and safety
of their orbits. However, such conditions can change,
especially in asteroid fields. Unexpected collisions by
nearby asteroids can completely alter the safety factor
of an installation.

Canyon: Canyons are a common choice for hidden
bases, especially canyons with multiple rivers and
complex landforms. These give pilots multiple choices
for entering and exiting the canyon system, providing
some aid in concealing the base’s location. The base
may be on the rim or valley floor, or in a relatively con-
cealed branch. Most terrestrial canyons have rivers
of some size, but not every world or environment has
active rivers.

Cavern: Natural caverns are ready-made bases for
virtually any size of operation. Small caves provide
unexpected hideouts in almost any environment.
Large cavern complexes can hide entire hangar bays
and hundreds or thousands of individuals. Some may
be expanded artificially, turning a known, smaller cave
into something unexpectedly bigger. This can throw off
pursuers if they believe an existing location is too small
to support the operation they re seeking.

Desert: Desert locations typically rely on natural rock
formations or oases for suitable concealment, and for
protection of bases from the extremes of the natural envi-
ronment. Extreme heat or radical changes in temperature
might cause problems with equipment. Sand and similar
particles end up everywhere, and inside everything, caus-
Ing engines, machinery, and droids to jam.

Jungle: A base in a jungle habitat usually relies on the
dense vegetation to conceal its location and on lively
local wildlife to protect it from unexpected visitors. While
the wildlife might also cause issues for base personnel,
they usually Know what to expect and how to handle it.

Mountainous: Mountainous locations may be con-
cealed in natural caverns, deep valleys, or the occa-
sional volcano. Active volcanoes are usually unaccept-
able; however, minimally active ones can provide a
certain amount of cover and deterrence if the risk of
eruption is deemed low enough.

Ocean: Deep ocean bases are typically inaccessible to
most starships, and must be approached and tended
by submersible vessels. Species that can breathe
underwater may have completely submerged bases
without any breathable-air chambers. Those beings
who require air to survive must include airlocks or
shields to maintain the integrity of the atmosphere.

THE REBEL MAIN BASE
AND YAVIN 4

Originally, the main Rebel Base housing Alliance High
Command was located on a small planetoid in the
Chrellis star system in the northern Outer Rim Terri-
tories. After the Empire reallocated its local sector

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

a

Starfleets, the undiscovered Chrellis Base became
uncomfortably close to Imperial Fleet activity. The Reb-
els relocated to Brigia, in the Tion Cluster, but were
forced to flee when the Imperials unleashed Operation
Strike Fear and targeted the base. The Rebels tempo-
rarily moved to Orion IV, but were again chased away.
Eventually, Dantooine proved to be a Suitable location
for the base, but the discovery of an Imperial tracking
device forced an emergency evacuation.

After abandoning Dantooine, the Rebels relocated
their main base to the long-abandoned Massassi Temple
on the virtually forgotten fourth moon of the gas giant
Yavin. Yavin 4’s dark side past was long forgotten, and
the Rebels moved in, blissfully unaware of the temple’s
Original uses and nature. Though some became con-
cerned about other nearby structures, it had little effect
on the main base. See page 4381 for more on Yavin 4.

The temple was a massive, stepped-stone, pyramid-
like structure with multiple exterior terraces. It easily
concealed multiple habitable levels both above and
below ground. Rebel technicians cleared the temple
of debris, vegetation, and creatures. They quickly
expanded the habitable spaces and transformed them
into a modern command center, packed with barracks,
hangars, briefing rooms, training facilities, defensive
systems, and much more.

Generally speaking, the lowest levels were used for
starship hangars and vehicle garages. The middle levels
contained the dwelling units, commissaries, barracks,
briefing rooms, conference rooms, sensor suites, and
more. The upper levels housed the main command
center, war rooms, security stations, high-level intel-
ligence analysis labs, and extensive medical facilities.
The uppermost chamber retained its original grand
appearance and was reserved for the few celebrations
the Rebels felt they could afford during their short time
on the moon.

The main base contained enough space to house
thousands of troops, staff officers, technicians, and
Support staff. It could field up to four starfighter squad-
rons, typically X-wings and Y-wings, as well as trans-
ports to quickly move personnel offworld should the
base be discovered. Additional landing zones were
concealed in the jungle near the temple for starships
that could not fit within the main hangars or were not
allowed to enter due to security concerns.

A power station was housed in a separate structure
about a kilometer away. The main generator originally
belonged to a Star Destroyer, and was salvaged and
relocated to Yavin 4. Auxiliary and backup generators
were located within the main temple structure.

/MAIN| RE

=x

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

WATERCREST SECRET
OUTPOST

The Watercrest outpost is an Alliance Intelligence base
of operations on the world of Reyniu, located just off
the Sanrafsix Corridor between the Omwat and Kabal
systems in the Mayagil sector. Reyniu is a terrestrial
world, with narrow bands of open ocean between its
eight major continents. The dominant landform com-
prises wide arid plains dotted with large inland sea
chains linked together by complex canyon systems.
Early forms of low vegetation cover much of the world,
and it has moderately developed animal life. The atmo-
sphere is breathable, but exposure for more than fif-
teen minutes typically requires a breath mask. The
need for specialized filters deterred colonization.

Prior to the Clone Wars, Reyniu enjoyed a brief boom
period as an exotic destination for the galaxy’s rich,
drawing interest from the nearby Eriadu system and
Seswenna sector. Resorts catered to sail barges and air
cruisers that would trace the canyons and take in the
considerable natural landscape. However, when the
Clone Wars broke out, Separatist space enveloped the
world. This cut it off from the Republic stronghold of Eri-
adu and, consequently, the tourist trade died off. Long
after the war, a few enterprising casino owners and star-
ship liners tried to restart the trade, but had very little
success rekindling Eriadu’s interest and soon moved on.

OPERATION FAR VIEW

After the Battle of Yavin, Alliance Intelligence wanted
to expand its operations in and around Seswenna and
its surrounding sectors. Eriadu is an important Imperial
world, and the Sullustan citizenry leans more toward the
Rebellion each day. The Bith homeworld is also in Mayagil
sector. Rebel Intelligence needed a staging area and base
for analysis of regional data. Spies, Special Operations
agents, cell network leaders for nearby worlds, and other
local Intel and minor military assets would use the base.

Al selected Reyniu due to its position near several
major and minor hyperlanes, and its proximity to a
variety of interesting worlds that could prove helpful
to Alliance activities. Nearby Kabal is a rare neutral
system, making it especially useful for Rebel trade
at Its legitimate markets and shadowports. Omwat is
an Imperial world, while Sanrafsix is known for spice
smuggling and lies on a hyperroute that eventually ties
the Rimma Trade Route to the Corellian Run.

WATERCREST OUTPOST

The outpost, code-named Watercrest, is housed in
the remote Cliffview Resort, making it one of the
fancier-looking and comfortable outposts in the Allli-
ance. Using a front company, Alliance Intelligence pur-
chased “The Cliffview” from its previous owners, who
were all too happy to be rid of the shuttered vacation

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

a

spot. Because Reyniu has scattered small settlements
clustered around a few remaining resorts struggling
to remain open, Alliance Intelligence “reopened” The
Cliffview for business. In reality, nearly all “guests” are
actually Alliance personnel.

In order to keep up appearances, Al occasionally
runs ads and hosts outsiders at one of the isolated
lodges. They are closely monitored and escorted as
part of the “exclusive and luxurious” personal service.
Rebel agent trainees are often used as hosts and staff,
to hone their acting and undercover skills. However, Al
doesn’t want the resort to become too well-known or
popular, or to have repeat customers, so disappointing
meals, staff, and service are part of the package deal.
lf needed, agents are dispatched to spread rumors or
adjust local perceptions of the facility.

The Cliffview sits on the rim of a 100-meter-tall, jag-
ged rocky cliff that borders and overlooks the Lunoa
inland sea. Guest rooms are built into the cliff face,
overlooking the water and connected by turbolift and
stairs to the main resort building above. Base opera-
tions are housed in and under the main building and
the adjoining domed hangar bays.

The Cliffview currently houses about 100 permanent
residents and 50 to 100 visiting agents and temporary
Staff. Its original (and considerable) droid housekeep-
ing and maintenance staff continue to keep the place
cleaned up and ready for visitors, albeit with some new
programming to ensure their discretion about the Reb-
els and their activities.

Starship traffic to and from Watercrest is strictly
controlled. To keep up appearances, all personnel are
shuttled in using Cliffview shuttles and airspeeders
from nearby spaceports or directly flown in from other
Star systems on tourist cruise ships that are also owned
by Rebel front companies for this purpose. The base
also occasionally serves as a way station or emergency
repair station for Rebel starfighters. Those ships dock
with modified bulk freighters at deep space rendezvous
points and are secreted away in the cargo holds. The
bulk freighters unload their containers at the resort.
Departure is handled in reverse order. If starfighter
operations are someday permanently added, the same
procedures will be used to conceal their presence.

WATERCREST BASE FACILITIES

The following are the base’s current facilities, but they
can be modified or expanded at any time by Rebel
engineers to fit changing missions.

Hangar Domes and Landing Fields: To protect
guests from the atmosphere, the resort features two
large hangar domes and a half dozen smaller domes
for private berths. An additional dome holds the thirty-
person airspeeder, used for local sightseeing tours.
The smaller domes take ten minutes to refresh their
air after opening. The larger domes can house up to a

ra

, i i vali i ih
os i i . wll i ie WA

# 3 No

bulk freighter and take thirty minutes to cycle. A few
tube accessways from the ship’s airlock to the building
are provided for guests who don’t want to wait. Rebel
technicians can provide basic repair and maintenance
services with the materials on hand. Landing fields
adjacent to the domes provide auxiliary landing zones.

Cliffview Main Building: The main building houses
all of the resort’s primary amenities, including two res-
taurants, a nominal gift shop, a pool, exercise rooms,
a small casino, kitchens, droid maintenance, offices,
and other facilities. Personnel use these for daily living,
tendea by the servant droids. Visiting agents regard it
as rest and relaxation.

Cliffview Guest Rooms: The guest rooms serve as
barracks and quarters for personnel. One isolated
bank is kept in prime condition for use with non-Rebel
guests. The rooms have enclosed balconies to maxi-
mize the view.

Air Scrubbers and Conditioners: These keep the air
within the resort breathable.

Command Center: Located in the basement of the main
building, the multilevel command center was once used
as a betting center for offworld races and sports events.
The Rebels now use those screens and holographic pro-
jectors to track Rebel operations and plan future events.

Main Generator: The Rebels modified and boosted
the existing main generator for their purposes. It sits
in the basement of the main building. The existing
backup generator remains for emergency power.

Briefing Rooms: One bank of guest rooms serves as
mission briefing rooms.

Defenses: Watercrest has few defenses, but four Cliffview
guest rooms have concealed, retractable laser cannons,

and two have concussion missile launchers. A pair of laser
cannons is also concealed in the touring speeder dome.
The base hopes to add shields in the future.

Sensors: The base relies primarily on passive sen-
sors to avoid detection. Regular speeder and ground
patrols provide supplemental protection.

Escape Equipment: |n case of evacuation, personnel
use all available starships and speeders. This includes
five speeder trucks and four airspeeders in a secret
hangar at the water's edge, accessed by using a secret
code on the guest room turbolifts.

Medical Suite: Two of the resort’s multi-room suites
hold a surgical suite, complete with a 2-1B medical
droid. Another suite holds two bacta tanks.

WATERCREST PERSONNEL

Alliance Intelligence operatives tend to rotate assign-
ments regularly, so it is rare for the permanent staff to
remain unchanged for more than a few months. Cur-
rently, the outpost is under the command of Major
Tohn Kalena, who defected from Imperial Navy intel-
ligence years ago. The human once lived on Eriadu,
giving him insight into intelligence operations on both
Sides. He gets to the point quickly and demands the
appropriate secrecy and care in all assignments. Prob-
lem agents are reassigned quickly.

About fifty percent of base personnel are human. A
large contingent of Sullustans focuses on operations on
Sullust and inside SoroSuub. About twenty Bith simi-
larly monitor operations around their nearby home
system of Colu (Clak’dor IV and VII). Transient person-
nel may be of any species in the Rebellion.

MON MOTHMA’S VISIT

Ms looking for a powerful and exciting ending

to a resistance-building campaign, or even a
single significant resistance-related adventure,
can use a visit by Mon Mothma as a reward for
a job well done. In a longer campaign, where
the players are building up their own resistance
forces, drawing the attention and gaining mem-
bership in the Rebel Alliance could be one of the
greatest story and campaign awards in the game.

Adventures featuring such a visit should be tension-
filled, but ultimately highly rewarding to the PCs.
Her visit should be met with all of the diplomatic
care taken between major governments. She is
heavily guarded, and her actions and movements
kept secret. Until negotiations are complete, she
and her escort are always on their guard. Even
prior to negotiations, some Rebel agents may be
dispatched to interview the major players and
perform some background checks.

The adventure should include Imperial threats.
Imperial agents might be tracking the local resis-
tance activities and detect the planned, impor-
tant meeting, though maybe not the partici-
pants. The resistance might have an Imperial spy
in their midst, who is flushed out while attempt-
ing to manage the unique opportunity to strike
at the Rebel leader. The Rebels might even bring
reinforcements, to save the day from a small
Imperial force attacking the resistance fighters.

Whatever happens, the GM should always keep in
mind how Mon Mothma and the Rebels view the
situation. If things go too far awry, or PC actions
or words make them suspect, Mon Mothma may
withdraw or offer different terms. In fact, the ten-
sion of completing the negotiations successfully
Should be as key as protecting the Rebel Alliance
Chief of State from unexpected threats.


### Rebel Battles and Tactics

REBEL BATTLES AND TACTICS

rom the smallest skirmish between opposing agents to

full-scale ground assaults and starship engagements,
virtually any incident can develop into a new front in the
Galactic Civil War. Massively outmanned and outgunned,
the Rebellion must rely on tactics other than pitched bat-
tles to strike at Imperial forces. Defeat and retreat against
the superior foe is common, and victories are hard-won.
Living to fight another day Is essential to the success of
the Rebellion. Retreat is often not only a wise long-term
move; it is built into Rebel battle planning.

REBEL BATTLES

Countless skirmishes, battles, and incidents occur
between the Rebellion and Empire without name or fan-
fare. While the Imperial bureaucracy strives to meticu-
lously record and report nearly every incident, Rebel
records are not as complete. The Alliance Military, Alli-
ance Intelligence, and most Allied Commands demand
regular reports, as any large military would, but with
SO many irregular forces and operations, details and
incidents are often omitted or underreported.

This section includes battles and operations signifi-
cant to the Rebellion as a whole, as well as some inci-
dents of a more common variety. These are important
to the day-to-day success of the Rebellion. Though
they may not have much galactic significance on their
own, they are important to local operations, morale,
and the lives of those involved.

ATRIVIS SECTOR BATTLES

The increasingly aggressive and effective resistance
fighters in the Atrivis sector accelerated the formal
establishment of a wider rebellion and the Rebel Alli-
ance itself. Fighting started with isolated resistance
groups on separate worlds. Initially, there was little
desire to work together, as these worlds were rivals
well before the Empire took control.

Given the remoteness of the sector in
the Outer Rim, the local Imperial forces
were small in number. As resistance
attacks increased, the Imperials
countered by installing a garrison
on Mantooine. The local fighters,
called the Mantooine Libera-
tors, attacked and destroyed
the garrison. In what would be
regarded as an early tactical
mistake, the Liberators tried to ~ ay
hold their ground and called
for a planet-wide rebellion.
Unknown to the Liberators,
the Empire was poised to strike
back, using a fleet stationed at

eve

the nearby system of Fest. Taken by surprise, the Lib-
erators were hunted down and utterly destroyed.

Meanwhile, the large resistance group on Fest watched
the battle with regret and fear, but also increasing deter-
mination. They realized that better communications
would have enabled them to warn the Liberators. They
changed tactics and linked up with other groups in the
sector to form the Atrivis Resistance Group, or ARG.
Their plan worked, improving their results and spread-
ing their movement. However, they soon discovered that
covering a single sector was not enough. Much like the
Liberators had no idea what was happening outside of
Mantooine, the ARG had no way of tracking Imperial
forces coming in from other sectors.

As she had done before, and would do again in
the months and years to come with other resistance
eroups, Mon Mothma visited the ARG to convince
them to join the growing Rebel Alliance. They nego-
tiated and signed the Treaty of Generis, bringing the
ARG into the Alliance and allowing Rebel assets into
the sector. Alliance High Command established an
important Outer Rim communications center at the
ARG main base to coordinate Rebel communications
throughout the northern sectors of the galaxy.

OPERATION STRIKE FEAR

As the Rebel Alliance initially became better organized,
attacks on Imperial targets became noticeably better
coordinated and more effective. The Empire ordered
Operation Strike Fear to crush the Rebellion and intim-
idate Rebel sympathizers by showing the high cost of
supporting the Rebel Alliance. The Imperials used their
superior strength to destroy Rebel bases, starships,
and other military units, severely damaging Rebel
operations in many sectors. Worse, relentless Imperial
attack and pursuit forced the main Rebel Base from
Brigia to Orion IV, until the Rebels finally disappeared
for a time to Dantooine.

A coordinated anti-Rebel propaganda campaign
boosted the high profile attacks even further. However,
the Rebels had victories of their own. The Rebel fleet
proved itself dangerous by capturing an Imperial frig-
ate and destroying the Star Destroyer /nvincible.

BATTLE OF YAVIN

By far the most important military victory for the Rebel
Alliance was the destruction of the Death Star at Yavin
4. The battle was unusual for many reasons, chiefly
because the Rebels did not immediately flee from the
superior firepower, as they normally would to save
their personnel and machines for future engagements.
This is especially surprising given that important Rebel
leaders were caught in the attack.

Given that Alliance High Command believed that they
could sustain the loss of the Main Base or the Alliance
Fleet, but not both, the fleet did not defend Yavin 4 from
the Death Star. Instead, using a hallmark of Alliance tac-
tics, the Rebels relied on their fighter squadrons to strike
a miraculous blow to destroy the battlestation. With
Han Solo and Chewbacca providing an unexpected last-
minute hand, Luke Skywalker made the impossible shot,
instantly becoming a Rebel hero. The cost to the Rebel-
lion was high, as few of the attacking fighters survived.

The impact on the Empire was even greater—the Reb-
els had scored an undeniable victory the galaxy could
not overlook, and the Empire’s reputation for power and
invulnerability was diminished.

COMMANDER ACKBAR AND
PROJECT SHANTIPOLE

After the Battle of Yavin, the Empire deployed Nebulon-
B frigates with increasing frequency for escort duty with
military and related convoys. As intended, the extra
firepower made Rebel hit-and-fade starfighter strikes
difficult. To make up for limited Rebel fleet resources
and starships, a new heavy fighter was designed to spe-
cifically counter the frigates and similarly sized escort
vessels. Commander Ackbar oversaw the project in the
Roche asteroid field, as the prototypes were designed
and built by the Verpine. Though some Verpine sym-
patnized with the Rebellion, their stated neutrality
and local Imperial garrisons kept them aligned with
the Empire overall. An Imperial spy enabled the Impe-
rial military to attempt a strike to seize the prototype
B-wing fighters. Ackbar and a band of Rebels battled
the Imperial strike force and managed to escape with
the ships. Ackbar was promoted to the rank of Admiral
and assumed his duties with the Alliance Fleet.

REBEL TACTICS

Rebel tactics share some of the same basic ideas and
methods, though the exact execution varies by force
size and proficiency. It is up to the tacticians and local
commanders to adapt successful tactics to their own
units and situations. Training is a never-ending process,
and Rebel leaders do their best to improve any unit's
battlefield abilities. This section includes some of the
Rebels’ more common tactics.

CELL NETWORKS

On staunch Imperial worlds, strong support of the
Empire combined with a high degree of security
makes most Rebel operations and recruitment efforts
extremely dangerous to the recruiters involved, as well
as to their relatives and friends. Dissidents and resis-
tance fighters find it difficult or impossible to meet,
whether openly or covertly, in large groups. On these
worlds, Alliance Intelligence tries to build a cell network
of clandestine agents and sympathizers.

Most of the network members are native to the
city or planet in question. Intelligence agents may be
imported to get the network going and to hold some
of the higher levels in order to coordinate with other
cell networks in the region and the Alliance itself.
Occasionally, a cell network is established by a local
resistance operation, and then becomes tied to the
Alliance. These networks often have different configu-
rations compared to the Alliance standard.

Cell networks are set up to protect the secrecy of the
operatives and their fellow agents. Most individuals in
the network have no idea how big the actual network is,
or who else is involved beyond their immediate cell. A
typical cell holds three people (some use four), with two
people connected to lower-level cells, and one person
connected to a cell above them. Upward connections
are blind, meaning that they don’t know the identity of
the person above them. Orders flow down through the
network, while return information is sent through blind
drops and other techniques up the chain of cells. The
top cell communicates with an area coordinator. The
area coordinator relays orders from the network leader.
The network leader oversees several area coordinators.
For safety reasons, the network leader is isolated from
the case officer at the local Sector Command. The case
officer manages the network and analyzes data and
operations for signs of Imperial infiltration.

Cell networks can produce irregular results. Depending
on the position and jobs of the cell network operatives,
they can be a treasure trove of data, or simply provide
the Alliance with basic manpower to carry out simple
intelligence gathering and the occasional sabotage or
civil disobedience operation. Ultimately, the cell network
helps to grow the Alliance in many different ways and to
counter Imperial activity in its area of operation.

TARGETS

he Imperial military is by far the most com-

mon target of Rebel attacks. Rebel military
forces prefer to engage Imperial military units
whenever possible. The Rebels are keenly
aware of the importance of public perception
and support. Any attack or action that need-
lessly endangers the public or targets nonmili-

tary operations is generally frowned upon on
principle. Such attacks damage the Rebels’
reputation and desired moral high ground,
and only play into the hands of the Imperial
propaganda machine.

In some cases, Imperialized companies or other
corporations that work closely or exclusively
with the Imperial military, or are involved in
institutionalized slavery, may be seen as legiti-
mate targets. Imperial shipyards and military
manufacturers often fall into this category.
Targets such as these are often protected by
Imperial forces as well as corporate security.

COMMANDO RAIDS AND
SPECFORCE OPERATIONS

Commando raids and Special Forces operations are
the surgical strikes of the Rebel Alliance. They carry
out targeted attacks against specific Imperial opera-
tions or locations, often in coordination with other
Rebel attacks or activities in the area. SpecForce oper-
ations are among the most highly planned and well-
executed attacks the Alliance can muster. They handle
the toughest operations, including raids on Imperial
bases and outposts, securing landing zones for inva-
sion forces, ambushing important enemies, and com-
mandeering enemy starships.

Commando raids can be carried out by SpecForce
troops, but are also handled by highly trained units in
the Sector Forces. These troops are typically intimately
familiar with their theater of operations. They know the
terrain, the enemy, the allies, and the mix of econom-
ics and culture within their target area. SoecForce units
sometimes train these commandos in preparation for
a given raid. As SpecForces are a limited asset, local
commando forces see the bulk of the action.

Raids are rarely carried out simply for wanton destruc-
tion of Imperial assets. Specific goals are always set. It
might be the acquisition of information, disabling a ship
or important vehicle, rescuing captured personnel, or
causing confusion and havoc prior to a larger attack.

FLEET DETACHMENT RAID

Although Mon Mothma has ordered an avoidance of all
large-scale fleet engagements, she recognizes the need
for the Alliance Fleet to remain active and engaged in

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

the war. Detachments are regularly ordered from the
main fleet to carry out missions in support of Sector
Forces or as ordered by Alliance High Command. The
most common of these are raids against vulnerable
Imperial targets.

Alliance Intelligence identifies and produces the
majority of targets, sending them on to Alliance High
Command for assessment and tactical planning. Given
the importance of the fleet, detachment raids are care-
fully planned, with every detail scrutinized to avoid Impe-
rial traps and maximize their damage potential. Detach-
ment raids can also bolster morale within the Alliance
and their allies, with a show of strength and support.

Raids typically target Imperial installations, from dis-
tant listening posts to small battlestations, and small fleet
or convoy actions. These targets are normally well-known
In advance, either by location, flight pattern, or both. The
Rebels avoid targeting one area too many times to avoid
potential Imperial traps set on repeated targets.

Detachments typically use one to three frigates
or crulsers, Supported by corvettes, gunships, and
armored freighters. A few feature starfighter carriers
beyond the capital ships, though these are rare. The
carriers boost starfighter strength significantly, and
are often deployed as their own task forces with a few
escort vessels.

HIT-AND-FADE ATTACKS

The hit-and-fade attack is the most basic and most
common Rebel attack type. In theory, it can be applied
to units as small as a few people, to starfighter attacks,
and even fleet raids. The idea is simple: strike a spe-
cific target quickly, and escape before the target can
adequately respond or enemy reinforcements arrive.
The speed and accuracy of the attack are all important
to this tactic’s success.

Basic hit-and-fade attacks are designed to inflict
as much damage in as little time as possible. More
advanced methods combine this tactic with a raid,
which attempts to secure and abscond with a specific
item, vehicle, or starship. One secret to planning this
type of attack is to successfully estimate how much
time the attackers have before the defenders respond
with enough force to destroy them or repel them. Such
raids might have multiple objectives, targeting one
after another until reinforcements arrive.

Hit-and-fade attacks are hallmarks of Rebel
Starfighter attacks against enemy vessels. However,
saboteurs and commandos also use this method regu-
larly. Well-designed attacks that occur over time can
frustrate enemy units and their commanders to the
point where they begin to alter their tactics, defenses,
and their planning.

When these attacks occur too often in a given area,
or against a specific type of target, Rebel commana-
ers must become wary of Imperial traps. Imperial or

THE REBELLION
AGE OF REBELLION

Be

CRACKEN'S CREW

racken’s Crew are the near-legendary sabo-

tage and guerrilla warfare experts led by
Airen Cracken. Originally, Airen and his crew
operated on their Mid Rim homeworld of Con-
truum, where they developed their cunning
techniques against local Imperial forces. Not
all of these were wholly destructive; many were
tailored to achieve a certain effect or accom-
plish a specific need. Often, their tactics were
to disable or destroy a specific target, while
avoiding damage to other equipment or injur-
ing bystanders. Cracken’s Crew was extremely
effective, and they signed their work with the
line “Cracken’s Crew Says Hello.”

Cracken’s efforts resulted in an extensive and
significant resistance campaign. With Con-
truum of limited value, Imperial forces actually
withdrew in frustration. Cracken soon moved
on to the Rebellion, taking much of his crew

with him. There, they teach their techniques
to Rebels throughout the galaxy. These range
from turning a blaster powerpack into a bomb
to rigging reactors or disabling large-scale
shield generators.

Rumor has it that Cracken once rigged a
virtual fleet of MSE “mouse” droids to fol-
low squads of marching stormtroopers while
they were patrolling an Imperial station. The
droids lined up behind the oblivious troopers,
mimicking their formation. When the troopers
finally noticed, the droids scattered, each rac-
ing up to a different trooper, squealing loudly.
When the inevitable intruder alert sounded in
response to Cracken’s actual sabotage activi-
ties, the shrieking droids raced after the squad
as it ran down the corridors. Some ran under-
foot, tripping troopers and sending them clat-
tering to the floor in a white, armored heap.
The rest of the droids maintained their pursuit
of the remaining troopers, squealing a siren-
like warning to any saboteurs still in the area.
Once Cracken escaped the station, the droids
rolled up to nearby Imperial troopers and
officers, chirping a makeshift “Cracken’s Crew
Says Hello” in a piercing, grating squeal—over
and over and over...

planetary military forces may use decoys and false
information to draw out the attackers and ambush
them. Alternatively, some Imperial commands create
rapia response forces, able to respond almost immedi-
ately to the reports of an attack.

SABOTAGE

Sabotage is very common Rebel tactic. While any
Imperial installation or military target could be tar-
geted at any time, this method works best when It is
coordinated with other attacks or activities, in order to
achieve maximum results. Sabotage Is typically carried
out by Rebel sympathizers, Rebel commandos, secret
Rebel cells, or individual Rebel intelligence agents.

Sabotage can be coordinated with hit-and-fade
attacks or other military tactics to strike a surprise blow
against an enemy ship or force. It might take down a
Shield generator at a critical moment, open the blast
doors to incoming raiders, disable a walker or starship
at a critical juncture, or use any number of other cre-
ative tactics to surprise and inhibit the enemy. A well-
planned and well-timed sabotage attack can confuse
and even demoralize enemy forces.

The saboteur must also have a reasonable escape
route, so they might live to fight another day. Depend-
ing on the situation, the saboteur may hook up with
enemy forces and withdraw with them, or find his own
way out. Undercover agents and sympathizers typi-
cally prefer to escape on their own in order to protect
their identities. If they are very good, or very lucky, the
Empire might not ever know they were there.

Sabotage is often carried out with the use of explo-
Sives. However, damage may also be inflicted by repro-
erammine vital systems, starship or vehicle navigation
computers, or even enemy droids. Any tactic that dam-
ages or disrupts a target counts as sabotage.

SPECIAL OPERATIONS MISSIONS

Special Operations missions run the gamut of opera-
tions types. These are the really strange missions that
fall outside of normal military, sabotage, or intelligence
activities, but may overlap with any or all of them at
the same time. Missions to investigate Jedi artifacts or
strange Force-related objects (or people) can fall into
this category. Unusual rescue or recruitment missions,
especially those in hard-to-reach locales or unusual
environments, also fall to Special Operations.

SPIES AND INTELLIGENCE OPERATIONS

Alliance Intelligence utilizes all sorts of fesources to
gather information on Imperial and related enemy
action across the galaxy. Spies are often autonomous,
deep cover agents who feed information to Al on a
regular basis. The Empire is constantly watching for
spies and runs counterintelligence operations regularly
to flush them out. Many true spies are adept in their
own area of expertise, but struggle when the situa-

tion changes, and they must go underground or a
for their lives. They are not general, jack-oFall-trades

agents, and they must be extracted from a deter ior
ing situation before it turns into a rescue MISSION:

STARFIGHTER STRIKES

To the Imperial Fleet and transport services, the Rebel
Starfighter attack is the most common and classic tac-
tic. Starfighter strikes are highly effective hit-and-fade
attacks. Most are well-planned, using the best intel
ligence data available. However, the realities of the
Galactic Civil War and Imperial movements can force
a spur-of-the-moment attack of opportunity against an
unlooked-for target.

Starfighter strikes are typically carried out by one
or more squadrons. They may operate from the same
base or starship, or they may rendezvous at the tar-
get or a specific location ahead of time. As starfighters
are a scarce resource, it is not uncommon for squad-
rons unfamiliar with each other to be brought in and
ordered to work together during a given attack. This is
especially true in larger assaults.

As a squadron normally uses only one type of
starfighter, missions that require multiple fighter types
usually use multiple squadrons. X-wings and/or A-wings
often escort Y-wings and/or B-wings in attacks against
capital ships or reinforced targets. Other fighter models
such as Z-95s are also used by Alliance Forces, though
they are less common, especially in the Alliance Fleet.
Starfighters that are not equipped with hyperdrives are
of much less value to Alliance Forces. The hyperdrive
is the main reason these small ships can operate with
such freedom of movement, even in heavily guarded or
patrolled Imperial space.


## 12: Adversaries

he galaxy is a vast, unbelievably varied place. There

~are thousands upon thousands of inhabitable worlds
within it, and even more that do not naturally support
life. According to the letter of galactic law, they are all
part of the Galactic Empire, whether they are aware of
it or not and whether they consent to it or not.

Few, if any, alien species are found among the ranks
of the Empire's leadership beyond a tiny handful whose
unique skills and value have overruled the Empire’s
discriminatory practices. Conversely, the Rebel Alli-
ance seeks to unite all species in the same manner in
which the Galactic Republic did. The diversity among
its ranks is extraordinary, with hundreds of different
species joining in hopes of securing peace and pros-
perity for their people, or at the very least, equality
with the human civilizations of the Core Worlds.

There are three different levels of opponents and
allies that characters will readily-encounter in the
galaxy, with different levels of threat posed to the
average squad of soldiers and spies. Although some
variation is to be expected, the broad categories are
minions, rivals, and nemeses.

Kam a,
RSARI Ee

Obi-Wan Kenobi

MINIONS

Minions are the most common NPCs encountered in
the Star Wars universe. These are nameless individu-
als who provide the muscle to flesh out encounters.
Squads of stormtroopers, packs of alien gang mem-
bers, and groups of Gamorrean thugs waving large
axes are all examples of minions. Their only real
threat is in numbers, and a minion is not expected to
stand toe-to-toe with a Player Character.

Minions are typically not real threats when encoun-
tered alone. Unfortunately, they are almost never
encountered alone, as they tend to operate in small
groups to increase their effectiveness. Minions will
rarely be more than a minor obstacle to most parties
of Player Characters, a delaying tactic that can stand
in the way of achieving a goal that a more powerful
enemy wishes to see prevented. Minions also tend to
have lower wound thresholds than other NPCs, mak-
ing it very likely that they’ll drop in one or two hits.

When characters attack a group, they attack the
group as a whole, not an individual. Working as a
group also allows minions to use skills. A minion
group gains | skill rank for each member of the
group beyond the first, as long as that skill is on
the minion’s list. So, a group of four stormtroopers
would count as having 3 ranks (for the three troop-
ers after the first) in any applicable skill checks the
group Is called on to make.

The nemesis is the opposite number of the Player
Character. An Imperial Moff, a powerful Force-user, ora
deadly assassin droid are examples of potential neme-
ses. They are identical to PCs in virtually every respect,
and may, in fact, be more powerful; this is necessary
to ensure that they are able to pose a threat to an
entire party. Nemeses frequently possess a number of
talents, have high characteristics and skills, and wield
equipment that rivals the gear of even the most well-
supplied of parties. Nemeses suffer strain and wounds
normally and do not have any special rules governing
their operations.

ADVERSARIES
AGE OF REBELLION


### Adversary List


### The Rebel Alliance

ADVERSARY LIST

he following is a list of adversaries the GM can use

in his adventures. These adversaries are divided
into several groups, depending on their affiliations and
motives. Each adversary's profile is also broken down
into several discrete sections, as follows:

¢ Name: The adversary’s name or type, plus whether
It is a minion, rival, or nemesis.

e Description: A section describing the adversary.

e Characteristics: The rating of the adversary’s six
basic characteristics.

¢ Soak/Defense and Thresholds: These are
the adversary’s soak, his defense (listed in both
melee and ranged values), and his wound and
Strain thresholds. Only nemeses have listed strain
thresholds. If a GM wishes to give a rival a strain
threshold, it should have the same value as the
listed wound threshold.

e Skills: Skills (if any) possessed by the adversary. If
the adversary is a minion, these skills only apply
in a group.

e Talents: Talents (if any) possessed by the adversary.

e Abilities: These are special abilities that are not
skills or talents.

e Equipment: This is the adversary’s equipment. The
entry only lists important equipment such as weap-
ons, armor, and vital pieces of gear. Items such as
clothing, comlinks, spare change, glow-rods, and so
forth are not listed but are still present if it makes
sense (and at the GM's discretion). Any bonuses
from equipment are already added to the profile.

It is important to note that adversaries do not always
follow the same rules that Player Characters do. These
profiles are generated for simplicity and ease of use, so
non-essential information can be omitted. For exam-
ple, some characters in this chapter have equipment
exceeding their encumbrance threshold. However, they
do not suffer from being over-encumbered. The equip-
ment they are listed with Is to allow them to be diverse
enough to be used in many different situations.

THE REBEL ALLIANCE

ormally known as the Alliance to Restore the Repub-

lic, this organization is Known throughout the galaxy
as the Rebel Alliance or simply the Rebellion, even
among its own members. Essentially a large coalition of
different factions opposed to the Galactic Empire, the
Rebellion is vastly less organized than most believe. The
core group of dedicated assets and personnel is com-
manded by Alliance High Command, but a great num-
ber of their cells and satellite forces are affiliated but
largely autonomous. This has created any number of
difficulties for the Alliance as a whole, particularly when
their associates commit an act that causes a public rela-
tions backlash against the entirety of the organization.
Given the nature of their struggle, it is the best that the
Alliance can hope to achieve until the balance of power
begins to shift.

One of the Alliance’s greatest strengths is the diver-
sity of its members. While the Empire is known for its
ruthless stance against non-humans, the Alliance rec-
ognized the strengths and rights of all species, and as
such, a significant number of Rebel agents and opera-
tives are non-human. Indeed, some of the more perse-
cuted species, including Bothans, Mon Calamari, and
Sullustans, joined the Rebellion en masse, helping in
part to compensate for the overwhelming advantage
of numbers that the Empire enjoyed over the Alliance.

ALLIANCE INFANTRY [MINION]

The rank-and-file of the Rebel Alliance’s Army and
Naval troopers are not that much different from their
Imperial counterparts, all things considered. They are
trained in a number of different arenas and in vari-
ous contingencies, and they are assigned in similarly
organized military units. Even their equipment tends
to be similar, although the Imperials generally have an
advantage in that area. What separates them is moti-
vation. The troops who serve the Alliance believe that
they are not just fighting for their own well-being, but
that of their families and innocent people all across the
galaxy. This conviction is what the Alliance believes will
allow them to ultimately win the day,

Skills (group only): Melee, Ranged (Heavy), Vigilance.
Talents: None.

Abilities: Covering Fire (may spend a maneuver to add
+ 1 ranged defense to up to three allied characters or
minion groups within short range until the end of the
next round; this does not stack with multiple uses).

ADVERSARIES
AGE OF REBELLION

Equipment: Blaster rifle (Ranged [Heavy]; Damage 9:
Critical 5; Range [Long]; Stun setting), combat knife
(Melee; Damage 3; Critical 3; Range [Engaged]), blast
vest (+ 1 soak).

BOTHAN SPY [RIVAL]

To most Bothans, information is king. Equally willing to
sell to either side in the conflict, they remain officially
neutral and the Empire has left them largely unmo-
lested. There is, however, a growing unofficial faction
that sees support of the Rebellion as the way forward.
Given their natural affinity for backroom politics, the
Bothans have taken very easily to the practice of espi-
onage, and have secretly caused a great deal of dif-
ficulty for the Empire. It is a Bothan spy network that
ultimately secures the plans for the second Death

Star, giving the Alliance the opportunity to strike a

devastating blow to the Empire.

WILLPOWER

5

DIPLOMAT [RIVAL]

The adamant, absolute stance of the Empire
when dealing with others, even its allies, is a
benefit of its overwhelming size and force, but

it also serves as an advantage of sorts for the
Alliance. With each atrocity or military inter-
vention that the Empire commits, it becomes
easier for the Alliance to point toward them
when attempting to recruit new allies in their
struggle. While some are afraid of the power
of the Empire, validating such terror tactics,
just as many individuals take great offense at the
oppression and totalitarianism of their would-be
masters and offer their support to the Alliance.
High Command has a limited corps of diplomats,
many formerly associated with the Galactic Sen-
ate, which is employed to make this sort of agree-
ment happen. These diplomats are extremely
patient, willing to wait as long as necessary for the
innocent people of the galaxy to realize the truth;
trying to press the point only increases their resis-
tance. Waiting to secure what is necessary for vic-
tory as more atrocities are committed is a price
that weighs quite heavily upon many of them.

Equipment: Comlink, datapad, robes (+ 1 soak).

ALLIANCE COMMANDER [NEMESIS]

The individuals chosen by Alliance High Command to
serve as individual commanders, whether of mili-
tary units or fixed assets, are invariably
intensely focused on their duties. The
position of commander leaves no
time for a personal life, and those
who accept the duty understand that
they are surrendering all in order to
serve the Alliance. In addition to
their talent for logistics and
tactics, commanders must
also be prepared to make
difficult decisions that will,
without question, result
In the loss of lives under
their command. These
Individual commanders
are granted tremen-
dous autonomy by
High Command, and
thus are often free to
pursue objectives of their
own choosing.
