49


## 13: Perlemian Haul

PERLEMIAN

he Perlemian Trade Route—also known as the

Axis—runs coreward from Coruscant to just past
Quermia, and is one of the oldest trade routes in
the galaxy. The Rebel Alliance has long maintained
reports of a mysterious Imperial convoy that travels
the length of the Perlemian Trade Route. Known col-
loquially as the Perlemian Haul by spacers and Reb-
els alike, it is rumored to carry sensitive (and sinister)
cargo to far-flung Imperial facilities and strongholds.
Its existence has never been confirmed...until now.

Intelligence has reached the Rebel Alliance indi-
cating the convoy’s next port of call, as well as hints
as to the nature of the high-value cargo it contains.
The Alliance isn’t about to let the elusive convoy—
or its valuable contents—pass them by. They require
a skilled team of operatives to confirm the convoy’s
existence, infiltrate one of its heavy freighters, and
successfully commandeer the vessel so they can take
possession of it.

FUL

HAUL

ADVENTURE SUMMARY

In this adventure, the Player Characters begin ir
media res, jumping out of hyperspace and approach-
ing Martle Station, an Empire-governed orbital plat-
form and the preeminent port of call for the Perle-
mian Haul. Once aboard the station, the PCs must
gather information about the convoy and prepare to
infiltrate it once it arrives.

When the convoy appears in the Remduba sys-
tem, the PCs have only a short. amount of time to
execute whatever plans they've formulated before
the Imperial freighters complete their cargo transfers
and enter hyperspace. Once aboard, they can be as
direct or oblique as they like, though being stealthy
certainly has its advantages.

The Perlemian Haul’s cargo—or, rather, that of one
of its freighters, designated as M226—is undeter-
mined, allowing the Game Master to make its con-
tents relevant to the party’s Duties.

PERLEMIAN HAUL

AGE OF REBELLION a39


### Planning and Preparations

PLANNING AND PREPARATIONS

efore beginning this adventure, the GM may want

to spend some time preparing and considering
ways to integrate his players into the ongoing narra-
tive. Perlemian Haul is designed to be easily modified
to emphasize one or more of the Player Characters’
Duties. With a little forethought, the GM can add this
to the adventure easily.

Specifically, the GM needs to decide what cargo
is on board Freighter M226 when the PCs hijack
the vessel. This could be something that pertains to
their Duty, something the GM thinks will be interest-
ing, or some combination of the two. Freighter M226
is large enough for several cargoes.

INVOLVING THE
PLAYER CHARACTERS

Since this adventure begins in media res, some of the
players may wonder why their characters are on this
mission in the first place. The simplest approach to this
is that the Alliance is always stretched thin. When they
received this information, the PCs were the only group
of trained Rebel operatives close enough to undertake
the time-sensitive mission.

However, if the GM wants to elaborate on the PCs
involvement, he could use the following plot hooks:

e The PCs were the agents who uncovered the data
about the convoy in the first place. After capturing
an Imperial Navy officer, they were able to inter-
rogate him and learn about the convoy.

e Alliance Command does not believe the convoy
exists. However, a slicer working for the Alliance
does believe. He has contacted the PCs and con-
vinced them of the validity of the information. Now
the PCs are acting independently. If they succeed,
they may be lauded as heroes, but if they fail, they
could be branded as traitors.

MISSION OBJECTIVES
AND PC DUTY

The precise cargo carried by the ships of the Perlemian
Haul is entirely up to the GM. It is suggested that the
GM take into account the Player Characters’ individual
Duties and customize the adventure based on the fol-
lowing suggestions.

The heavy freighters that make up the convoy are
large ships with massive cargo capacities, and it’s
entirely plausible for one or more of the following
Duty-based cargoes to be incorporated into the adven-
ture. Doing so gives every Player Character a personal
Stake in seeing that the mission succeeds, even if the

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

S

presence of said cargo is only rumored or hinted at by
the intelligence the Alliance has gathered.

COMBAT VICTORY

PCs with the Combat Victory Duty have plenty to con-
tribute to the mission. After all, the Empire uses some
of its finest naval troops as security for the Perlemian
Haul. Though the PCs will doubtless be outnumbered
and outgunned, overcoming such overwhelming odds
should prove to the Empire that the Rebel Alliance
isn't to be trifled with, even when the odds are stacked
against its operatives.

With skill and thoughtful preparation, much of the
mission can be accomplished without firing a single
shot. Despite this, direct confrontation with Imperial
forces is almost unavoidable.

COUNTER-INTELLIGENCE

Though the Perlemian Haul’s amenities are hardly luxu-
rious, the secret nature of its composition and route
has always proven useful to the Imperial intelligence
community. At the GM's discretion, Soren Talis, an
Imperial COMPNOR agent, can be traveling aboard
the convoy. An agent from this reviled agency presents
the Alliance with a tempting target for assassination or
acquisition. Use the profile for the COMPNOR agent,
found on page 416.

INTELLIGENCE

Even though the location of the convoy’s next stop has
been revealed, the exact nature of the cargo it carries is
Still a subject of debate. If PCs with the Intelligence Duty
can learn the precise details of the Perlemian Haul’s
cargo—as well as acquire a list of the myriad destina-
tions it is scheduled to visit along its route—the Alli-
ance’s own intelligence agents would gain substantial
traction in their fight against the Empire. Securing this
information will allow the Alliance to plan ambushes,
raids, and strikes against the convoy in the future.

INTERNAL SECURITY

One of the convoy’s ships is transporting a high-priority
Rebel traitor who possesses extensive knowledge of the
Rebel Alliance and its operations, personnel, and objec-
tives. The traitor, Ravin Zael, is traveling on the convoy
secretly to a debriefing with COMPNOR agents. The GM
should inform the PC with this duty that a Rebel agent in
the area has gone missing (as part of a routine informa-
tion brief from the Rebellion), but not that Zael is a trai-
tor. Once the PCs locate Zael, they must deduce he is a
traitor, not a prisoner, then decide what to do with him.
Use the Quartermaster profile on page 414.


### Episode I: Martle Station

PERSONNEL

A small group of Alliance SpecForce troops have been
captured and are being transported as prisoners within
the convoy. These unfortunate individuals have nothing
to look forward to but torture and death at the hands of
the Empire. Given their skill, loyalty, and renown within
the Alliance, staging a rescue of these individuals would
serve to both boost morale and bolster the Rebellion’s
ranks. Use the SpecForce Infiltrator profile found on
page 414, removing equipment and weapons.

POLITICAL SUPPORT

A vocal opponent of the Empire hailing from a neutral
planetary system has recently been taken into custody
by Imperial Security “for his own protection.” Freeing
Temmin Vyn from his captivity aboard one of the con-
voy's transports displays the Empire’s sinister nature to
his people, the Quarren, and likely motivates them to
support the Rebel Alliance in the future. Use the Quar-
ren Agitator profile found on page 4135, removing his
equipment and weapons.

RECRUITING

The Empire isn’t squeamish about utilizing slave labor,
especially in remote parts of the galaxy. One or more
of the convoys transports is carrying a large number
of slaves. Rescuing these unfortunates from Imperial
servitude will allow the Player Characters to offer them
a place as personnel within the ranks of the Rebel Alli-
ance, in addition to denying their labor to the enemy.

The GM is unlikely to need profiles for the slaves. How-
ever, if he needs them, he can treat them as human
minions with a 2 in each characteristic, and no skills.

RESOURCE ACQUISITION

The Alliance is always in need of equipment, and many
of the convoy’s ships are listed as carrying vast quan-
tities of military gear intended for Imperial troops in

remote garrisons. If even a small fraction of this gear
can be captured on behalf of the Alliance, it could
mean a lot to the troops on the front lines.

SABOTAGE

Though this adventure assumes that Alliance High
Command wishes to divert one of the convoy’s ships to
alternate coordinates so that it can be appropriated, it
is also possible that the PCs are asked to sabotage the
ship instead. Planting a bomb or setting the ship’s reac-
tor to detonate when it arrives at its destination can
cause massive collateral damage to the rest of the con-
voy. Alternately, the ship in question may be carrying
a vast collection of droids or other sensitive cargo that
the players can sabotage, turning it against the Empire.

SPACE SUPERIORITY

Destruction or appropriation of one or more of the con-
voy's valuable transports weakens the Empire's fleet.
In addition, the transport in question might contain
Starfighters, starship weapons, or other technological
systems that can be stolen from the Empire and used to
strenethen the Rebellion’s fleet of starships.

SUPPORT

Support-minded PCs should find satisfaction in merely
helping their fellows achieve their own goals on behalf
of the Rebel Alliance.

TECH PROCUREMENT

A top secret device is being transported by the convoy
—a power source, a new Classification of personal
weapons or armor, a highly trained scientist, or even
experimental cybernetic components. As such, PCs
with the Tech Procurement Duty must try to acquire
the new technology so that Rebel scientists and tech-
nicians can study or reverse-engineer it to further the
cause of the Alliance.

EPISODE I: MARTLE STATION

he adventure begins with the PCs arriving at Martle
Station in a stolen, disarmed Lambda-class shuttle.
Read aloud or paraphrase the following:

The PCs are, of course, that elite strike force. They were
briefed on their mission before they left their base of oper-
ations, and they know the following pieces of information.
The GM can make this information available immediately,
or if the PCs prefer to get directly into the action, he can
dole this information out as the game progresses.

e The Perlemian Haul refers to a secret Imperial
convoy that purportedly plies the Perlemian Trade
Route. Its existence has never been proven, and it
is typically considered to be a myth. If it exists, it
is used by the Empire to transport highly classified
equipment, classified prisoners, or important per-
sonnel between Imperial installations.

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

e The convoy is rumored to be composed of modi-
fied bulk transports and escorted by heavily
armed capital ships that carry no Imperial mark-

ings or insignia.

e The PCs’ target is Freighter M226. The cargo of

Freighter M226 has already been determined by

the GM, and should be detailed here. The freighter’s

captain, Lt. Commander Jilan Noor, is a highly moti-
vated career officer who may be facing court mar-

tial, making him even more eager to prove himself.

The PCs must infiltrate Martle Station using cover

identities as traders, then contact a local informant
named Passk. He should be able to assist them

in their efforts to infiltrate Freighter M226. Once
aboard, the Player Characters are to commandeer
the vessel by any means available, drop out of
hyperspace, and rendezvous with an Alliance strike
group that will take possession of the ship.

Once the players have had a few minutes to digest
whatever information the GM imparts, he should con-
tinue with the following:

The PCs possess false identification provided by the

station officials. Getting aboard the station is a Simple
matter of giving a remotely viable reason for WiShe

traffic control staff is lazy and lackadaisieal in the
extreme. Docking fees are inflated to reflect the fact
that the authorities are corrupt and willing to Ove

look all but the most egregious displays Of Bra
or stupidity.

a

Once the Player Characters are aboard
Martle Station, they are free to explore
it within reason. Certain decks—notably
those given over to the Imperial Gov-
ernor, the garrison’s troops, and other
Imperial concerns—are off limits, but
the majority of the station is open and
boasts only light security in the form
of cameras, mechanical locks, and the
occasional patrol consisting of bored
Imperial Navy troopers.

PERLEMIAN HAUL

Alliance that should stand up to the lax scrutinyeOiias

So long as the Player Characters don’t do anything
to attract unwanted attention, they have little trouble
locating their contact, a shady Trandoshan infochant by
the name of Passk. Tracking down Passk should require
an Easy (>) Streetwise or Knowledge (Underworld)
check, or an Average (@ @) Knowledge (Outer Rim)
check. Failure means the PCs do not find Passk, but can
attempt the check again at a later date. Failure with ¢°p
means that Passk finds them, instead (and spends much
of their subsequent encounters gloating about It).

PASSK’S ODDITIES

A Trandoshan known as Passk runs a threadbare curi-
osities shop in what is considered to be Martle Sta-
tion's urban center. The station’s citizenry—of which
there are nearly two thousand—consists primarily of
shiphands, dockworkers, and station support staff, as
well as the civilian businesspeople and employees who
keep them fed and entertained. Passk is one of the
latter, though his contribution to the station's overall
functionality is somewhat questionable.

When the Player Characters finally locate Passk’s
Oddities and enter the shop, read or paraphrase the
text on the following page aloud:

Passk greets all visitors with a broad, toothy grin.
Speaking in a sibilant voice with a unique and some-
what dissonant cadence, he presents the junk in his
shop to potential customers as if it is the finest trea-
Sure from around the galaxy. Though some of the items
might be of mild interest to collectors or scholars, the
majority of Passk’s stock is mundane, overpriced junk.

Despite his pleasant and unassuming demeanor,
Passk is an incredibly savvy individual who is con-
stantly on the prowl for a profit of some kind.
He deals with both Alliance agents and their

ticed con man. Passk prides himself on knowing
nearly all there is to know about Martle Sta-
tions underground economy, and what
he doesn't know he can usually find
out as long as the proper bribes
are made.

sy

order to get Passk’s ‘ia’.
complete cooperation, ey

able to placate him ff
they can offer him enough
money for his services. Even
though Passk plays both sides of
the fence, his ultimate loyalties are
to the Alliance, for reasons he would
rather not explain.

Passk has heard of the Perlemian
Haul, though his knowledge of the
mythical convoy is nowhere near
complete. He isnt surprised by
the convoy’s existence, but the
potential confirmation of such
an obscure piece of spacers lore
makes him practically giddy.
Unless the players have specific
plans of their own, Passk is all too
happy to offer advice for gather-
ing information on the impending
arrival of the Perlemian Haul.

PASSK, TRANDOSHAN

Passk appears somewhat short for a Trandoshan, and
he dresses in outdated business attire. His amber eyes
glisten with a hidden cunning, and he always seems
to be smiling about something. He prides himself on
being able to find out anything; with enough time and

credits, he usually can.

PASSK’S SUPPLIES

If approached, Passk admits that he possesses two
navy trooper uniforms {including blast helmets and
vests) that the PCs may be able to use as disguises—
fora small fee, of course. Obviously, any PC attempting
to disguise himself as an Imperial Navy trooper must
be human. Non-human PCs might fill the roles of pris-
oners so long as they are appropriately bound and
restrained. Wearing the uniform adds [_] to any Decep-

THE IMPERIAL GOVERNOR

Another option open to the PCs is to attempt to coerce
the Imperial Governor, a human female named Varla
Prule, into aiding them. Though drunk with the ques-
tionable power of her position, Prule is under no illu-
sions as to her status in the grand scheme
of things. As such, she is as corrupt
as they come,

and is more than willing to accept a bribe or two in
exchange for looking the other way.

As he is on a first-name basis with her, Passk has little
trouble introducing the PCs to Governor Prule. Passk
insists the Player Characters dress for the occasion, as
the governor is a vain woman who prefers to socialize
only with those individuals who have at least the out-
ward appearance of respectability. He also cautions that
her cooperation doesn't come cheaply, and she insists
on a large sum of credits in exchange for her help.

Governor Prule enjoys negotiations, especially in
cases where she expects to profit. She is also overly
fond of dashing young human men, reacting quite
favorably to them. Any such Player Characters gain J
when making opposed Charm, Deception, or Negotia-
tion checks against the governor.

lf appropriately compensated, Governor Prule can
provide docking schedules, work rosters, and other rel-
atively mundane reports that shed additional light on
the Perlemian Haul’s schedule in the Remduba system.
Though she looks the other way and provides informa-
tion to the Player Characters, she is far from willing to
throw away her career if the stakes get too high.

Varlas base price for providing the Player Charac-
ters with restricted information is 1,000 credits. She
is more than willing to negotiate this sum up or down,
depending on the type of information she is providing.
Player Characters with no money to speak of might be
able to barter their skills for information, as well.

PASSK’S INSURANCE POLICY

Passk deals with the governor on a near-constant basis,
but he isnt a fool. He's kept records of every illicit
transaction he’s made with the governor, and even has
some solid physical evidence that can damn her if it
were to wind up in the wrong hands. Should the PCs
learn of Passk’s ability to blackmail the governor, they
may attempt to gain access to his evidence against her.

Though he can be convinced to hand it over, Passk
is less than thrilled to give away what he perceives as
his nest egg. The information is his form
of insurance while dealing with
the governor, but reveal-
ing its existence would
be sure to cause his
relationship with her
to suffer—at least in
the short term.

VARLA PRULE,
CORRUPT GOVERNOR [RIVAL]

Varla Prule is a middle-aged woman of means who man-
aged to secure the questionably prestigious rank of Gov-
ernor of the Remduba system. She speaks with a refined
Coruscanti accent, and dresses in a fine white Imperial
uniform when seeing to her duties aboard Martle Station.

Skills: Charm 3, Coercion 2, Deception 3, Discipline 2,
Leadership 3, Negotiation 3, Ranged (Light) 1.
Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Fine clothing, jewelry, uniform, light
blaster pistol (Ranged [Light]; Damage 5; Critical 4;
Range [Medium]; Stun setting).

FRIENDS IN LOW PLACES

If bribing the local governor doesn’t appeal to the
Player Characters, Passk has many contacts and asso-
ciates among Martle Station’s working-class citizens.
Many of these associates are employed in the plat-
form's cargo bays, moving equipment and commodi-
ties to and from the transports visiting Remduba Il.

Given that many of these unscrupulous individuals
live hand-to-mouth, they may be willing to accept pay-
ment from the Player Characters in exchange for their
complicity. Some can even access transport scheda-
ules, while others can provide transportation to and
from starships that come to Martle Station for loading,
unloading, or resupply.

Of all the workgangs on Martle Station, the most
prominent identifies itself as the Big Black. Members of
the Big Black come from all species, and each one has
a black spot visibly tattooed somewhere on his body.
Passk can introduce the players to the Big Black’s
leader, a surly and characteristically bad-natured
Zabrak named Raf. Raf is no friend of the Empire.
Should he learn the Player Characters are members of
the Rebel Alliance, they find him much easier to work
with. Any opposed Charm, Coercion, Deception, or
Negotiation checks made against Raf under these cir-
cumstances gain (_]. Raf is ultimately loyal to his work-
gang, however, and he won't risk any of their lives in the
process of helping the Player Characters.

RAF, ZABRAK WORKGANG LEADER [RIVAL]

Raf is a large, muscular, and albino Zabrak, with pale skin
and pink, brooding eyes. A large black spot has been
tattooed in the center of his forehead. He dresses in a
worn work suit and carries a number of vibroknives. Raf
has a reputation on the docks for being easily insulted.

Skills: Athletics 4, Brawl 4, Coercion 2, Melee 3, Resil-
lence 3, Vigilance 3.

Talents: Soft Spot (after making an attack with a non-
starship/vehicle weapon, may spend one dark side
Destiny Point to add damage equal to his Cunning to
one hit of the successful attack).

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Three vibroknives (Melee; Damage 5;
Critical 2; Range [Engaged]; Pierce 2, Vicious 1), space
suit, breath mask, datapad.

GETTING A JOB

Though it requires a bit more doing, the cheapest
method of gaining access to the Perlemian Haul’s
freighters is to apply for and gain employment with
Martle Station’s civilian shiphands and dockworkers.
Though the job market is relatively stagnant, and new
employees are rare and conspicuous, the Player Char-
acters can make the proper inroads with one or more
of Passk’s contacts and attempt to get hired.

Being employed on Martle Station itself is dangerous.
The dockworkers are affiliated with specific workgangs
that bid for contracts and occasionally fight among
themselves in order to secure paying work in the sta-
tion’s cargo bays. Any new workers, especially those who
aren't affiliated with an existing workgang, are subject
to intimidation and violence from their perceived rivals.

lf the PCs can survive their employment long enough for
the convoy to arrive in the Remduba system, they should
have little trouble gaining access to Freighter M226.

MARTLE STATION DOCKHANDS [MINION]

The dockhands of Martle Station work together in tight-
knit groups called workgangs. Each workgang dresses In
a similar fashion, using distinguishing markings and colors
to proclaim their allegiance. Some workgangs are divided
along species lines, though most integrate members of
any species so long as they are loyal and productive.

Skills (group only): Athletics, Brawl, Coercion, Mechan-
ics, Melee.

Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Heavy tool or club (Melee; Damage 5;
Critical 5; Range [Engaged]; Disorient 2), workgang
uniform, breath mask, space sult.

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

saad ery SP “ah
fz SSS dl

BOARDING
FREIGHTER M226

Once the Perlemian Haul arrives in the Remduba sys-
tem, Martle Station becomes a hotbed of activity. Large
quantities of ore, brought up from Remduba II and
stored in orbital containers, are immediately loaded
and ferried out to the convoy’s waiting freighters. Mar-
tle Station and the convoy’s two Star Galleons disgorge
a number of TIE/In fighters to provide a security screen
for the convoy as it loads and unloads its cargo.

Nearly every workgang aboard the: station is
assigned a docking bay. Even the station’s security
troops emerge from the proverbial woodwork in order
to keep an eye on the proceedings. Useful methods
for gaining access to Freighter M226 include stowing
away on one of Martle Station’s cargo tugs or cargo
canisters or posing as part of a workgang sent to either
deliver or retrieve cargo from one of the freighters.

So long as the Player Characters have done a
respectable job gathering information and making
plans to board Freighter M226, they should be able
to take advantage of all the activity and board the
freighter with very little trouble.

OTHER OPTIONS

ue to their inventive nature, Player Charac-

ters have a habit of coming up with plans
of their own. In the event they proceed in a
direction that is unforeseen, either by this
adventure or by the Game Master, their plans
should never be discarded out of hand. If their
plans are reasonable, they should be allowed
to play them out. If these plans intersect with
any of the aforementioned options, try to
weave them together if possible.

CONVOY COMPOSITION

The Perlemian convoy is comprised of a dozen /Jem-
ple-class heavy freighters, including Freighter M226.
Escorted by two Star Galleons, which -are in turn
equipped with TIE/In fighters to provide fighter cover-
age when the convoy has dropped out of hyperspace,
the convoy is anything but helpless.

While the Empire could certainly provide the convoy
with more powerful escorts, such as cruisers, dread-
noughts, or even Star Destroyers, such high-profile
ships would attract unwanted attention from Alliance
forces, as well as pirate groups and other criminals. So
far, keeping a low profile has worked for the Empire,
and the convoy is rarely considered to be unusual.

STAR GALLEON ARMED TRANSPORT

Though used to provide armed escort for the Perle-
mian convoy, the two Star Galleons are also used to
transport cargo when necessary.

, vat2t2i2 a I (i

ALLE PSS THRESHOLD SS
5 fm is |

Hull Type: Transport/Star Galleon.

Manufacturer: Kuat Drive Yards.

Hyperdrive: Primary: Class 2; Backup: Class 15.
Navicomputer: Yes.

Sensor Range: Medium.

Ship’s Complement: One hundred fifty officers and
enlisted crew.

Encumbrance Capacity: 10,000 or more, depending
on configuration. —

Passenger Capacity: 400.

Consumables: Six months.

Price/Rarity: 1,500,000 credits (R)/7.
Customization Hard Points: 2.

Weapons: Five port and five starboard-mounted
retractable medium turbolasers (Fire Arc Port or
Starboard; Damage 9; Critical 3; Range [Long]; Slow-
firing 1, Breach 4).

Forward concussion missile launcher (Fire Arc For-
ward; Damage 6; Critical 3; Range [Short]; Breach 4,
Blast 4, Guided 3, Slow-Firing 1).

TEMPLE-CLASS HEAVY FREIGHTER

The bulk of the Perlemian convoy consists of Jemple-
class heavy freighters. These heavy freighters sport a
number of modifications, making them anything but
stock. The majority of these modifications include
sensor-jamming equipment designed to keep the spe-
cific nature of their cargo a secret.

The Jemple-class heavy freighter is a relatively new
design constructed by Kuat Drive Yards. At nearly 400
meters in length, each one is a behemoth. Designed pri-
marily to haul cargo, the Jemple-class heavy freighter
is painfully slow and ponderous. This ship's standard
crew complement numbers 50 individuals, including
the captain, executive officer, six bridge crew, six engi-
neers, one surgeon, one corpsman, fourteen crew, and
twenty navy troopers acting as security.

The cargo bays of the ship, of which there are three,
comprise the majority of its length. Joined end-to-end
and connected by a series of ventral transport corri-
dors, each cargo bay is modular and detachable, and
can haul nearly 90,000 metric tons.

Each ship in the Perlemian convoy is slightly differ-
ent from its fellows, depending on the precise nature of
the cargo it carries. For example, those that carry addi-
tional passengers (such as VIPs, prisoners, or slaves)
require at least one passenger or detention block com-
partment. A full list of compartment types and their
specifications is provided elsewhere.

SILHOUETTE | SPEEQ | HANDLING

Hull Type/Class: Heavy Freighter/Temple.
Manufacturer: Kuat Drive Yards.

Hyperdrive: Primary: Class 2; Backup: Class 18.
Navicomputer: Yes.

Sensor Range: Close.

Ship’s Complement: Fifty crew.

Encumbrance Capacity: 430,000 depending on
configuration.

Passenger Capacity: 150 depending on configuration.
Consumables: Four months.

Price/Rarity: 950,000 credits/6.

Customization Hard Points: 6.

Weapons: None.

TEMPLE-CLASS MODULAR CARGO HOLDS

One of the Jemple-class heavy freighter’s more attrac-
tive features is a series of modular cargo holds designed
for it by Kuat Drive Yards. Each hold is capable of trans-
porting up to 90,000 tons of cargo, depending on con-
figuration. Though they have no locomotive systems of
their own, most of the Jemple-class cargo holds have
self-contained life-support systems. At the freighter

captain's option, individual cargo bays can be detached
in open space, though this is typically frowned upon
unless the reasons for jettisoning the cargo outweigh
the risks of keeping it attached to the ship.

Each type of modular cargo hold is detailed below.
This list is far from exhaustive. GMs with specific ideas
concerning the construction, purpose, and/or layout
of Freighter M226’s specific holds should feel free to
modify them however they wish.

BULK CARGO HOLD

The standard cargo hold utilized by the Jemple-class
heavy freighter is known as the bulk cargo hold. Gener-
ally speaking, bulk cargo holds are unusual by most
estimations, and are capable of storing and transport-
ing nearly any kind of cargo. Though not specifically
conditioned for extreme environmental controls, the
temperatures and humidity within these massive holds
can be adjusted within certain baseline parameters,
therefore allowing for a perpetually steady (if some-
what mundane) internal climate.

DETENTION BLOCK/PRISON CONTAINMENT

Detention block/prison containment holds are designed
to confine prisoners and other unwilling passengers.
Internally, they resemble Imperial detention facili-
ties, and they are staffed by additional security crew
depending on the number of prisoners being housed
within them. Prisoners in detention blocks are kept
under one of three circumstances: solitary confine-
ment, shared confinement, or general confinement.

Prisoners in solitary confinement are kept alone in
small cells with little more than a bed and a refresher
as amenities. Interrogations are carried out within
these cells, and the walls are soundproofed. So long as
the cell’s blast door is sealed, the prisoner's screams
are not audible to his neighboring captives.

Shared confinement spaces include cells that house
between two and four prisoners apiece. Like solitary
confinement cells, they have limited amenities and no
privacy. A single refresher serves all occupants, and
Slabs for sleeping and sitting extend from the room's
walls. Prisoners kept in shared confinement are typi-
cally taken to a separate interrogation room should
they need to be questioned or tortured.

General confinement houses up to fifty prisoners
together in close quarters. The conditions are squalid,
with as few as one or two refreshers shared by all pris-
oners. If necessary, the Empire has been known to
place two or three times as many prisoners into general
containment as can be comfortably accommodated. In
such conditions, diseases spread quickly, and prisoner
life expectancy is generally short and uncomfortable.

Each detention block hold provides cramped accom-
modations for up to fifty additional security personnel,
though this number varies depending on how many
prisoners are kept aboard.

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION


### Episode II: Stern to Stem

IT-O INTERROGATION DROID [RIVAL]

One of the droids most commonly encountered in Impe-
rial detention facilities is the IT-O, a spherical black inter-
rogation droid studded with sensors and surgical tools.
Most often accompanied by two navy troopers, the IT-O
is an expert at forcibly convincing prisoners to talk.

Skills: Coercion 3, Cool 2, Discipline 2, Medicine 3,
Melee 2, Perception 2.

Talents: None.

Abilities: Droid (does not need to breathe, eat, or drink
and can survive in vacuum or underwater. Immune to
poisons and toxins).

Equipment: Laser scalpel (Melee; Damage 3; Critical
5; Range [Engaged]; Pierce 2), hypodermic injectors,
interrogation tools.

ENVIRONMENTALLY CONDITIONED HOLD

From scorching temperatures to sub-freezing climates,
environmentally-conditioned cargo holds can maintain
any form of internal climate. Whether used to transport
finicky cargo (such as chemicals or other elements) that
can only be preserved at such temperatures, or an alien
species with special environmental requirements, these
cargo bays are relatively rare but useful nonetheless.

Crew or other individuals wishing to move about envi-
ronmentally conditioned holds must wear appropriate
gear, such as hazmat suits, breath masks, environmen-
tal clothing, or space suits. Both internal and exter-
nal airlocks help to maintain different atmospheres
between the ship’s crew and the cargo hold itself.

HAZARDOUS MATERIALS CONTAINMENT

Similar in design to an environmentally conditioned
hold, hazardous material containment holds are
slightly different in that their cargo is volatile in some
way. From explosives to fuel to radioactive materi-
als, hazardous materials holds are able to transport
such dangerous cargoes with relative ease and safety.
Shielding comes standard with such holds, prevent-
ing unwanted emissions from harming nearby ships or
orbital environments.

PASSENGER COMPARTMENTS

Unlike detention/prisoner containment holds, Jemple-
compatible passenger compartments are practically
luxurious. They are designed to accommodate far
fewer passengers than their detention counterparts do,
and they are far more comfortable and accommodat-
ing. Security is generally less of a concern in passenger
compartments, as they are not designed to keep their
occupants confined for any length of time.

In addition to conveying VIPs, high-value prisoners
are also kept in passenger compartments, though they
are traditionally assigned a security detail.

EPISODE Il: STERN TO STEM

ith the appropriate plans and actions, the Player

Characters should soon find themselves aboard
Freighter M226. Freighter M226 is a slightly modified
lemple-class heavy freighter. Its three modular cargo
holds, from fore to aft, are designated Mern, Aurek, and
Dorn. Each of M226's cargo holds should meet certain
specifications, depending on the requirements of the
GM's adventure and the cargo that M226 is carrying.

Regardless of the methods they use to get aboard,
they find themselves in the ship's loading bay, which
is situated at the extreme rear of the ship. Outside of
a number of droids and load-lifters, this portion of the
ship is crewed by a handful of Imperial navy crews and
a small security detail.

While the crews assist Martle Station’s dockwork-
ers in the unloading of cargo, the troopers ensure that
none of the civilians go further into the ship than they
are authorized to. They are firm and entirely willing to
shoot anyone who gets out of line.

When the Player Characters enter M226’s loading
bay, read or paraphrase the following text aloud:

The Player Characters have a choice to make,
depending on their preference. They can choose to
confront the Imperial troopers directly or they can gain
access to, and sneak through, one of the many access
panels within the loading bay.

THE DIRECT APPROACH

The Player Characters need to get past both the troop-
ers and the crewmen if they wish to infiltrate the ship.
The loading bay is full of cargo containers, and there
are plenty of spots for infiltrators to hide. Given the
variety of hiding places and dark corners in the bay,
any Player Character making a Stealth check for any
reason benefits from (J.

Starting a firefight here will probably alert the rest
of the ship to the presence of intruders unless the PCs
make some effort to keep the troopers from commu-
nicating with the freighter’s bridge. Just as there are
plenty of places to hide, the loading bay also presents
a lot of cover that can be easily used to the PCs’ advan-
tage. This is reflected in the PCs’ ability to spend cp
to gain +1 to their ranged defense rating during any
combat that takes place in the loading bay.

LOADING BAY CREW

Aside from anyone the PCs might have brought with
them-or.come aboard with (such as workers from
MartleStation), the ship’s massive loading bay
is staffed-by a number of Impe-

srial Navy personnel and their
s——Security detail. At the bare
= minimum, there are

eight Navy crew, four Navy troopers, and Petty Officer
Tera Wen, the security chief assigned to the loading bay.
Should the GM choose, he may increase or decrease the
number of Imperial troops in the loading bay to either
accommodate or challenge his players.

Though they are loyal Imperial troops, the Navy crew
arent used to (or equipped for) firefights. If a fight
breaks out, they spend most of their time hiding unless
they have an opportunity to engage one or more play-
ers in melee combat. The Navy troopers, on the other
hand, have been on a long voyage and relish a chance
for a little action.

Petty Officer Tera Wen, the security chief assigned
to the loading bay, is a very serious customer. She's
tougher on her troopers than is strictly necessary, but
as a result they follow her orders without question. If
a fight breaks out, she uses her tactical know-how and
leadership to direct her troopers. She won't surrender,
especially if she learns the Player Characters are afflli-
ated with the Alliance.

PETTY OFFICER TERA WEN,
SECURITY CHIEF [RIVAL]

Tera Wen is a stern-looking young woman from the
Core Worlds with regulation-length blond hair, brown
eyes, and a fair complexion. She’s been assigned to
Freighter M226 for several months, and has made a
name for herself as someone who can be counted on
to get the job done. She is just as loyal to the Empire
as the troopers who serve under her.

Skills: Coercion 1, Discipline 3, Leadership 2,
Perception 2, Piloting (Planetary) 2, Ranged
(Light) 2, Vigilance 3.
Talents: Coordinated Assault 1 (two allies
engaged with Tera Wen add &°} to com-
bat checks until the beginning of her
next turn).
Abilities: None.
Equipment: Blaster pistol (Ranged
[Light]; Damage 6; Critical 3;
Range [Medium]; Stun setting),
blast vest and helmet (+ 1 soak),
comlink (handheld).

M226 NAVY TROOPERS
[MINION]

The Navy troopers assigned to
the Perlemian Haul’s freight-
are pulled from some of the

fial Navy's best personnel.

As such, they are highly motivated and willing to die for
the Empire if need be. A group of four of these troop-
ers, commanded by Petty Officer Wen, is assigned to
provide security in M226's loading bay.

Skills (group only): Discipline, Perception, Piloting
(Planetary), Ranged (Light), Vigilance.

Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Blaster pistol (Ranged [Light]; Damage 6:
Critical 3; Range [Medium]; Stun setting), blast vest
and helmet (+ 1 soak), comlink (handheld).

M226 NAVY CREWMAN [MINION]

The crew of M226 are typical Navy personnel with very
little to set them apart from their peers on other ships.
They are hard-working and loyal, but aren't extraordi-
nary in any way. They tend to work in groups of 3 or 4
individuals. Each one wears a grey Navy jumpsuit.

Skills (group only): Athletics, Brawl, Mechanics,
Melee, Vigilance.

Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Comlink (handheld), breath mask, utility belt.

GETTING ARIDE

M226's cargo holds are connected via a series of load-
ing tunnels that run the ventral length of the ship. These
tunnels can be accessed on foot but are more routinely
traversed via repulsorlift cargo skiffs that carry cargo
up and down the length of the ship. Cargo elevators
placed in compartments at regular intervals along the
ventral corridors are utilized by the crew to access the
ship’s cargo holds.

At least three cargo skiffs are present in the loading
bay when the players arrive aboard M226, with a num-
ber of others available along the way—assume there
are one or two such skiffs at each elevator chamber, in
addition to miscellaneous crew and Navy troopers who
provide them with security.

ARATECH “SHIPHAND” CARGO SKIFF

The Shiphand cargo skiff is built exclusively for the
Imperial Navy by Aratech Repulsor Company. Com-

monly found in Imperial port facilities and used to
quickly transport cargo from ships to holding facilities,
the Shiphand provides /Jemple-class freighter crews
with a quick, tough, and reliable method of moving
from the ship’s fore to aft and back.

SILHOUETTE | SPEEO | HANDLING |

Vehicle Type/Model: Cargo Skiff/Shiphand.
Manufacturer: Aratech Repulsor Company.
Maximum Altitude: 2 meters.

Sensor Range: None.

Crew: One pilot.

Encumbrance Capacity: 250.

Passenger Capacity: 6.

Price/Rarity: 12,000 credits/3.
Customization Hard Points: |.

Weapons: None.

THE FAST ROUTE

Player Characters who choose to utilize M226’s cargo
skiffs to traverse the length of the ship can do so easily
(and speedily) enough, but they are liable to encounter
resistance along the way. If the troopers in the loading
bay aren't sufficiently pacified, they pursue any PCs
that attempt to flee on cargo skiffs.

Such an encounter offers an opportunity for a dra-
matic chase within M226’s underbelly, with the PCs on
the run from Imperial troopers. The tunnels themselves
are about four meters wide, with a ceiling height of
about three meters. Though kept moderately clear, they
are occasionally blocked by obstacles such as cargo
containers, maintenance droids, and debris. Even if they
aren't being chased, Player Characters who choose to
traverse M226’s ventral tunnels are still at risk.

In addition to the normal tables used during vehicle
combat, the Game Master is encouraged to use Table
13-1: Spending ¢?, @, % and WY in the Ventral
Tunnels, on the following page, if the Player Characters
spend any appreciable time maneuvering a cargo skiff
through the ship's cargo tunnels.

VENTRAL SECURITY COMPARTMENTS

Each of the freighter's three cargo holds is accessed via
elevators in one of six security compartments—two per
cargo hold. These compartments can be sealed off indi-
vidually by sets of heavy blast doors, and each is staffed
by four crew and two troopers who act as security. If the
Player Characters raise the alarm, the troopers in these
compartments are instructed to close the blast doors
and seal them off from the ventral access tunnels.

The blast doors that secure the compartments are
simple enough to open from the inside, but attempting
to do so from the tunnel side of the compartment is diffi-
cult and requires the proper security tools. Player Char-

acters must succeed on a Hard (@ @ @) Computers
or Skulduggery check in order to hack the lock and

Alternately, players can attempt to enter the secu-
rity compartments by utilizing maintenance tunnels
and duct work, though this method may have its own
associated risks.

THE SOMEWHAT SLOWER ROUTE

Stealthy players may prefer to make their way to the
fore of the ship by accessing conduits, maintenance
tunnels, and ductwork. Though making their way into
the ducts is relatively simple, traversing them with any
degree of accuracy is difficult. There is no sense of
direction within the tunnels, and the Jemple-class heavy
freighter is obscure enough that its technical layout is
little-known outside the Empire.

In a perfect universe, and given the circuitous route and
confined spaces they find themselves in, it takes a group
of Player Characters five hours to move from one end of
M226 to the other. This time frame assumes they have
some knowledge of the ship's layout, and PCs familiar
with the workings of starships and freighters are better
equipped to either know or guess which tunnels to take.

successfully navigating M226’s maintenance tunnels
and ducts requires a Hard (@ @ @) Piloting (Space)
check, utilizing the Player Character’s Intelligence char-
acteristic instead of his Agility. Alternately, a PC can
attempt a Daunting (@ @ @ @) Survival check to find
his way by relying on his own internal compass. One
skill check of either type must be attempted every hour.

A large piece of debris looms in the distance. The player piloting the skiff must succeed on a Hard (@@@) Piloting
(Planetary) check on his next turn or suffer a major collision.

v One of the Player Characters riding on the cargo skiff loses his footing. The PC must succeed on a Hard (@@@) Athletics

The lighting in this section of the cargo tunnels is either not working, or flickers off suddenly due to a power spike. All skill
checks (including Piloting [Planetary]) that require good visibility suffer J until the pilot's next turn.

Failure on either of these checks indicates the PC
and his companions move in circles and make little,
if any, headway. For every %@ result, an additional 30
minutes is added onto the time it takes to make the
trip. In the case of a W result, the PCs encounter a
hazard that must be overcome.

Likewise, any $°? generated on these checks reduces
the amount of time the players must spend in the tun-
nels by 30 minutes. In the case of a @, the players are
able to shave an additional hour from their transit time.

As if moving blindly through the ship’s maintenance
tunnels wasn't bad enough, the ducts are also home to
a small clan of Ranats left over from a failed Imperial
experiment. At the Game Master's option, the Ranats
can be encountered as a hazard in the case of a BW
result on one of the PCs’ skill checks. They can also be
introduced as a stock encounter.

RANATS IN THE WALLS

As mentioned, a clan of Ranats has taken up residence
in the ducts and maintenance tunnels of Freighter
M226. They were originally part of an Imperial experi-
ment that involved using the Ranats as biological weap-
ons with the goal of releasing them into Rebel facilities
and ships in an attempt to sabotage them.

Though the experiment itself had mixed results and
was ultimately judged to be a failure, the surviving
Ranats were shipped via M226 to an Imperial facility
in the Outer Rim in order to conduct further trials. In
the course of the voyage, a number of the creatures
escaped into the freighter’s ductwork.

Despite a number of attempts to wipe the Ranats
out, the Imperial crew was unsuccessful in finding and
killing them all. The Ranats, many of which had been
born into M226’'s maintenance tunnels and had known
no other home, were notoriously difficult to isolate and
proved to be extremely vicious when cornered. Rather
than spend his limited resources to further combat the
Ranats, the ship's captain at the time decided to keep
them on as a sort of organic security system.

As such, the majority of M226's crew is completely
aware of the Ranats. Though the crew fears the crea-
tures due to their stealth, viciousness, and carnivorous
nature, they also consider them to be ships’ mascots
of a sort. Specific protocols requiring maintenance are
observed by the crew, and this has significantly less-
ened the frequency of encounters with the creatures.
In addition, supplies of food are occasionally left for
the Ranats to find, which significantly reduces their
desire to hunt the ship’s crew.

THE RANAT ENCOUNTER

M226’s Ranat clan is small and self-contained, con-
sisting of no more than a dozen individuals. So long
as they are provided with food and left alone by the
freighters crew, the creatures are content to remain
in their warren, which they have established near the
warmth of the ship's power-generating systems.

The Player Characters are unlikely to Know about
the Ranats right off the bat, and they won't be using
the same sorts of protocols the Imperial crew uses
when they move around in the ship's access tunnels.
The Ranats, suspecting their “hosts” are attempting to
harm their warren, investigate the newcomers. Though
they are only partially sentient, the Ranats are none-
theless cunning and very familiar with their surround-
ings, making them a threat to the unsuspecting PCs.

The Ranats attempt to corner the Player Characters,
quietly hemming them in on either side before they
attack. Their leader, Tik, divides his forces, with two of
his stalkers to the PCs front and two more to the back.
He joins the latter group, sneaking up before darting in
with a high-pitched battle cry.

Player Characters must succeed on an opposed
Perception vs. Stealth check to detect the oncoming
Ranats as they scurry forward. Should the PCs fail, the
Ranats get the drop on them. Either way, read or para-
phrase the following text aloud:

The Ranats fight until their leader, Tik, orders them to
retreat. Should Tik fall, any Ranats that remain retreat
into the darkness to lick their wounds and choose a new
leader. Some Player Characters may wish to follow the
Ranats back to their warren. If they attempt to do so, they
find themselves approaching the ship’s power generators.
The heat in the tunnels will begin to rise, and tech-savvy
PCs may become aware of rising radiation levels.

In the case that any of the Ranats (including Tik) are
somehow captured, they can communicate—albeit
crudely—using a small pastiche of Basic words and
phrases they've learned from observing the Imperial
crew. Though communication is difficult due to the lan-
guage barrier (which can be broken if some manner of
translator is available), a Ranat may be willing to lead
the PCs to M226’s control center in exchange for his life.

TIK, RANAT LEADER [RIVAL]

Tik is larger and stronger than his Ranat companions,
and they obey him without question. His fur is a deep
and dirty yellow, tinged with grease and other, fouler sub-
stances. He is a cunning adversary who seeks to maintain
his lofty place in the tribe by whatever means necessary.

Skills: Brawl 3, Coercion 2, Melee 3, Perception 2,
Stealth 4, Survival 4, Vigilance 2.

Talents: Jump Up (may stand up from seated or prone
as an incidental), Natural Brawler (may re-roll any |
Brawl or Melee check).

Abilities: Bite (Brawl; Damage 4; Critical 4; Range
[Engaged]; Pierce 1), Cornered Fury (may upgrade one
skill die when engaged and outnumbered by enemies).
Equipment: Makeshift spear (Melee; Damage 4; Criti-
cal 3; Range [Engaged]), tribal fetishes.

RANAT STALKER [MINION]

The stalkers who accompany Tik on his hunting expedi-
tions are completely subservient to their leader. They
flee from their foes if Tik dies; otherwise, they bare
their yellow fangs and fight.

Skills (group only): Braw!, Melee, Stealth, Survival.
Talents: None.

Abilities: Bite (Brawl; Damage 2; Critical 4; Range
[Engaged]; Pierce 1) Cornered Fury (may upgrade one
skill die when engaged and outnumbered by enemies).
Equipment: Makeshift spear (Melee; Damage 3; Criti-
cal 3; Range [Engaged)).

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

SHIPBOARD HAZARDS
AND FEATURES

As the Player Characters proceed toward the ship’s
control center, they are likely to encounter trouble
along the way. The following shipboard hazards and
features can be utilized by the Game Master to increase
the excitement and tension in his game, as well as to
break up an otherwise unexciting stretch of gameplay.
Whether triggered by a poor dice result (one or more
& when attempting a Survival check to navigate the
freighter’s maintenance ducts, for example) or by the
GM's fiat, such encounters can be a valuable tool.

DUCT INSTABILITY

Though typically of sound structural design, wear-and-
tear (as well as damage caused by the Ranat vermin
that traverse M226's hidden byways) has caused some
ducts and maintenance passages to buckle or weaken.
Alternatively, a stretch of ductwork might have been
intentionally sabotaged by the Ranats in order to act
as a snare or trap to catch unwary crew.

Players moving through such a tunnel should be allowed
to attempt an Average (@ @) Perception check to
notice that the surface they're walking or crawling on isn’t
entirely stable. Success indicates they notice the instabil-
ity before it can collapse and harm them. A success with
one or more ¢@ results indicates that the duct collapses,
either in part or entirely, blocking the PC’s advance.

Failure, on the other hand, results in one or more
Player Characters falling with the collapsing ductwork
or tunnel, suffering 5 damage from both the fall and
Jagged pieces of metal that appear where the tunnel
shears. This damage is increased by 1 for every %} gen-
erated on the Perception check, and is decreased by 1
for every €% achieved. The collapse leads four meters
down into a debris-strewn portion of tunnel, and the
only way out is the way the PCs came. Players can climb
out if they succeed at a Hard (@@@) Athletics check.

Players who notice the instability before it can col-
lapse can either attempt to find another way around
it, or they can take some time to jury-rig a repair that
allows them and the rest of their companions to cross.
Doing so requires a Hard (@ @ @) Mechanics check.
Characters with appropriate tools or inventive solu-
tions should receive one or more [_] to this roll, while
those without the proper tools should suffer a I.

ELECTRICAL HAZARD

As the PCs continue through the tunnels or passage-
ways of the ship, they encounter an electrical hazard in
the form of an open maintenance panel, a bare wire, or
an electrified surface. Depending on the circumstances,
the electrical discharge might be plainly visible, its audi-
ble crackles echoing in the enclosed space.

PERLEMIAN HAUL
ACE OF REBELLION

=

If the PCs aren't interested in retracing their steps
and searching for another route, the easiest thing for
them to do is to edge around the hazard. Doing so
safely requires an Average (@ @) Athletics check.
Even if they are successful, they suffer 1 damage for
every % %) their check generates. Failing the roll
results in a full-strength shock, which inflicts 5 damage,
reduced by | for every {°P they roll.

FREIGHTER CREW

M226's crew members are always keeping busy
throughout the ship at all hours, performing their regu-
lar duties and ensuring that the ship's systems are
properly maintained and functional. It is entirely pos-
sible that a group of Player Characters encounters a
group of Imperial crew going about their business any-
where aboard the ship.

The freighter’s run-of-the-mill crew aren’t particularly
formidable, as their focus is on operating the ship, keep-
ing its systems up and running, and ensuring that cargo
is moved to and stored in its proper place. Despite
this, they are also military men and women who have
received basic combat training, so they can fight if nec-
essary. Such crews aren't typically armed. If confronted
by the PCs, they either surrender or attempt to escape.
Particularly brave or foolhardy crews might attempt to
attack the PCs, but this is largely a decision for the
Game Master to make depending on the situation.

M226 CREW CHIEF [RIVAL]

The typical naval crew chief is a seasoned veteran who
has been serving aboard Imperial ships for many years.
Crew chiefs are the most technically proficient in their
areas of expertise and are often looked up to by their
subordinates. Like other crew, they wear grey Navy
jumpsuits, but their rank is easily distinguished by the

Insignia on their lapel.

ee es Oo

Skills: Athletics 2, Brawl 2, Coercion 1, Discipline 2,
Leadership 3, Mechanics 3, Melee 2, Vigilance 2.
Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Comlink (handheld), breath mask, utility
belt, datapad.

M226 NAVY CREWMAN [MINION]

The crew of M226 are typical Navy personnel, with very
little to set them apart from their peers on other ships.
They are hard-working and loyal, but aren’t extraordi-
nary in any way. They tend to work in groups of three or
four individuals. Each one wears a grey Navy jumpsuit.

Skills (group only): Athletics, Brawl, Mechanics,
Melee, Vigilance.

Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Comlink (handheld), breath mask, utility belt.

MAINTENANCE DROIDS

Though they are largely simple-minded and harmless,
the Imperial maintenance droids assigned to the Perle-
mian convoy are programmed to raise an alarm in the
event they spot unauthorized individuals in their area of
activity. They have only simple programming, but they
are smart enough to recognize that armed individuals
lacking uniforms are out of place, especially if they hap-
pen to be crawling around the ship’s maintenance ducts.

Should such an encounter take place, the maintenance
droid attempts to escape without making too much of
a fuss. If confronted violently, it defends itself using its
heavy grasper arm as well as its integrated arc welder.

NR-5 MAINTENANCE DROID [RIVAL]

Designed by Kalibac Industries to address most main-
tenance issues aboard commercial and military vessels,
the NR-5 is small and unobtrusive. Though not particu-
larly bright or personable, it is able to traverse all but
the most constricted access ducts with relative ease.

Skills: Computers 2, Mechanics 2.

Talents: None.

Abilities: Droid (does not need to breathe, eat, or drink
and can survive in vacuum or underwater. Immune to
poisons and toxins).

Equipment: Arc welder (Melee; Damage: 3; Critical
—; Range [Engaged]; Stun damage), heavy grasper arm
(Melee; Damage 2; Critical 5; Range [Engaged]), fine
manipulator arm, built-in repair tools (counts as tool kit).

RANAT SCOUTS

The Ranats that make their home on M226 are always
scrounging around in search of food or other valu-
able materials. Their scouts are often the smallest of
the warren’s denizens, but they are fast and incredibly
stealthy. They typically work individually or in pairs, as
larger groups are easier to spot.

Should Ranat scouts encounter the Player Charac-
ters, they avoid them entirely unless they outnumber
them. Should one or more of the PCs possess some-
thing that catches the Ranats’ eyes, such as a fancy
tool, weapon, article of clothing, or piece of jewelry,
they may attempt to waylay the PC in question and
Steal the object of their desire.

RANAT SCOUT [MINION]

Ranat scouts are small of frame and wiry. They are
able to easily squeeze into spaces too small for other
creatures, and they use this to their advantage as they
sneak about the freighter. Though they aren't large by
any stretch of the imagination, they are just as danger-
ous when cornered as are their larger kin.

Skills (group only): Brawl 2, Melee 2, Skulduggery 3,
Stealth 4, Survival 2.

Talents: None.

Abilities: Bite (Brawl; Damage 1; Critical 4; Range
[Engaged]; Pierce 1), Cornered Fury (may upgrade one
skill die when engaged and outnumbered by enemies).
Equipment: Makeshift knife (Melee; Damage 2; Criti-
cal 4; Range [Engaged)).

REACTOR LEAK

One of the most dangerous hazards to be encoun-
tered in the belly of an operating starship is a reactor
leak. Such leaks consist of radioactive coolant that has
somehow made its way into the spaces where crew and
passengers spend their time. The radiation is danger-
ous and concentrated and requires special equipment
to properly lock down and contain.

Creeping around puddles of radioactive coolant is
insufficient to avoid exposure, and merely remaining in
the vicinity of the substance inflicts | wound to each
Player Character at the beginning of the PC’s turn until
sufficient distance is put between them and the coolant.
Making direct contact with the coolant is liable to con-
taminate clothing and equipment, cause radiation burns,
and make the subject incredibly ill in the long term.

Should this happen, the affected character must
succeed at a Hard (@ @ @) Resilience check, or he
immediately suffers 5 strain. Each additional <6 on the
check translates to another point of strain suffered in
this manner. &° on the Resilience check reduces the
number of strain suffered by | each.

It is up to the Game Master to determine how large a
threat the radiation leak is to his players. A large leak in a
confined space might be too great a hazard, though groups
of PCs who are prepared for such eventualities should be
able to minimize their losses. Space suits as well as envi-

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

mental gear designed for operation in radioactive
vironments provide protection from residual radiation,
Ough extensive exposure is not recommended.

SECURITY AND PATROL DROIDS

Despite the Empire’s reliance on human troops for
shipboard security, they are not above employing
smaller security and patrol droids to monitor sensi-
tive or constricted areas aboard their vessels. One
example of such a droid is the Mark IV patrol droid,
which is also used extensively in areas garrisoned by
the Imperial Army and Stormtrooper Corps.

Mark IV patrol droids are set to patrol alone and
provide ships’ security personnel with up-to-the-min-
ute reports via their broadband comlink. If encoun-
tered by Player Characters, a Mark IV immediately
transmits relevant information to the elosest security
station on the ship. This information includes the PCs’
position, as well as the number of enemies spotted.
The only hope for such PCs is to destroy the droid
before it can transmit the information it has acquired.

MARK IV PATROL DROID [MINION]

The Mark IV patrol droid is a small, spherical,
ering droid with a number of
control surfaces that
from its rear quart

Skills (group only): Discipline, Perception, Stealth.
Talents: None.

Abilities: Droid (does not need to breathe, eat, or
drink and can survive in vacuum or underwater.
Immune to poisons and toxins).

Equipment: Holorecorder, military comlink.

SECURITY PATROLS

Because of the sensitive contents of M226’s cargo
holds, the crew maintains regular patrols throughout
the ship's populated areas. Navy troopers regularly
patrol in groups of two in search of potential trouble.
If the alarm has been tripped elsewhere, the security
patrols double in strength and frequency until the
of the alarm has been neutralized.

126 NAVY TROOPERS [MINION]

2 N avy troopers assigned to the Perlemian Haul’s
sighters are pulled from some of the Imperial Navy’s

‘Dest personnel. As such, they are highly motivated
~ and willing to die for the Empire if need be.

HRAWN AGILITY


### Episode III: The Prize

FINDING HELP

Depending on the nature of the M226’s cargo, the PCs
might not be the only Rebels aboard the ship. For exam-
ple, if one of the PCs’ side goals is to rescue a group of
SpecForce troopers or slaves from captivity, they might
very well be able to free the troopers and get them to
lend a hand. In addition, they might be able to appropri-
ate heavy Imperial ordnance from the ship’s own stock.

It is up to the Game Master to determine what, if any,
help the Player Characters can expect from the individu-
als they plan to rescue or the equipment they plan to
appropriate. Anyone held prisoner by the Empire aboard
Freighter M226 is subsequently weak, wounded, sick,
or a combination of all three. The help they can provide
to the Player Characters, if any, is limited.

Likewise, nothing raises alarms quite like moving
through the ship with a large contingent of rescued
prisoners, slaves, and other personnel. If the PC’s ulti-
mate goal is to stealthily infiltrate the ship and gain
control of it, in addition to rescuing potential allies
from the clutches of the Empire, they are better off not
putting those individuals at risk.

COMBINING HAZARDS
AND ENCOUNTERS

a ny of the previously detailed hazards and

encounters can be combined for additional
effect or variety, and the GM is encouraged to
do so if the PCs seem to be sailing through the
scenario. For instance, one of the freighter's
maintenance crews might very well be working
on containing a radiation leak or electrical haz-
ard, while a security detail might be dealing with
a group of Ranat scouts, checking on a security
droid, or reporting damage caused by a struc-
tural fault in the ship’s maintenance ducts.

COMMUNICATING
WITH THE ENEMY

In the event that the Player Characters attract unwanted
attention, members of the ship's command staff—Lt.
Cmdr. Noor included—begin to monitor the situation
more closely. Noor likely chooses to communicate with
the PCs, especially if they are doing particularly well (or
even if they are making a poor showing of things).

EPISODE Ill: THE PRIZE

y the time the Player Characters reach the forward

end of Freighter M226, they have either progressed
Slowly and quietly through the ship, maintaining some
semblance of stealth, or they have alerted the ship's
commander and security personnel to their presence.
If they've attempted to keep their profile down, this is
where all their hard work pays off. If not, they have a
fight on their hands. The command module is difficult to
access once it’s been sealed off from the rest of the ship,
and it’s crawling with Navy troopers.

Unfortunately for the Player Characters, the com-
mander of M226—Lieutenant Commander Jilan
Noor—is not in his right mind, and stops at nothing to
ensure that the PCs are taken care of in as definitive a
manner as possible.

ACCESSING M226'S
COMMAND MODULE

The ventral tunnel that leads forward from the ship's
rear loading bay ends at a large, vaulted security com-
partment just prior to the command level. Coinciden-
tally, all ductwork and service passages terminate here
as well, given that the command module has its own
life support and vital systems.

The security compartment, like the others that con-
nect to the ventral tunnels, is equipped with a num-
ber of cargo skiffs and is staffed by a handful of ship's
crew and navy troopers. A pair of heavy blast doors
here seals the command module off from the rest of
the ship. Under normal circumstances, these doors are
kept open to facilitate security personnel as they move
to and from the command module.

THE EASY WAY

Assuming the PCs have done nothing to tip their hand
to the ships crew, they can emerge from hiding and
enter the command module through the open blast
doors. Of course, the troopers and ship’s crew on duty,
should they see them, arent likely to let them pass
unmolested. Even if a firefight does break out at this
point, its more than possible for the Player Charac-
ters to slip through the blast doors before news of their
intrusion reaches the ship's bridge.

THE HARD WAY

If the PCs have alerted the ship to their presence in the
previous episodes, the compartment is tightly locked
down. The blast doors are closed and secured, and
four Navy troopers staff the compartment in expecta-

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

AGDHt vx

1,

tion of the Player Characters’ arrival. Cargo canisters
and other obstacles have been set out to provide the
troopers with cover as well as to prevent anyone from
driving a cargo skiff into the compartment interior.

Like the blast doors leading into and out of the other
ventral security compartments, the doors sealing off
the command module can be sliced. The security pro-
gram is somewhat more difficult to breach, requiring
a Daunting (@ @ @ @) Computers or Skulduggery
check to do so.

THE COMMAND MODULE

M226's command module contains accommoda-
tions for all fifty members of the ship’s crew, as well as
independent life support and power systems. Though
still connected to the rest of the ship, the command
module is its own environment, and it can be sealed
off from the rest of the ship if the need arises. It also
contains the ship's bridge, which is the ultimate desti-
nation for the Player Characters if they intend to take
control of the ship.

The command module has five levels, each one
described here in brief. The levels can either be
accessed Via lift, or by a series of stairwells.

LEVEL 1: COMMAND DECK/BRIDGE

The freighters command deck is the smallest of all
the command module's floors. The bridge itself is the
central focus of the command deck and is accessed
via lift or stairwell. The crew complement of the bridge
numbers eight, including the captain and his executive

PERLEMIAN HAUL
AGE OF REBELLION

a

TEMPLE-CLASS BRIDGE

Tet “Ae oo | i

officer. In addition, two Navy troopers are assigned to
the bridge at all times to provide security.

LEVEL 2: SUPPORT SYSTEMS

The second level of the command module is devoted
to life support and power systems. The ship's medical
bay is also here, staffed by the ship’s surgeon and one
Or more corpsmen. Secondary power and computer
systems are also maintained on this level.

LEVEL 3: CREW QUARTERS

The largest of the command module's levels is devoted
entirely to the crew. Fifty officers and enlisted, includ-
ing security personnel, are housed here in relatively
cramped conditions. A small galley and recreation area
are also provided.

IF THE PCS CAN'T HACK IT

t's entirely possible that a group of trigger-

happy PCs with little in the way of technical
skills will paint themselves into a corner and
be unable to access M226’s command mod-
ule. If this happens, Noor contacts them via
the ship's intercom and urges them to surren-
der. Given his prior experience with the PCs,
Noor is all too willing to open the doors so

that his remaining troopers can capture the
them. Depending on how they play the hand
they're being dealt, the PCs may be able to
gain access to the command module at the
expense of another long, drawn-out firefight.

LEVEL 4: CARGO AND SUPPLIES

The fourth level of the freighter’s command module
is where additional cargo and supplies are kept. Such
Supplies typically consist of consumables and spare
parts. The droid service bay is also located on this
level, and all of the ship’s droids are brought here for
maintenance and repair at one time or another.

Another interesting feature of level four is that the
primary access doors leading to the security compart-
ment and ventral cargo tunnels are located here. If and
when the players make their entrance onto the com-
mand module, they find themselves here.

LEVEL 5: ENGINEERING

The lowest level of the command module is dedicated
to engineering and other technical systems. Half a
dozen engineers work on the engineering level, moni-
toring systems and making adjustments and repairs
when the need arises.

TAKING CONTROL OF M226

Once the Player Characters make their way into the
command module, it’s likely that the crew knows quite
well that they are under attack, if they didn't already.
Lieutenant Commander Noor opens the bridge's
armory and orders all bridge crew to take up sidearms
in order to repel boarders.

The rest of the crew goes to general quarters and

arms themselves as best as they can. Any i is.
Navy troopers set up defensive positions near the lifts
and stairwells in order to head the Player Charatll

off before they reach the bridge.

TROUBLE IN PARADIS

Lieutenant Commander Noor’s §assign-
ment to Freighter M226 has not gone
particularly smoothly. The fact that Noor
abhors his assignment has not been lost
on his crew, and his willingness to throw
their lives away in order to settle his score
with the Player Characters vexes Hyzhan
Troo, his executive officer.

Prior to Noor’s assignment to M226,
Lieutenant Troo was slated for a promo-
tion so she could take command of the
freighter. Unfortunately, the paper-
work had barely cleared Imperial Com-
mand when Noor was reassigned to the
freighter, seemingly as a form of punish-
ment. Since that time, he has treated the
crew and his executive officer poorly.

If given the opportunity to surrender,
Lieutenant Troo gladly does so in an
attempt to save the lives of her remaining

crew. This won't happen as long as Noor is firmly in
command of the ship, though he appears conflicted to
anyone observant enough to notice with an opposed
Perception vs. Discipline check.

ASSAULTING THE BRIDGE

When the PCs arrive on the bridge, it is from either
the lift or one of two stairwells that run parallel to it.
In either case, Noor has formed his bridge crew into
two three-person groups, one of which he leads per-
sonally. The other group is commanded by Lieutenant
Troo. A third group, comprised of the two Navy troop-
ers who are assigned to guard the bridge, operates
Independently.

The bridge crew and Navy troopers take cover
behind control consoles and lay in wait for the Player
Characters’ attack. When the PCs arrive, the bridge
crew does its best to fight them off.

When the Player Characters arrive on the bridge,
read or paraphrase the following text aloud:

Should Noor be disabled or killed in the ensuing com-
bat, Lieutenant Troo surrenders at once if she is still alive
and acquiesces to the Player Characters’ terms. As long
as the PCs are reasonable and not obviously blood-
thirsty, Iroo orders the remaining crew to stand down.

LT. COMMANDER JILAN NOOR,
IMPERIAL OFFICER [NEMESIS]

Jilan Noor is a human male in his early thirties with olive
skin, dark eyes, and short-cut black hair. He Is a career
Navy officer, having risen quickly through the ranks
after graduating from the Imperial Academy at the top
of his class. He is ruthless and punishes failure harshly
among his subordinates. Unsurprisingly, he is hardest
on himself, and this Is likely his biggest weakness.

Skills: Athletics 2, Brawl 2, Coercion 3, Cool 2, Disci-
pline 4, Leadership 4, Ranged (Light) 3, Vigilance 3.
Talents: Adversary 1 (upgrade difficulty of all combat
checks against this target once), Tactical Direction (may
perform a maneuver to direct one Imperial Navy minion
group within medium range; the group may perform an
immediate free maneuver or add [_] to its next check).
Abilities: Imperial Valor (may perform a maneuver to
cause all ranged attacks targeting Noor to instead hit
one ally or helpless enemy he is engaged with, until the
beginning of his next turn).

Equipment: Imperial uniform, blaster pistol (Ranged
[Light]; Damage 6; Critical 3; Range [Medium]; Stun
setting), military comlink.

LIEUTENANT HYZHAN TROO,
IMPERIAL OFFICER [RIVAL]

Hyzhan Troo was set to take command of M226 fol-
lowing the promotion of the ship's last commanding
officer. Unfortunately, Noor was assigned to the ship
before her promotion could take place. Troo is a com-
petent-looking and authoritative human female in her
late twenties.

PERLEMIAN HAUL
ACE OF REBELLION

oS

Skills: Astrogation 3, Brawl 2, Computers 3, Discipline
2, Leadership 3, Piloting (Space) 3, Ranged (Light) 2.
Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Blaster pistol (Ranged [Light]; Damage 6;
Critical 3; Range [Medium]; Stun setting), uniform, mili-
tary comlink, datapad.

M226 BRIDGE CREW [MINION]

The bridge crew of M226 are an experienced lot, and
many of them have served aboard the heavy freighter
since it was first commissioned. Though loyal to the
Empire and willing to follow Noor’s orders, they are
much more comfortable under the command of their
executive officer, Lieutenant Troo.

Skills (group only): Astrogation, Computers, Piloting
(Space), Ranged (Light).

Talents: None.

Abilities: None.

Equipment: Blaster pistol (Ranged [Light]; Damage 6:
Critical 5; Range [Medium]; Stun setting).

EPILOGUE

Assuming the PCs take control of Freighter M226, they
will need to accomplish a number of tasks in order to
complete their mission. The first of these tasks Is to drop
the ship out of hyperspace, a relatively easy task as long
as they are familiar with the workings of spacecraft.

Once out of hyperspace, the PCs need to lay in coor-
dinates for the rendezvous with a strike element from
their fleet. If M226’s crew surrendered, Lieutenant
Troo or her duly-appointed replacement request that
they be allowed to leave the ship via escape pod rather
than be taken prisoner by the Alliance.

REWARDS FOR RISKS

As long as the mission is a success, the Player Char-
acters are lauded by their superiors. News of the vic-
tory will spread throughout the Alliance, and they are
celebrated for their deeds. More importantly, PCs who
risked themselves in order to stand up for their Duties
benefit for doing so. For example, if Imperial prisoners
were rescued, they are certain to know the name of the
Rebel operative who put his life on the line for them.

antasy Flight Games would like to thank the following
people for their hard work playtesting Ace or REBEL-
“Wraith Squadron” Jordan Mill-

ward with Keri Harthoorn, Kyle Harthoorn-Burton, Kie-
ren Smith, Julia Smith, and Malcolm Douglas Spence.
“The Librarians” Pim Mauve with Gerlof Woudstra,
Keesjan Kleef, Jan-Cees Voogd, Joris Voogd, and Jeroen
Brugman. “Forged in Death” Aric Wieder with Dawn

Wildfong, Katie Shannon, Joey Shannon,
Sheriff. “The Ghosts of Alderaan” Max Brooke with
Zoé Robinson, Katrina Ostrander, Dan Clark, and Sam
Stewart. “40 Million Credits!” Andrew Fischer with Matt
Harkrader, Chris Eckes, Rico Saucedo, and Mike Lori.

and Emily

pated in the Ace or ReBetuion Beta Test.

AGEs
REBELLION

ROLEPLAYING CAME

A jong time ago in a galaxy far, far away..

Civil War engulfs the galaxy. The Empire rules over thousands of star systems
using its military might and the power of fear. Struggling against all odds,
soldiers and spies, mechanics and medics, aces and ambassadors all fight to
bring freedom to the galaxy.

Join the Rebellion and embark on your own adventures in the Star Wars
galaxy with the Star Wars: AGE oF REBELLION RoLepLayiING Game. This complete
tabletop roleplaying game dives into the conflict between the Rebel Alliance
and the evil Galactic Empire. Face down legions of stormtroopers, steal secret
plans and restricted codes, and stay on target in the fight against the ultimate
power in the universe.

This core rulebook includes:

e Adynamic dice mechanic that goes beyond success and failure to enhance
narrative gameplay.

e Full rules for playing six careers, twenty specializations, and eight species,
enabling flexible character creation and development.

e Duty rules to invest player characters in the story of AGE oF REBELLION.

e Starship combat, customizable equipment, Force powers, dangerous
adversaries, and much, much more!
